price volatility, and should never take your account size into
consideration. Calculating a volatility target, how much of your capital
to put at risk, is a function of account size and your pain
threshold.[[[72](#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#footnote-069){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#footnote-069-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Positions should then be sized based on how
volatile markets are, how confident your price forecasts are, and the
amount of capital you wish to gamble.

Each of these components is part of the modular [framework]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} which together form a complete trading system.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor140}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor141}Why a modular framework? {#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idParaDest-45 .Heading-4 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor142}Remember that I drew an
analogy between cars and [trading systems]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} in the introduction of this book. [Trading
rules]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} are the engine of the system.
These give you a forecast for [instrument]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} prices; whether they are expected to go up or
down and by how much.In a car the chassis, drive train and gearbox
translate the power the engine is producing into forward movement.
Similarly, you will have a position risk management [framework]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} wrapped around your trading rules.[ ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}This translates forecasts into the actual
positions you need to hold.

As I said in the introduction the components of a modern car are
modular, so they can be individually substituted for alternatives. The
trading rules and other components in my framework can also be swapped
and changed.

The words module and component could imply that these are complex
processes which need thousands of lines of computer code to implement.
This is not the case. Every part involves just a few steps of basic
arithmetic which require just a calculator or simple spreadsheet.

Let's look in more detail at the advantages of the modular approach.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor143}Flexibility {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

The most obvious benefit of a modular design is flexibility. Cars really
can be any colour you like, including black. Similarly my framework can
be adapted for almost any trading rule, including the discretionary
forecasts used by [semi-automatic traders]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and the very simple rule used by [asset
allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. If you don't like
the position sizing component, or any other part of the framework, you
can replace it with your own.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor144}Transparent modules {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

It's possible to have frameworks that are nicely modular but which
contain entirely opaque black boxes. Most PCs are built like this. You
can replace the hard disc or graphics card, but you can't easily modify
them or make your own, so you are stuck with substituting one mysterious
part with another.

In contrast each component in my framework is transparent -- I'll
explain how and why it is constructed. This should give you the
understanding and confidence to adapt each module, or create your own
from scratch.

##### Individual components with well defined interface {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

If you replace the gearbox in your car you need to be sure that the car
will still go forward or backwards as required. But if the drive shaft
output is reversed on your new gearbox you will end up driving into your
front door when you wanted to reverse out of your driveway. To avoid
this we need to specify that the shaft on the gearbox must rotate
clockwise to make the car go forward, and vice versa.

Similarly if you use a new trading rule then the rest of the modular
trading system framework should still work correctly and give you
appropriately sized positions. To do this the individual components need
to have a well defined [interface]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} -- a
specification describing how they interact with other parts of the
system.

For example in the framework it will be important that a trading rule
[forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of say +1.5 has a consistent
meaning, no matter what style of trading or instrument you are
using.[[[73](#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#footnote-068){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#footnote-068-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

##### Getting the boring bit right {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

The part of the [trading system]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} wrapped
around the [trading rules]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, the
[framework]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, is something that's easily
ignored. Creating it is a boring task compared with developing new and
exciting trading rules, or making your own discretionary forecasts. But
it's incredibly important. By creating a standard framework I've done
this dull but vital work for you.

The framework will work correctly for any trading rule that produces
forecasts in a consistent way with the right interface. So it won't need
to be radically redesigned for any new rules. Also by using the
framework [asset allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
and [semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} can get much
of the benefits of systematic trading without using trading rules to
forecast prices.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor145}Examples give you a starting point {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

Creating a new [trading system]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} from
scratch is quite a daunting prospect. In the final part of this book
there are three detailed examples[ ]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}showing how the framework can be used to suit
[asset allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2},[
semi-automatic traders ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}and[ staunch
systematic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. Together these
provide a set of systems you can use as a starting point for developing
your own ideas.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor146}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor147}The elements of the framework {#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idParaDest-46 .Heading-4 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

Table 15 shows the components you'd have in a small [trading
system]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} with two [trading rules]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, a total of four trading rule
[variations]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, and two
[instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. You first create a
[trading subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} for each instrument.
Each subsystem tries to predict the price of an individual instrument,
and calculate the appropriate position required. These subsystems are
then combined into a portfolio, which forms the final trading system.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor148}

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idContainer017}
![](image/TradingSystemComponents_Master.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

Table 15: Example of components in a trading system

This trading system has two trading rules A and B; three rule variations
A1, A2 and B1; and two instruments X and Y. Dotted lines show trading
subsystems for X and Y.

##### Instruments to trade {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

[Instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} are the things you trade
and hold positions in. They could be any financial asset including
directly held instruments such as equities and bonds, or
[derivatives]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} like options, futures,
contracts for difference and spread bets. You can also trade [collective
funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} such as [exchange traded funds
(ETFs)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, mutual funds, and even hedge
funds.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor149}Forecasts {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

A [forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is an estimate of how much a
particular [instrument's]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} price will
change, given a particular [trading rule]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
[variation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. For example a simple
equities strategy might have three forecasts: two variations on a trend
following rule, each looking for different speeds of trend, and a
separate [equity value ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}trading rule with
a single variation. If you are trading two instruments as in table 15
then there will be a total of 3 × 2 = 6 forecasts to make.

The trading rules which produce forecasts are the engine at the heart of
all [trading systems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} used by [staunch
systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. The biggest difference
between strategies will be in which rules and variations are used, and
which instruments are traded. In comparison the rest of the framework
will be fairly similar.

[Semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} make
discretionary forecasts, rather than using systematic rules. [Asset
allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} don't try and
predict asset prices and use a single fixed forecast for all
instruments.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor150}Combined forecasts {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

You need a single forecast of whether an instrument will go up or down
in price, and by how much. If you have more than one forecast you will
need to combine them into one [combined forecast ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}per instrument, using a weighted average. To do
this you'll allocate [forecast weights ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}to each trading rule variation.

##### Volatility targeting {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

It's important to be precise about how much overall risk you want to
take in your trading system. I define this as the typical average daily
loss you are willing to expose yourself to. This [volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is determined using your wealth,
tolerance for risk, access to leverage and expected profitability.
Initially we'll assume that you're putting all of your capital into a
single [trading subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, for just one
instrument.

##### Scaled positions {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

You can now decide how much of the underlying asset to hold based on how
risky your instruments are, how confident you are about your
[forecasts]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, and your [volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. The positions you will calculate
assume for now that you're just trading one isolated instrument, in a
single [trading subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

At this point you've effectively got a complete trading system, but for
a single instrument. Just as the cells in the human body are each
individual living organisms, these trading subsystems are self-contained
units, but in the next stage you'll be putting them together.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor151}Portfolios {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

To get maximum diversification you'd usually want to trade multiple
instruments and put together a portfolio of[ trading subsystems]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, each responsible for a single instrument. This
requires determining how you are going to allocate capital to the
different subsystems in your portfolio, which you will do using
[instrument weights.]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} After applying this
stage you'll end up with [portfolio weighted positions]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} in each instrument, which are then used to
calculate the trades you need to do.

##### Speed and Size {.Heading-5 lang="en-GB" lang="en-GB"}

This isn't a separate component in the framework, but a set of
principles which apply to the entire system. When designing trading
systems it's important to know how expensive they are to trade, and
whether you have an unusually large or small amount of capital. Given
that information, how should you then tailor your system? I'll address
both of these issues in detail in the final chapter of [[part
three]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-13.xhtml#_idTextAnchor135).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {._idFootnotes}
:::

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor152}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor153}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor154}Chapter Six. Instruments {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idParaDest-47 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idContainer018}
![](image/TradingSystemComponents_chap6_NEW.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

[B]{._idGenDropcap-1}efore you think about [how]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} you trade you need to consider [what]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} you're going to trade -- the actual
[instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} to buy or sell. It's likely
you will know which [asset classes]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you
want to deal with, based on your knowledge and familiarity with
different markets.

However there are certain instruments that should be completely avoided
for systematic trading. Others have characteristics which make them
worse than other alternatives, or would force you to trade them in a
particular way. Finally there is often a choice of how you can access a
particular market; you could get Euro Stoxx 50 European equity index
exposure by buying the individual shares, trading a future, a spread
bet, a contract for difference, a [passive index fund]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} or an [active fund]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. Which is best?

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor155}Chapter overview {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idParaDest-48 .Heading-4}

  ------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Necessities                           The minimum requirements that need to be met before you can trade an instrument.
  Instrument choice and trading style   Characteristics that influence instrument choice between alternatives and how to trade particular instruments.
  Access                                Different ways to get exposure to instruments, and the benefits and downside of each.
  ------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor156}Necessities {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idParaDest-49 .Heading-4}

There are a few points to consider when deciding whether an instrument
is suitable for systematic trading.

##### Data availability {.Heading-5}

I'd like to be able to trade UK Gilt futures, but I don't have the right
data licence so I can't get quoted prices. You can't trade
systematically without access to prices and other relevant data.

At a minimum you will need accurate daily price information. Fully
automated strategies that trade quickly or incorporate execution
algorithms will need live tick prices. [Fundamental]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} trading strategies require yet more kinds of
data. The costs of acquiring data on certain instruments, like Gilts
perhaps, may be uneconomic for amateur investors.

##### Minimum sizes {.Heading-5}

Another future I would like to trade -- but can't -- is the Japanese
government bond (JGB) future. The contract currently sells for around
150 million yen, which is well over a million dollars. If I put this
into my portfolio the maximum position I would want is 0.1 of a
contract, which obviously isn't possible. Few amateur investors will be
able to trade these behemoths.

In stocks the minimum size is one share, normally costing less than
\$1,000, although the A class of Berkshire Hathaway shares currently
sell for over \$100,000. Even for cheaper stocks, it may not be economic
to trade in lots of less than 100 shares.

[Minimum sizes]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} reduce the granularity
of what you can trade. Your positions become binary -- all or nothing
(in the case of JGBs, always nothing). This is an important subject and
I will return to it in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size'. As you'll see in that chapter this problem also
affects the number of instruments you can hold in your portfolio.

##### Why do prices move? {.Heading-5}

Do you know why bonds, equities and other instruments go up, or down, in
price? "More buyers than sellers" is not an acceptable answer! I
personally think it's important to have an understanding of what makes a
market function; whether it be interest rates, economic news or
corporate profits. This is vital if you're going to design [ideas first
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}trading rules.

It's also important to understand market dynamics once your system is
running, if you want to avoid unpleasant surprises in markets that have
become dysfunctional. As I'm redrafting this chapter, the Swiss
government has just removed the peg which had held since 2011 fixing
their currency at 1.20 to the euro, resulting in a massive Swiss franc
(CHF) appreciation against all currencies. Thousands of traders
including large hedge funds and banks were on the losing side, including
many who were trading systematically.

Fortunately I wasn't trading the EUR/CHF or USD/CHF FX pairs. For me
there didn't seem any point in trying to make systematic forecasts,
since I knew the market was controlled by central bank intervention
rather than the normal historic factors driving prices in the
[back-test]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. Keeping abreast of markets
will help you to avoid similarly dangerous instruments.

##### Standard deviation {.Heading-5}

There is another reason why I excluded Swiss FX positions from my
systematic trading strategies, which was the extremely low
[volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of prices whilst the peg was
in place. In principle my framework can deal equally well both with
assets whose returns naturally have low [standard deviations ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}and those that are very risky. It can also cope
with changes in volatility over time.

However instruments which have extremely low risk like pegged currencies
should be excluded. Firstly, when risk returns to normal it is liable to
do so very sharply, potentially creating significant losses. Secondly,
these positions need more leverage to achieve a given amount of risk,
magnifying the danger when they do inevitably blow up. Even if you don't
use leverage they will limit the risk your overall trading system can
achieve.[[[74](#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#footnote-075){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#footnote-075-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Finally, they also tend to be more costly to
trade, as you will discover in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size'.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor157}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor158}Instrument choice and trading style {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idParaDest-50 .Heading-4}

With your pool of available instruments narrowed by excluding those
which don't have the necessary attributes mentioned above, you need to
decide which of those remaining you prefer to trade. These
characteristics will also influence [how]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} you'll trade the instruments you've chosen.

##### How many instruments? {.Heading-5}

I like my portfolio of instruments to be as large and diversified as
possible, as long as I don't run into issues with minimum sizes, for
example as I would do with Japanese government bonds. The maximum number
of instruments you can have will depend on minimum sizes, the value of
your account and how much risk you're targeting (which you'll learn
about in [[chapter nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205),[
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}'Volatility targeting').

In [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', you'll see how to calculate the point at which you
could run into problems with minimum instrument size. You will then be
able to work out what size of portfolio makes sense.

Then in part four I will give some recommended portfolios in each
example chapter, which have been constructed to be as diversified as
possible given the level of the [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and the available instruments.

##### Correlation {.Heading-5}

If you already owned shares in RBS and Barclays then the last thing you
would want to add to your portfolio is another UK bank like Lloyd's.
Generally you should want to own or trade the most diversified portfolio
possible, where the average [correlation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
between the assets is lower than the alternatives. If there are a
limited number of instruments that you can fit in your portfolio then it
makes sense to pick those with lower correlations.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor159}Costs {.Heading-5}

Given the choice between two otherwise identical instruments you should
choose the cheapest to trade. So, if you can, use a cheap FTSE 100
future rather than an expensive spread bet to get exposure to the UK
equity
index.[[[75](#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#footnote-074){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#footnote-074-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Instruments that are expensive to trade are
clearly less suitable for [dynamic]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
strategies, particularly those that involve faster trading.

Sometimes you have to trade an expensive instrument, if it's the only
way of accessing a particular asset. In this case you should trade it
more slowly. There is however a maximum acceptable cost depending on the
type of trading that you're doing, so some instruments will be
completely unsuitable. Because the cost of trading is so important it
will be covered in great detail in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size'.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor160}Liquidity {.Heading-5}

Closely related to costs is [liquidity]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.
Less liquid instruments are likely to be more expensive to trade quickly
or in larger amounts. This is more of a problem for large institutional
investors and those trading fast. Liquidity is not constant and can
reduce quickly in times of severe market stress, particularly for non
exchange traded 'over the counter' instruments, as in the Credit Default
Swap derivatives markets in 2008.

Again [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', will explain how those with larger account sizes will
need to understand costs and liquidity better than small investors.

##### Skew {.Heading-5}

Should you avoid negative[ skew ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}in your
portfolio from instruments like holding short VIX (US [equity volatility
index]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}) futures? Remember I covered the
skew of assets and trading rules in [[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039),
'Systematic Trading Rules'. [Static]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
strategies will inherit the skew of their underlying instruments, but
the skew of a [dynamic]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} strategy also
depends on the style of your trading rule. So using a positive skew rule
like [trend following]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} on a negative skew
asset will alleviate some of the danger.

As you will see in [[chapter nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205),
'Volatility targeting', you need to be extremely careful if the overall
returns of your [trading system]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} are
expected to have negative skew. Instruments with extreme negative skew
will often have very low [standard deviation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} for most of the time, and should be excluded on
those grounds.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor161}Access {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idParaDest-51 .Heading-4}

Finally you need to choose the route by which you access the underlying
assets you're going to trade.

##### Exchange or OTC {.Heading-5}

Does your instrument trade on an exchange like shares in General
Electric and Corn futures, or over the counter (OTC) like foreign
exchange (FX)? There are often different ways of trading the same
underlying asset, some via exchange, others OTC. So a spread bet on the
CHF/USD FX rate is OTC, whilst the Chicago future on the same rate is
exchange traded.

If you have a choice then, all other things being equal, should you
trade on exchange or OTC? In the January 2015 Swiss franc meltdown
traders using OTC brokers suffered a variety of difficulties, including
dealers not accepting orders or displaying quotes, trades not being
honoured and fills re-marked after the fact, and in extreme cases
potential account losses as brokers went into liquidation.

Those who traded the CHF/USD future had difficulty finding deep
liquidity, but otherwise the market operated as normal. In conclusion
it's normally better to trade on exchange if you can.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor162}Cash or derivative {.Heading-5}

'Cash' is simply where you own the underlying asset directly -- perhaps
a share in British Gas or a bond issued by General Electric.
Alternatively you can own a [derivative]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
on an asset, like a future, Contract for Difference or spread
bet.[[[76](#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#footnote-073){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#footnote-073-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} The main advantage of derivatives is that they
offer straightforward leverage. Without leverage it might be difficult
to reach your[ volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
[target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, which will reduce the returns
you can earn. There may also be different tax treatments; in the UK for
example spread bets are treated as gambling, which means winnings are
tax free but losses aren't deductible.

Various types of derivatives may have different trading costs, and also
have different minimum sizes, liquidity and market access. For example a
FTSE 100 future is cheaper to trade and more liquid than the
corresponding spread bet. It also has the advantage of being accessed
via an exchange, whereas the spread bet is OTC. But the future has a
larger minimum size which precludes its use by smaller investors.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor163}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor164}Funds {.Heading-5}

Other options for trading the FTSE 100 are to buy an [index
tracker]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} like an [exchange traded fund
(ETF)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. These are [collective
funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} -- instruments which buy you a
share in a portfolio of assets. As well as ETFs, collective funds
include US mutual funds, UK unit trusts and investment trusts. Normally
for systematic trading you will be interested in [passive funds]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} like [index trackers]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. These contain baskets of assets weighted to
match an index like the FTSE 100 or S&P 500.

Passive funds normally have relatively low annual fees and minimum
sizes, but frequently cost more than the relevant [derivative]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} to trade. But they can be useful instruments when
leverage can't be used, or when a market can't be accessed another way.

In some cases you might want to use [active funds]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, where the weights to different assets are
determined by the fund manager. This might make sense if there is no
relevant passive fund or derivative, but fees are higher on active
funds, and the presence of any compensating manager skill or
[alpha]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is very hard to prove.

Collective funds can have quirks such as daily remarking, tax treatment,
internal leverage and discounts to net asset value which you should
fully understand before using them.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor165}Summary of instrument choice {#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idParaDest-52 .Heading-4}

  ------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Data availability               At a minimum you need daily price data to trade an instrument systematically. For [fundamental]{.Table_strong} strategies you also need other relevant data, e.g. price:earnings if you're using an [equity value rule]{.Table_strong}.
  Minimum sizes                   For small investors large minimum trading sizes can be a problem.
  Understand what moves returns   Don't trade from an ivory tower; have some idea of the factors driving returns. If unusual forces are at play then avoid that instrument.
  Standard deviation of returns   Volatility must not be extremely low.
  How many instruments?           Given the size of your account and the minimum size of each instrument you can determine whether you will run into problems given a particular sized portfolio. You should then hold the largest portfolio you can given those constraints.
  Correlation of returns          You should always try and have a portfolio where assets are as diversified as possible.
  Costs                           Cheaper is better. Expensive instruments will need to be traded more slowly, and may be too pricey to trade at all.
  Liquidity                       For larger and less patient investors liquidity is vital.
  Skew of returns                 Assets with strong negative[ skew]{.Table_strong} need careful handling and shouldn't dominate your portfolio. Using the right trading rules can improve skew to some degree.
  Trading venue                   Is the market accessed via exchange, or over the counter (OTC)? On exchange is preferable.
  Cash or derivative              Should you trade the asset outright, or a derivative based on its value, and if so which one?
  Collective funds                Investment through collective funds can make sense when derivatives can't be used.
  ------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now you know [what]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} you are going to
trade, the next step is to think about [how]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}. So the next chapter will cover the business of
forecasting prices.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {._idFootnotes}
:::

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor166}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor167}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168}Chapter Seven. Forecasts {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-53 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idContainer019}
![](image/TradingSystemComponents_chap7.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

[W]{._idGenDropcap-1}hat do these terms all have in common: breakouts,
Elliott waves, Fibonacci waves, [exponentially weighted moving average
crossover ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}and Bollinger bands? Answer:
They can all form the basis of [trading rules]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. These rules give you [forecasts]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}[ ]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}of what they
think will happen to the prices of [instruments]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} you are trading or investing in.

[Staunch systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} use multiple
systematic rules like these. But rules can also be extremely simple,
like the single rule used by [asset allocating investors]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. It's also possible to use my [framework]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} without any systematic rules at all, as a
[semi-automatic trader]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} making your own
discretionary forecasts.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor169}Chapter overview {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-54 .Heading-4}

  ------------------------------------------------ ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  What makes a good forecast                       Understand the important properties of the forecasts which trading rules produce.
  Discretionary trading with stop losses           How semi-automatic traders can make forecasts that have the right characteristics.
  The asset allocating investor's 'no-rule' rule   The simplest possible trading rule used by asset allocating investors -- a constant and identical forecast for all assets.
  Two systematic rules                             A pair of suggested trading rules for staunch system traders.
  Using other people's rules                       How to adapt publicly available trading rules or invent your own.
  Selecting trading rules and variation            A brief reminder of how I recommend selecting rules and variations.
  ------------------------------------------------ ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor170}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor171}What makes a good forecast {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-55 .Heading-4}

Parts of this section are quite technical; [semi-automatic]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and
[asset allocating]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} [investors]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} should read it but need only skim the content.
However, [staunch systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} who
will design their own trading rules need to understand it in detail.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor172}A forecast is a scaled quantity {.Heading-5}

When I started trading options at a large investment bank I was
encouraged to put on small proprietary positions outside of the normal
customer flow trading. The idea was to give novices some practical
experience in managing risk without exposing the book to large losses.
Initially I was limited to trading single futures contracts.

Once I graduated beyond one contract I was faced with a dilemma. How big
should my position be for a purchase of German 10 year bond futures
(Bunds)? Should I stick to one contract, or increase my size? I asked
for advice from a more experienced trader,
Sergei.[[[77](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-084){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-084-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} He sighed, exasperated at the ignorance of youth,
and turned towards me.

"You need to know three things. First, [how much do you like this
trade?]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} That comes from here," Sergei
replied, pointing to his heart.

"Second thing, [how much can you afford to lose?]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} That is down here, in your gut," and he patted
his extensive stomach.

"Last thing, [how risky is it?]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} You
calculate that here." said Sergei, tapping his forehead.

His answer was unhelpful, completely lacking in any detail, but totally
correct.

It's not enough to predict that prices are rising or falling; you also
need to decide whether they will go up a lot, or a little. You need to
decide [how much]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} [you like the
trade]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}. You need a [forecast]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} -- a prediction of how much prices are expected
to go up or down. This chapter is about making forecasts. I'll return to
the rest of Sergei's wisdom in subsequent chapters.

A forecast is a number: a positive value means you want to buy the asset
because the price is expected to go up and a negative indicates you want
to short the asset. Investors who don't use derivatives and can't short
sell will only make positive forecasts. A forecast shouldn't be binary
-- buy or sell -- but should be scaled. Forecasts close to zero indicate
a small movement in prices and larger absolute values mean you expect
bigger returns.

There are three reasons why scaled forecasts make sense. Firstly, if you
were to examine the returns made by a trading rule given the size of its
forecasts, you'd normally find that forecasts closer to zero aren't as
profitable as those further away. Secondly, binary systems cost more to
trade, since to go from long to short you'd need to sell twice a full
size position immediately. Finally, the rest of the framework assumes
that the forecasts you get are not binary or lumpy in other
ways.[[[78](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-083){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-083-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} It's better to see forecasts changing
continuously rather than jumping around.

It's relatively easy to design or adapt a trading rule to produce scaled
forecasts, if you're a [staunch systems trader]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. If you're a [semi-automatic trader ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}generating forecasts in a more discretionary way
then you need to be able to quantify the strength of your convictions
and I'll return to that problem later in this chapter. [Asset allocating
investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} have it easy since they use a
fixed forecast value.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor173}Forecasts proportional to risk adjusted return {.Heading-5}

How do you set forecasts so that they embody how much you like a
position, or to be precise how strong you think its subsequent rise or
fall will be?

Simple, you might think. As I was trading Bunds when I spoke to Sergei
then I should set up my [trading rule]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} so
a weak forecast of 50 would mean a purchase of 50 Bund futures. A strong
forecast of 100 implies 100 contracts, and so on.

Unfortunately there are several flaws in this idea. What if you wanted
to use my forecasting rule? You might not want to take a gamble on even
five Bunds, or you could work in a large fund for whom an average
position would be 50,000 contracts. This kind of forecast doesn't
account for [different account sizes]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}
or [varying appetite for risk]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}.

Secondly, what if the [standard deviation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of Bund prices were to double? A position of 50
contracts would now be twice as risky as you'd originally expected. The
simple forecast doesn't account for [risk changing over time]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}.

Finally, you'd normally want to use the same trading rule across
different[ instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, because it's
simpler and it allows you to pool data if you're [fitting]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. However 50 Bunds expose you to much less risk
than 50 Japanese government ten year bond futures, but to much more than
50 German Schatz (two year) bond futures. The rule can't cope with
[instruments which don't have the same risk]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}.

The solution is to use my favourite technique, [volatility
standardisation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. So in my framework
forecasts are [proportional to expected risk adjusted returns]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}. For example suppose that the Bund has expected
returns of 2% a year and an expected annualised standard deviation[
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}of 8%. Schatz futures have an expected
return of 1% a year, but you only expect [volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of 2% a year. After adjusting for risk the
expected return on Schatz (1% [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 2% = 0.5)
is twice as much as on Bunds (2% [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 8% =
0.25).

That implies the forecast for Schatz should be twice the forecast for
Bunds. This deals with instruments that have different risks. It also
means any trading rule can be used without modification on all
instruments and that if you fit trading rules you can do so with pooled
data from multiple assets. If you continuously adjust your estimate of
expected volatility then you also cope with risk changing over time.

Finally with forecasts like these different investors can use the same
trading rules, but then scale positions depending on the size of their
accounts and their appetite for risk. I show how this is done in
[[chapter nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205),
'Volatility targeting', and [[chapter ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229),
'Position sizing'.

You've seen before that the ratio of average return to standard
deviation of returns is the [Sharpe ratio]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. So expected Sharpe ratios make good forecasts,
which makes creating trading rules very intuitive.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor174}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor175}Forecasts should have a consistent scale {.Heading-5}

I said above that a forecast needs to be [proportional]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} to the risk adjusted return. In the example above
forecasts of 2.5 for Bunds and 5 on Schatz; 0.0000005 and 0.000001
respectively, or 5,000 and 10,000, are all valid. Which of these, or the
infinity of other options, is best?

In truth it doesn't really matter, as long as you are consistent, but
whichever scaling you choose here will affect the rest of your trading
system. In particular the expected average absolute value[ ]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}of your forecasts is a key part of specifying the
system. Life will be easier if you choose a scaling that is easy to work
with. Very small or large numbers are difficult for humans to cope with
and even computers struggle with floating point representation of tiny
fractions.

My recommendation is to create forecasts which have an expected absolute
value[ ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}of 10[. ]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}So +10 is an average buy and -10 an average sell.
A forecast of +5 would be a weak buy, and -20 is a very strong sell.
These forecast numbers may seem very abstract right now. Don't be too
concerned as in [[chapter ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229)
'Position Sizing' I will show you how to translate them into actual
positions.

If you're a [semi-automatic trader ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}using
discretionary forecasts, it's straightforward to have the right scaling,
as I will show you below. The rule used by [asset allocating
investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} also has the appropriate
scale. The example rules I provide for [staunch systems traders
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}later in the chapter are also correctly
scaled. But if you're making your own rules, or adapting others, you
will need to standardise their natural scaling, as I'll explain later in
the chapter.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor176}Should we allow forecasts to be as large as possible? {.Heading-5}

With an expected absolute value of 10, a forecast of +10 is bullish, and
one of +20 is really bullish. What if you're confident that this is the
chance of a lifetime, like the 2009 Barclays trade which I told you
about in the introduction. Should you allow your rule to have a forecast
of +100? I think not. Forecasts should be capped at a maximum absolute
value and I recommend a limit of 20. Here's why:

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Risk control                     | Diversification is the           |
|                                  | investor's best friend. But it's |
|                                  | pointless diversifying if you    |
|                                  | then allow one part of your      |
|                                  | system to dominate your returns. |
|                                  | You need to limit the risk that  |
|                                  | any [trading rule                |
|                                  | variation]{.Table_strong} can    |
|                                  | contribute to the overall        |
|                                  | system.                          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Limited data                     | When I [back-test                |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}a profitable     |
|                                  | trading rule I normally find     |
|                                  | that positive forecasts are      |
|                                  | associated with price rises, and |
|                                  | larger values mean larger rises. |
|                                  | However for a properly scaled    |
|                                  | rule very large forecast values  |
|                                  | will appear rarely in a          |
|                                  | back-test. So there is a lot of  |
|                                  | uncertainty around whether       |
|                                  | extremely large forecasts        |
|                                  | translate into proportionally    |
|                                  | larger price movements, as I     |
|                                  | don't usually have enough data   |
|                                  | to support this finding.         |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | If a forecast had a[ Gaussian    |
|                                  | normal                           |
|                                  | distribution]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | you'd only see absolute forecast |
|                                  | values over 20 around 5% of the  |
|                                  | time, so there is only limited   |
|                                  | evidence that forecasts of this  |
|                                  | magnitude are actually correct.  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Extremes are often different     | Normally markets trend with      |
|                                  | falls followed by further falls, |
|                                  | but after very sharp drops       |
|                                  | subsequent one day rises are     |
|                                  | more likely; the so called 'dead |
|                                  | cat bounce'. Similarly high      |
|                                  | yielding stocks do well, but     |
|                                  | those with dividend yields above |
|                                  | 50% are probably going bankrupt, |
|                                  | or at the very least about to    |
|                                  | cut their dividend. Many other   |
|                                  | patterns also reverse at         |
|                                  | extremes. These effects are not  |
|                                  | often strong or common enough to |
|                                  | exploit directly, but they       |
|                                  | should give you concern about    |
|                                  | betting too much when you have   |
|                                  | very strong forecasts.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Higher realised return           | A high forecast could mean that  |
| volatility                       | you expect large returns, but    |
|                                  | equally might be due to very low |
|                                  | [standard                        |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | returns. Unfortunately periods   |
|                                  | of subdued                       |
|                                  | [volatility]{.Table_strong} are  |
|                                  | usually followed by jumps to     |
|                                  | much higher levels, often        |
|                                  | combined with reversals in       |
|                                  | price.                           |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | I had very strong positive       |
|                                  | forecasts in the Japanese Bond   |
|                                  | markets in January 2013, mainly  |
|                                  | due to relatively low            |
|                                  | volatility. When Prime Minister  |
|                                  | Abe unveiled his latest set of   |
|                                  | economic reforms volatility      |
|                                  | spiked rapidly as prices dropped |
|                                  | fast. Fortunately because my     |
|                                  | forecasts were capped the damage |
|                                  | was contained.                   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Limited downside                 | If forecasts have a [Gaussian    |
|                                  | normal]{.Table_strong}[          |
|                                  | distribution]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | then it's rare, again around 5%  |
|                                  | of the time, that you'll see     |
|                                  | values bigger than 20. So the    |
|                                  | reduction in your returns from   |
|                                  | capping is quite small compared  |
|                                  | to the potential risk control    |
|                                  | benefits.                        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

[Staunch systems traders ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}shouldn't
ignore forecasts that are outside of the range -20 to +20, but cap them.
A forecast of +25 from your trading rule should be reduced to +20.
Increase any forecast lower than -20 to that level. All your forecasts
will then be in the range -20 to +20.

For [semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} all your
discretionary forecasts should be in the range of -20 to +20, with no
exceptions, even for the trade of a lifetime. [Asset allocating
investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} don't need to worry as you
will have a fixed forecast of +10.

If we can't short an instrument, because of limited access to
derivatives or difficulties selling short, then you'll also need to
change all negative forecasts to zero.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor177}Discretionary trading with stop losses {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-56 .Heading-4}

Semi-automatic Trader

If you're a [semi-automatic trader]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} then
you won't use systematic trading rules but instead make
[discretionary]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} forecasts either on gut
feeling, or using complex analysis which can't be systematised, like my
2009 Barclays trade in the introduction.

As well as the sign of the forecast, positive for long and negative for
short, you must indicate how strongly you feel about it. Your forecasts
must be correctly scaled: the expected absolute value should be around
10, and they must be between the recommended limits of -20 and +20.

Forecasts don't have to be finely quantified, such as "I love the Nikkei
today, about 8.7663." However, you should be able to give a rough
indication of the strength of your passion for the Nikkei. You can then
translate your opinion into a forecast number, as table 16 shows.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor178}Table 16: Translating
opinions into a quantified forecast

  ------------------ ------------- ------ ----------- --------- ---------- ----- ------------ -----------------
  Very strong sell   Strong Sell   Sell   Weak sell   Neutral   Weak buy   Buy   Strong buy   Very strong buy
  -20                -15           -10    -5          0         5          10    15           20
  ------------------ ------------- ------ ----------- --------- ---------- ----- ------------ -----------------

If you can't go short assets, because you're not using derivatives like
spread bets or can't short sell in your account, then you'll need to
limit yourself to positive forecast values.

You shouldn't change your forecast once a bet is open. Otherwise there
is the risk of meddling, taking profits too early by reducing the
conviction of your forecast or doubling up on a loss. Instead you will
be using a systematic trailing stop loss rule to close all your
positions. I'll explain this further in [[chapter thirteen]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor298),
where I describe a semi-automatic trading system in more detail.

There are substantial advantages to using discretionary forecasts in my
systematic [framework]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} with stop losses;
you can make predictions about the behaviour of your live trading and
measure expected risk more precisely. Your positions will always be the
correct size for a given forecast, tolerance for risk, and account size.
This will give you more time and energy to get the forecast right.
Finally, by using stop losses you'll be trading somewhat like an 'early
loss taking' [trend follower]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, so it's
more likely your strategy will have benign positive [skew]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor179}The asset allocating investor's 'no-rule' rule {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-57 .Heading-4}

Asset Allocating Investor

Think about a [static]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} portfolio of two
[collective funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}: an S&P 500 equity and
a US benchmark bond[ ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}fund. Suppose you
bought them in equal weights, with half your cash in each. As bond funds
tend to have a quarter of the [volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of equities this portfolio will have 80% of its
risk coming from stocks. As I explained in [[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039) the
solution is to hold the two assets in [risk parity]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, each contributing the same amount of expected
risk. This implies a cash weight to equities of roughly 20% and 80% to
bonds.

By using the [framework]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, instruments
will automatically have identical expected [standard deviation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of returns for a given forecast level. This means
if you have an equally weighted portfolio of two [trading
subsystems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2},[ ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}one for equities and one for bonds, each with
[identical forecasts]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} for their
individual instrument, then you'd always have the same expected
volatility for both equities and bonds. You'd effectively have a risk
parity strategy where risk was being constantly adjusted, or what I
described in [[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039) as a
'fourth degree' static portfolio ([page
44](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor072)).

Hence the correct rule for an [asset allocating investor]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} is to have a constant forecast equal to the
expected average forecast, +10 for all instruments at all times. I call
this the 'no-rule' rule. You don't think you can predict how different
assets will perform, so you buy them all. Remember all forecasts are
proportional to return adjusted for standard deviation. So using an
equal forecast for all instruments is equivalent to assuming all assets
have the same expected [Sharpe ratio]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

There are certain advantages to using this constant forecast approach
within the systematic framework, rather than just buying a normal risk
parity portfolio. For starters you can probably do better than equal
weights, as you will remember from [[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116),
'Portfolio allocation'. Secondly, many risk parity funds don't adjust
for the standard deviation of returns changing over time, whereas you
will.[[[79](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-082){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-082-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} As I'll discuss in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', that adjustment will be done after carefully
considering the trading costs of our instruments. A DIY risk parity
portfolio avoids paying the management fees you'd have with an external
fund manager.

Finally, if you do decide to introduce expectations of asset returns
there is a neat and relatively robust way to do this, by adjusting
[handcrafted]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} portfolio weights in line
with where you think prices will go. I'll give you more detail in the
[asset allocating investor]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} example in
part four of the book.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor180}Two example systematic rules {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-58 .Heading-4}

Staunch Systems Trader

[Semi-automatic traders]{.NoteAsideStrong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and
[asset allocating investors]{.NoteAsideStrong ._idGenCharOverride-2} can
skip the remainder of this chapter, which is for [staunch systems
traders]{.NoteAsideStrong ._idGenCharOverride-2} only.

The two examples of [trading rules]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} above
aren't very interesting. I include them to show how my framework can
still be used by people who don't believe that market returns can be
forecasted, or others who think they can do it better than a simple rule
can. However [staunch systematic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
disagree with both of these groups and wish to forecast prices with
fully systematic trading rules.

There isn't enough space in this book to include every trading rule in
existence. Fortunately, there's a plethora of other books and websites
available which contain explicit trading rules, or ideas from which they
can be developed. Just one book, Perry Kaufman's [Trading Systems and
Methods]{.BookTitle ._idGenCharOverride-1} has over 1000 pages and
includes dozens of
rules.[[[80](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-081){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-081-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} The next section in this chapter will show how
you can create your own trading rules, or modify other people's.

In this section I will briefly outline two rules that I use in my own
trading
system.[[[81](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-080){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-080-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} If you want to use them you can find more detail
in [[appendix B]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342). In
part four of this book I'll show how these rules can be incorporated
into the framework to create a complete system for the staunch systems
trader.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor181}Trend following with the EWMAC rule {.Heading-5}

Many asset prices seem to show [momentum]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}: what goes up, usually carries on going up; and
vice versa. This effect can be captured by using [trend
following]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} rules. I am a big fan of these
types of rules for several reasons.

Firstly, they work. As we saw in [[chapter one]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor019), the
trend following 'early loss taker' rule made mincemeat of its
opposition, the 'early profit taker'. There's a lot of academic research
and real performance statistics supporting the use of trend following
rules. Secondly, I like rules which I can explain; as I said in
[[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039)
prospect theory justifies why these rules work, and should continue to
do so. Also, trend following is an easy to implement [technical]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} strategy using only price data. Finally, it has
benign positive[ skew]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Of course you could use the now familiar early loss taker rule for trend
following, but there are better
alternatives.[[[82](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-079){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-079-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} One of the most popular trading rules for
capturing trends goes by the unwieldy name of [Exponentially Weighted
Moving Average Crossover]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, or
[EWMAC]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} for
short.[[[83](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-078){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-078-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Fortunately the idea is simpler to implement than
it is to say. You take two [exponentially weighted moving averages
(EWMA)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of an asset's price. One average,
the slower, looks back over a longer period than the faster average.
When the faster is above the slower it means that prices are in an
uptrend and you should buy; and vice versa.

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idContainer020}
![](image/chap7ewmac.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

Figure 18: Riding the downtrend in crude oil futures with the EWMAC
trend following rule. We sell when the fast EWMA crosses the slow; and
buy when this reverses

Figure 18 shows an example. Here we've got the price of crude oil
futures from summer 2008 onwards. After a long uptrend in prices there
is a reversal in July 2008. Within a few weeks the fast EWMA has gone
below the slower and goes short. The gap gets bigger and the position is
increased until January. A gentle bullish trend begins and the short is
reduced, and then reversed once the fast EWMA climbs back above the slow
in June 2009. There is a detailed specification of the EWMAC rule in
[[appendix B]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342).

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor182}The carry rule {.Heading-5}

[EWMAC]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is a positive [skew]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} rule which needs prices to trend in one direction
or another. To balance it out here is the carry rule which benefits when
nothing happens. [Foreign exchange carry]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
is a well known strategy for exploiting stable markets; if you borrow in
Japanese yen at 0.5% and invest the money in Australian dollars at 3.5%
then you'll earn 3%, assuming that the JPY/AUD exchange rate doesn't
budge. But what does carry mean for other
assets?[]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor183}[[[84](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-077){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-077-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

Carry, which is sometimes called roll down or contango, is the return
that you will get if asset prices are perfectly stable. If the world
doesn't change this will be equal to the yield on the asset, less the
cost of borrowing (or the interest you would have got from holding
cash). So if you buy shares in French supermarket Carrefour which
currently has a dividend yield of 3%, and you pay 1% to borrow the
purchase cost, then you earn 2% carry on the position if the share price
is unchanged.

Similarly today I can buy June 2018 Eurodollar futures at 97.94, or
March 2018 at 98.01. If there is no change in the shape of the yield
curve then in three months' time June futures will rise to 98.01,
earning 0.07 per contract.

Academic theory predicts that prices should move against us to offset
these returns, but it often doesn't. Carry is usually earned steadily on
these kinds of trades although occasionally they go horribly wrong and
the relationship temporarily breaks down, giving this trading rule some
evil negative[ skew]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. I think the rule is
profitable as a reward for taking on this nasty skew and various other
kinds of [risk premium]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} which tend to be
correlated to carry trades.

Full details on how I calculate carry are given in [[appendix B]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342),
from [page 297](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor349) onwards.
There is an example of the carry trading rule in action in figure 19,
again showing crude oil futures during the great crash of 2008. The
price of the nearer futures contract dips below the contract we are
trading in mid-August. This is a bearish carry signal and the rule sells
to enjoy some of the continuing fall in price.

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idContainer021}
![](image/chap7carry.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

Figure 19: Trading crude oil with carry in 2008-10. We are slow to sell
and stay short too long

Unfortunately it remains bearish long after the market has turned, but
you can't win them all. It makes sense to combine diversifying trading
rules like the positive skew, trend following EWMAC rule, with a
negative skew carry rule.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor184}Adapting and creating trading rules {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-59 .Heading-4}

Staunch Systems Trader

The above trading rules, one positive and one negative [skew]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, are sufficient to create a pretty good trading
strategy. The benefits of adding further rules within the same styles
are relatively limited. Just using these two simple rules would generate
roughly 85% of the back-tested performance of my own more complicated
system.

However you might still want to create your own rules, or adapt those
you find elsewhere. Here are some pointers, with an example of how I
modified a trading rule I read about whilst researching this chapter,
which I've nicknamed 'Close to Open'.

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Simplicity and objectivity       | Trading rules in books tend to   |
|                                  | be quite complicated, with lots  |
|                                  | of conditionals and a degree of  |
|                                  | subjectivity. Try and pare the   |
|                                  | rule down to its essential       |
|                                  | elements, to create a simple and |
|                                  | objective trading rule.          |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | The example rule I found could   |
|                                  | be reduced to 'Buy if the open   |
|                                  | is higher than yesterday's       |
|                                  | close[]{#Autopilot_eb            |
|                                  | ook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor185}'. |
|                                  | (I haven't tested this rule and  |
|                                  | I have no idea if it will work   |
|                                  | or not.)                         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Continuous, not binary           | Many trading rules are           |
|                                  | unfortunately of a buy or sell,  |
|                                  | binary, nature. If possible you  |
|                                  | should make them continuous. I   |
|                                  | converted the simple rule above  |
|                                  | to calculate the raw forecast as |
|                                  | \[Open price - Close price\].    |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Not market specific; volatility  | The value of a forecast should   |
| standardised                     | be comparable across markets and |
|                                  | across time. If a forecast is in |
|                                  | price units, as in the simple    |
|                                  | example here, then you should    |
|                                  | [volatility                      |
|                                  | standardise]{.Table_strong} it   |
|                                  | to get a forecast proportional   |
|                                  | to [Sharpe                       |
|                                  | ratio]{.Table_strong}. So your   |
|                                  | forecast would be:               |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | \[Open price - Close price \] ÷  |
|                                  | (Recent [standard                |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | daily returns)                   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | This is the same standardisation |
|                                  | that I use in the                |
|                                  | [EWMAC]{.Table_strong} and       |
|                                  | [Carry]{.Table_strong} rules.    |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Changing forecast                | Separate entry and exit rules    |
|                                  | are not suitable for the         |
|                                  | fr                               |
|                                  | amework.[\*]{.Footnote-reference |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-6} Ideally a |
|                                  | forecast should change           |
|                                  | continuously, independently of   |
|                                  | what our position is, throughout |
|                                  | the life of a trade.             |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | This suggests that you should    |
|                                  | create rules which recalculate   |
|                                  | forecasts every time you have    |
|                                  | new data, and then adjust your   |
|                                  | positions accordingly. Normally  |
|                                  | this is simply a matter of       |
|                                  | modifying the entry rule. Rather |
|                                  | than use the 'Close to Open'     |
|                                  | rule only for entries, you would |
|                                  | calculate the forecast value     |
|                                  | every day.                       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Correct scaling                  | If you're going to create        |
|                                  | trading rules you need to        |
|                                  | rescale them from their natural  |
|                                  | scaling, so that the expected    |
|                                  | absolute value[                  |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}of the forecast  |
|                                  | is around 10. You do this by     |
|                                  | multiplying your raw forecast by |
|                                  | a [forecast                      |
|                                  | scalar]{.Table_strong}. You need |
|                                  | to [back-test]{.Table_strong}    |
|                                  | the behaviour of the rule with   |
|                                  | real data to find the average    |
|                                  | forecast, but in line with my    |
|                                  | recommendations in [[chapter     |
|                                  | three]{.URL                      |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor093), |
|                                  | 'Fitting', you shouldn't look at |
|                                  | performance data whilst doing    |
|                                  | this. See [[appendix D]{.URL     |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor364), |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 311](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor367), |
|                                  | for more details.                |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | The natural average absolute     |
|                                  | value of this rule is 0.33,      |
|                                  | implying the forecast scalar in  |
|                                  | this case is 10 ÷ 0.33 = 30.     |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

\* If you absolutely have to use an entry rule which doesn't adjust
throughout the life of the trade, with a separate exit rule, then I
strongly suggest you use the stop loss rule I advocate for
[semi-automatic traders]{.Source_strong ._idGenCharOverride-10}. I don't
recommend using any other kind of explicit exit rule as they are usually
over-fitted and make life very complicated.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor186}Selecting trading rules and variations {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-60 .Heading-4}

Staunch Systems Trader

Even if you don't adapt or create new rules and just use mine, there are
still a theoretically infinite number of [EWMAC]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [trading rule]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
[variations]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} on the menu. If you do get
creative and make your own rules the number of options will explode.
Which rules and variations should you use in practice?

As I said in [[chapter three]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor093),
'Fitting', I don't recommend using [back-tested]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} profitability to select trading rules or
variations. Instead you should focus on behaviour such as the
[correlation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} between variations, and the
speed they trade at, whilst ignoring [Sharpe ratio ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}and other performance metrics.

You should then use two selection criteria. The first is to avoid
including any two variations with more than a 95% correlation to each
other, as one of them will not be adding any value to the system. I
discuss how to prune possible variations of the EWMAC rule in [[appendix
B]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342) (in
the section on EWMAC, from [page
294](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor345)).

Secondly, you must exclude anything which trades too slowly, or too
quickly. As I mentioned in [[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039), very
slow rules are unlikely to generate significant Sharpe
ratio.[[[85](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-076){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#footnote-076-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} I suggest you remove any rule which holds
positions for longer than a few months. At the other end of the spectrum
for many instruments fast rules will be too expensive to trade. This
could mean you end up choosing a different set of variations for each
instrument. I'll return to this in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', where I'll show how to determine which variations
should be excluded for a given level of trading costs.

The [staunch systems trader]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} example
chapter in part four will give an overview of the rule selection
process.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor187}Summary of trading rules and forecasts {#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idParaDest-61 .Heading-4}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Where do forecasts come from     | If you're [staunch systems       |
|                                  | traders ]{.Table_strong}then     |
|                                  | each of your trading rules       |
|                                  | produces a forecast, including   |
|                                  | any variations that we're using; |
|                                  | for example to capture different |
|                                  | trend lengths.                   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | [Asset allocating                |
|                                  | investors]{.Table_strong} use a  |
|                                  | constant identical forecast for  |
|                                  | all instruments.                 |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | [Semi-automatic                  |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} make     |
|                                  | discretionary forecasts on an    |
|                                  | ad-hoc basis for a changing set  |
|                                  | of instruments.                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
