\$2,450 in interest on your loan. The block value is
\$2,450.[[[110](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-112){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-112-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor235}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor236}Price volatility {.Heading-5}

We've established that a 1% fall in quoted price will cause a certain
degree of pain, equal to the [block value]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, for each [instrument]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [block]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of an
instrument that you're long. But how likely is a 1% fall in price?
Equity prices can easily change by 1% or more in minutes, but a two-year
bond might not see a 1% move over several months. How should you take
these different levels of risk into account?

This is another job for [volatility standardisation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. You need to calculate an expected [standard
deviation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of daily instrument percentage
returns, which I'm going to define as the [price volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of an instrument. So for example the price
volatility of an average equity is around 1%, whereas as I write this
the German two-year Schatz bond future has a daily standard deviation of
just 0.02%.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor237}CONCEPT: Measuring recent
standard deviation

Remember that my standard definition of predictable risk (from [page
44](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor075)) is the expected daily
standard deviation of prices. One of the simplest ways to estimate
future volatility is to use a measure of recent standard deviation. This
works surprisingly well because volatility tends to persist; if the
market has been crazy over the last few weeks it will probably continue
to be crazy for a few more weeks.

I recommend using one of three methods to calculate recent standard
deviation.

The first method is to eyeball the chart. If you look at figure 22 you
can see that the average daily change in crude oil futures over the last
month of the chart is around \$1.0 a day, or 1.33% of the final price of
\$75. This method is fine for semi-automatic traders who will probably
be staring at charts anyway, but I don't recommend it for others.

The second method is to calculate the precise standard deviation of
price changes over a moving window of time -- the volatility look-back
period. You can use a short window like ten business days (around two
weeks), or a longer one such as 100 days.

As the upper panel of figure 23 shows, shorter windows mean your
volatility estimate is noisy, which as you'll see in [[chapter
twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266) will
mean higher trading costs, but reacts very quickly to changes. The
slower estimate is smoother, however it takes longer to adjust to new
volatility environments. It's not obvious which look-back would be best.

In my research I found almost no difference in performance before costs
for look-back periods from a few days up to six months in
length.[[[111](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-111){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-111-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Rather than risk over-fitting I decided on a
default look-back of 25 business days, or five weeks. This corresponds
to the most popular value used across the industry, for example by the
RiskMetrics (TM)
system.[[[112](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-110){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-110-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} I'll discuss in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', when and why you'd use a longer look-back for
instruments that are expensive to trade.

The lower panel of figure 23 shows the volatility estimation using a 25
day look-back. It also shows the estimate coming from my third method,
which is to use an exponential weighted moving average (EWMA) of
standard deviation.

This gives a smoother measure than a simple moving average that still
reacts quickly to significant changes in the state of the market. I've
chosen the default look-back for this as 36 days, which is equivalent to
a 25 day window for a simple moving
average.[[[113](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-109){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-109-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Details of how to calculate volatility with both
kinds of moving average are shown in [[appendix D]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor364) on
[page 312](#Autopilot_ebook-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor371).

Using recent price volatility is generally a good way to get the right
risk adjusted position size. However it would be a disaster if you were
using this method to trade Credit Default Swaps in early 2007, the front
of the Eurodollar futures curve at any time in the US zero interest rate
period, or for that matter Swiss franc FX in early January 2015.

During all of these periods there was really low price volatility. If
volatility is low then you would buy large numbers of instrument blocks,
since each block has very little expected risk. But after periods of
calm markets have a nasty habit of becoming crazy overnight, leaving you
with dangerously large positions.

As I've already mentioned in earlier chapters, holding very low
volatility assets is generally a bad idea, and this is yet another
reason to avoid them.

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idContainer027}
![](image/chap10vol2.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

Figure 22: Price and daily changes in crude oil futures

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idContainer028}
![](image/chap10vol1.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor238}Figure 23: Measuring crude
oil price volatility using simple moving average (MA) and exponentially
weighted (EWMA) standard deviations with various look-backs, in days

Returning to the example I opened the chapter with, if you use the
bottom panel of figure 23 the price volatility of crude oil futures here
is around \$1 per day, which is 1.33% of the final price shown
(\$75).[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor239}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor240}

##### Instrument currency volatility {.Heading-5}

If one oil futures contract has a [block value]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of \$750, and a [price volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of 1.333%, then what is the risk of owning one
contract? This is the [instrument currency volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}; it's the expected [standard deviation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of daily returns from owning one instrument block
in the currency of the instrument. For oil futures you'd expect on any
given day to see a price move of around 1.33% and since each 1% price
move will cost you \$750, your daily profit or loss will average around
1.33 × \$750 = \$997.50. So the instrument currency volatility for WTI
crude oil futures is \$997.50. This value shouldn't be rounded.

In general instrument currency volatility is equal to the block value
multiplied by the price volatility. It will be in the same currency in
which the block value is measured.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor241}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor242}What's that in real money? {.Heading-5}

This is all fine if you are a US investor trading crude oil futures, a
Brit trading UK equities, or a Euro area investor buying German bonds.
However many investors and traders will want to buy and sell in
currencies that are different from their trading capital. The
[instrument currency volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} isn't
good enough. Instead you need to know the volatility of the instrument
value in the currency of your account, which will be the same currency
as your [cash volatility target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. I call
this the [instrument value volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

The instrument currency volatility needs to be converted to an
instrument value volatility[ ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}using an
appropriate exchange rate. The exchange rate will have the currency of
the instrument as the numerator, and the account value currency as the
denominator.

As an example for a UK investor with a GBP account, the instrument
currency volatility of a crude oil future (\$997.50) will need to be
multiplied by the current USD/GBP exchange rate, to work out what those
dollars are worth in British pounds. As I write this paragraph the
USD/GBP rate is 0.67, giving an instrument value volatility of \$997.50
× 0.67 = £668.325. Again you shouldn't round any numbers yet. Note I'm
using USD/GBP, rather than GBP/USD which is normally quoted. Be sure you
are using the correct rate.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor243}Volatility target and position risk {#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idParaDest-79 .Heading-4}

Now let's relate this back to your [cash volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} -- the risk you require and are comfortable with
from your trading account. From the previous chapter on volatility
targeting ([page 146](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor211))[
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}your [daily cash volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is your long-term expected daily
standard deviation of returns, measured in the currency of your [trading
capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Let's refer back to the example I opened the chapter with. We had an
investor with a £1,000,000 [annualised cash target volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, implying a[ ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}daily target of one-sixteenth of that,
£62,500.[[[114](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-108){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#footnote-108-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} I worked out above that the crude oil future had
an [instrument value volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of
£668.325 for a UK investor. This is the daily risk of owning one
instrument block -- a single futures contract. How many of these blocks
of crude oil should this investor hold?

For now let's assume we are putting together a [trading
subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} for the investor; a trading
system which only has a single instrument. We'll be achieving the entire
cash volatility target by having positions in just one instrument; in
this case crude oil. This assumption will be relaxed in the next
chapter, when we look at creating a portfolio of subsystems.

We also assume that we get the required target risk by having a constant
amount of expected volatility from owning a fixed long position in the
asset. So we won't worry yet about the[ ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}value of any [forecast]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} for the instrument. I'll consider forecasts in
the next section of this chapter.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor244}For the investor in the
example to get the entire required daily standard deviation of £62,500
from being long crude oil futures they need to buy £62,500, divided by
the risk of one contract, £668.325, or 93.52 contracts. The value of
93.52 is a scaling factor which accounts for the difference between an
instrument's natural volatility and the required volatility of the
portfolio. I call it the [volatility scalar]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.

In general the volatility scalar is equal to your daily cash volatility
target, divided by the instrument value volatility. You'll notice that
both of these variables are in the same currency as your trading
capital. Also please note that you should not round the volatility
scalar to a whole number.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor245}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor246}From forecast to position {#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idParaDest-80 .Heading-4}

I've shown that the investor in the simple example for this chapter
would need to own a long position in 93.52 crude oil futures (the
[volatility scalar]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}) to realise their
desired [volatility target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. This however
ignores any [forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} that the investor
has made; either a [combined forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
from multiple [trading rules, ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}or the
single forecasts made by [asset allocating investors]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and [semi-automatic traders]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. At the start of the chapter I'd assumed that the
forecast is -6 in the example. Clearly the investor is going to want to
be short, rather than long, but by how much?

To work this out you need to think about [average]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} forecasts. Over a long period of time you'll
still want to be hitting your [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} for returns, no matter what your daily forecasts
are. In [[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168)
([page 122](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor174)) I recommended
that forecasts should have a long run average absolute value of 10. This
implies that to hit your target over the long run you'd want your
positions to have the same average expected variation as if you had a
constant forecast of +10.

Effectively then the volatility scalar gives you a position which is
consistent with having a constant forecast of +10. If you're currently
more optimistic with a larger positive forecast you should buy more
blocks; and if you're pessimistic you should have fewer blocks, or go
short if the forecast is negative.

In the example above I calculated a volatility scalar of 93.52 crude oil
futures contracts. This will be consistent with the long run forecast,
so a forecast of +10 equates to buying 93.52 contracts (ignoring for the
moment that you can't hold fractional contracts). If the forecast falls
to +5 you'd only be half as confident, and want half the original
position, 46.76 contracts. A forecast of -20 would mean you'd want to go
short twice the original position, a sell of 187.04 blocks.

The resulting quantity of blocks is the [subsystem position.]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} It's a subsystem position because, as I said
earlier, we're assuming in this chapter your entire capital is invested
in a subsystem trading one instrument, rather than in a complete
[trading system]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} running across a number
of instruments.

A subsystem position is equal to the volatility scalar multiplied by the
forecast, then divided by the long average of 10. So for the crude oil
example with a forecast of -6, and where I've worked out the volatility
scalar to be 93.52, the subsystem position will be (93.52 × -6)[ ÷
]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"}10 = -56.11, implying a short of 56.11
contracts. Again you shouldn't do any rounding of non integer positions
for now.

If you're an [asset allocating investor]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
you've probably spotted that with a constant forecast which is always
equal to +10 (from the single 'no-rule' rule), your position will always
be equal to the [volatility scalar]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

For other traders and investors your position will depend on your
forecast and on the components of the volatility scalar (your [cash
volatility target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} for your portfolio and
the [account value volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of the
instrument). Higher absolute forecasts, bigger account sizes, a greater
appetite for risk and lower instrument price volatility will mean larger
positions; and vice versa.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor247}Summary for position sizing {#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idParaDest-81 .Heading-4}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument forecast              | [Staunch systems                 |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong}: This is |
|                                  | the [combin]{.Table_strong}[ed   |
|                                  | forecast]{.strong                |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-12}          |
|                                  | calculated in [[chapter          |
|                                  | eight]{.URL                      |
|                                  | ._i                              |
|                                  | dGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ |
|                                  | ebook-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor190) |
|                                  | by weighting your [trading rule  |
|                                  | forecasts]{.Table_strong} for an |
|                                  | instrument and accounting for    |
|                                  | the [forecast diversification    |
|                                  | multiplier]{.Table_strong}.      |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | [Asset allocating                |
|                                  | investors:]{.Table_strong} Equal |
|                                  | to a constant value of +10.      |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | [Semi-automatic                  |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong}: Single  |
|                                  | discretionary forecast.          |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | All groups will end up with one  |
|                                  | forecast per instrument. It will |
|                                  | have an expected average         |
|                                  | absolute value of 10 and it is   |
|                                  | limited to a range of -20 to     |
|                                  | +20.                             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Annualised cash volatility       | This is the [percentage          |
| target                           | volatility target                |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}multiplied by    |
|                                  | the [trading                     |
|                                  | capital]{.Table_strong},[        |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}from [[chapter   |
|                                  | nine]{.URL                       |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205). |
|                                  | It's an annualised measure of    |
|                                  | expected risk from your          |
|                                  | portfolio.                       |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | It will be in units of the       |
|                                  | currency your trading capital is |
|                                  | in.                              |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Daily cash volatility target     | [Annualised cash volatility      |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} divided   |
|                                  | by 'the square root of time';    |
|                                  | with approximately 256 business  |
|                                  | days in a year you should divide |
|                                  | by 16.                           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Price volatility                 | Expected daily [standard         |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | instrument price changes. Watch  |
|                                  | out for very low standard        |
|                                  | deviations.                      |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | It will be in units of           |
|                                  | percentage of price.             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument block                 | This is the size you trade       |
|                                  | instruments in. For equities     |
|                                  | this would usually be one share, |
|                                  | though 100 shares might make     |
|                                  | sense if trading fewer is        |
|                                  | uneconomic. For futures this     |
|                                  | will be a single contract and    |
|                                  | for spread bets it is the        |
|                                  | minimum bet per point.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Block value                      | This is how much each 1% change  |
|                                  | in the price of a                |
|                                  | [block]{.Table_strong}           |
|                                  | translates into monetary value.  |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | It will be in units of           |
|                                  | instrument currency, i.e. \$ for |
|                                  | US equities.                     |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument currency volatility   | This is the daily standard       |
|                                  | deviation of instrument value.   |
|                                  | Equal to [block                  |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong} multiplied |
|                                  | by the [price                    |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}. It   |
|                                  | will be in units of instrument   |
|                                  | currency.                        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Exchange rate                    | This is the exchange rate        |
|                                  | between instrument currency      |
|                                  | (numerator) and the currency     |
|                                  | your [trading                    |
|                                  | capital]{.Table_strong} is in    |
|                                  | (denominator).                   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument value volatility      | Daily standard deviation of      |
|                                  | instrument value, measured in    |
|                                  | your trading capital currency.   |
|                                  | Equal to [instrument currency    |
|                                  | volatility                       |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}multiplied by    |
|                                  | the [exchange                    |
|                                  | rate]{.Table_strong}.            |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | It will be in units of your      |
|                                  | account currency.                |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Volatility scalar                | Number of position units to hold |
|                                  | if you are investing your entire |
|                                  | [trading capital]{.Table_strong} |
|                                  | in one instrument, ignoring      |
|                                  | forecasts. Equal to [daily cash  |
|                                  | volatility                       |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} divided   |
|                                  | by the [instrument value         |
|                                  | volatility, ]{.Table_strong}both |
|                                  | in your currency.                |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | It's in units of position        |
|                                  | blocks.                          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Subsystem position               | How many blocks do you need to   |
|                                  | hold given your forecast? Equal  |
|                                  | to the [instrument               |
|                                  | forecast]{.Table_strong},        |
|                                  | multiplied by [volatility        |
|                                  | scalar,]{.Table_strong} divided  |
|                                  | by 10.                           |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | This is in units of position     |
|                                  | blocks.                          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor248} {.Heading-4}

##### Worked example for position sizing {.Heading-5}

From the start of the chapter the investor in this example has an
annualised £1,000,000 [volatility target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
and is investing in WTI Crude oil futures contracts, with a forecast of
-6.

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument forecast              | -6                               |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Annualised cash volatility       | £1,000,000                       |
| target                           |                                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Daily cash volatility target     | [Annualised cash volatility      |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} divided   |
|                                  | by 16: £62,500                   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Price volatility                 | For crude oil the bottom panel   |
|                                  | of figure 23 shows average       |
|                                  | returns of \$1 a day, which with |
|                                  | a price of \$75 equates to       |
|                                  | 1.33%.                           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument block                 | One futures contract             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Block value                      | For a WTI Crude contract of 1000 |
|                                  | barrels with price \$75 a 1%     |
|                                  | price move will equate to \$0.75 |
|                                  | × 1000 = \$750.                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument currency volatility   | This is the [block               |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong} multiplied |
|                                  | by the [price                    |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | For crude this is \$750 × 1.33 = |
|                                  | \$997.50                         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Exchange rate                    | Numerator is instrument          |
|                                  | currency, USD. Denominator is    |
|                                  | account value currency, GBP. The |
|                                  | USD/GBP exchange rate is         |
|                                  | currently around 0.67.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument value volatility      | This is the instrument currency  |
|                                  | volatility multiplied by the     |
|                                  | exchange rate.                   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | \$997.50 × 0.67 = £668.325       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Volatility scalar                | Daily [cash volatility           |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} divided   |
|                                  | by the instrument value          |
|                                  | volatility, both in the          |
|                                  | investor's currency.             |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | £62,500 divided by £668.325      |
|                                  | equals 93.52 contracts.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Subsystem position               | Final combined instrument        |
|                                  | forecast, multiplied by          |
|                                  | volatility scalar, then divided  |
|                                  | by 10.                           |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | (-6 × 93.52) ÷ 10 = short 56.11  |
|                                  | contracts (no rounding).         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

You can now answer all of Sergei's questions and you've finally got
positions for a trading system that has a single instrument, the
[subsystem position]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. But any good
trading system will have multiple assets and even [semi-automatic
traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} normally have more than one bet
on the table at a time. The next chapter examines how you can put
several subsystems together into a portfolio.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {._idFootnotes}
:::

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor249}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor250}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor251}Chapter Eleven. Portfolios {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-82 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idContainer029}
![](image/TradingSystemComponents_chap11.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

[Q]{._idGenDropcap-1}uestion: What do many traders and amateur investors
who buy individual equities rather than invest in [collective
funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} have in common? Answer: They both
tend to have too few assets in their portfolios.

Research into individual investors finds many hold less than five
shares, whilst traders often only prefer to deal with one or two markets
where they feel most expert. So far I haven't helped solve this problem,
since in the last chapter I showed you how to put together a [trading
subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} which holds positions in just
a single instrument.

But diversification really is the only free lunch in investment.
Allocating across different [asset classes]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} can easily double your expected [Sharpe
ratio]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, as we saw in [[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039)
([page 52](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor088)). It's best to
simultaneously run a [portfolio]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} of as
many trading subsystems and instruments as possible and allocate your
[trading capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} between them. To
allocate within this portfolio you will use [instrument weights]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. Even though [semi-automatic traders ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}don't have a fixed set of assets they should also
try and hold a number of positions in several instruments at once.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor252}Chapter overview {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-83 .Heading-4}

  -------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Portfolios and instrument weights                                    What is a portfolio of trading subsystems and what are instrument weights?
  Instrument weights -- Asset allocators and Staunch systems traders   How to allocate weights when you are trading a fixed set of trading subsystems.
  Instrument weights -- Semi-automatic traders                         The special case of semi-automatic traders who have multiple bets on, but not necessarily in the same instruments.
  Instrument diversification multiplier                                Accounting for diversification between subsystems for different instruments.
  A portfolio of positions and trades                                  Calculating what position you should hold, given your instrument weights, and what trades you should generate.
  -------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor253}Portfolios and instrument weights {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-84 .Heading-4}

This section is suitable for all readers

In an earlier chapter I used an example of a three asset portfolio to
illustrate the black art of [portfolio optimisation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. The three assets were the S&P 500, NASDAQ and a
20 year US Bond. Suppose now that you're [asset allocator investors
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}who are trying to allocate capital
between these [instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, or [staunch
systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} who have forecasting
rules that predict their returns. Alternatively, imagine that you're a
[semi-automatic trader]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} who happens to
have placed bets on each of these three assets.

Using the methods in the previous chapter you would calculate three
[subsystem positions]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, one for each
instrument. Each position will depend on your [forecast]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and how risky each instrument is. You would also
have pretended to put all of your [trading capital]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} into trading each one of these systems.
Effectively you will create three [trading]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [subsystems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, each
of which nominally uses your entire capital to trade one instrument.

In practice though you're going to share your capital across a
[portfolio]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} of subsystems; similar to
the way I created a portfolio with the same three underlying assets in
[[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116).
Each instrument's trading subsystem will get a positive [instrument
weight]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, with weights summing to 100%.
Your [portfolio weighted position]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} will
be the instrument's subsystem position, multiplied by the relevant
instrument weight to reflect its portfolio share. Once you have this you
can work out what trades are needed for each instrument.

As I'll show you later in the chapter you also need to account for the
effect of portfolio diversification. But first we need to consider how
to calculate the instrument weights.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor254}Instrument weights -- asset allocators and systems traders {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-85 .Heading-4}

Asset Allocating Investor

Staunch Systems Trader

[Asset allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} run a fixed
portfolio of [trading]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
[subsystems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, one per
[instrument]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, each consisting of a single
[trading rule]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} -- the 'no-rule' rule
producing a constant identical forecast. [Staunch systems traders
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}have a group of [trading rules]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} whose forecasts are combined and then scaled into
a position. In both cases you are allocating your [trading
capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} between subsystems relating to a
fixed set of instruments. This is different to traditional portfolio
allocation, where you allocate capital directly to positions in each
instrument.

Because of your hard work in the last couple of chapters all the
subsystems will be [volatility standardised ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}and have the same expected [standard
deviation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of returns. As you saw in
[[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116),
this makes the job of [portfolio optimisation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} simpler. You can easily use the
[handcrafting]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} method introduced in
[[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116) to
determine the instrument weight each subsystem gets. It's also possible
to use [bootstrapping]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} as an
alternative.[[[115](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-120){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-120-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

To handcraft your weights you need to group the assets and for this you
need [correlations]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. There are two
alternatives that can be used to find these. Firstly you can estimate
them using [back-tested]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} data.
Alternatively if you don't have back-tested correlations then tables 50
to 55 (from [page 305](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor360) in
[[appendix C]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350))
give indicative correlations between instrument returns.

But you need the correlations between trading subsystem returns,
[not]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} the returns of the underlying
instruments. For [dynamic ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}trading
systems the correlations between subsystem returns tend to be lower than
those of the underlying instruments. A good approximation is that the
correlation between subsystem returns will be 0.70 of the correlation of
instrument returns. So if two assets have a correlation of 0.5 between
their instrument returns in [[appendix C]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350),
then their subsystems will have a correlation of 0.7 × 0.5 = 0.35.

Because asset allocating investors use a [static]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} strategy, the correlation of your subsystems is
likely to be much closer to what you'd have with fixed long positions in
the underlying instruments. So rather than multiplying the correlations
in [[appendix C]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350) by
0.7, I recommend that you use them without adjustment.

In [[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116) I
showed you how to change portfolio weights if you had evidence that
assets had different Sharpe ratios. But I wouldn't recommend adjusting
instrument weights for Sharpe ratios, since there's rarely enough
evidence of different performance between subsystems for different
instruments, even once we account for different levels of costs. I come
back to this in the chapter on 'Speed and Size'.

Let's look at an example. Here's how I would handcraft the instrument
weights for the simple example of three instruments (S&P 500, NASDAQ, 20
year bond). Row numbers refer to the relevant rows of table 8 ([page
89](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor127)).

+-----------------------+---------------------------------------------+
| First level grouping  | Group one (bonds): One asset, so 100% in 20 |
|                       | year bond. Row 1.                           |
| Within asset classes  |                                             |
|                       | Group two (equities): Two assets, 50% in    |
|                       | each. Row 2.                                |
+-----------------------+---------------------------------------------+
| Second level grouping | I have two groups to allocate to, each gets |
|                       | 50%. Row 2.                                 |
| Across asset classes  |                                             |
+-----------------------+---------------------------------------------+

This gives me the same weights I had in [[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116) for
the actual assets, as shown in table 28. Of course this wouldn't
normally be the case; it's only because this is a trivial example with
no more than two assets in each group, and the same groupings as before.

Table 28: In this simple example handcrafted weights for trading
subsystems are the same as for the assets themselves

  ------------------ --------------------
                     Handcrafted weight
  US 20 year bond    50%
  S&P 500 equities   25%
  NASDAQ equities    25%
  ------------------ --------------------

I give more complex examples of handcrafting instrument weights in each
of the example chapters in part four.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor255}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor256}Instrument weights -- semi-automatic trading {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-86 .Heading-4}

Semi-automatic Trader

[Semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} don't have fixed
portfolios of [instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, always
creating [forecasts]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and holding
positions in each and every instrument. Instead you're likely to have a
relatively small number of opportunistic positions (bets) on your books
at any one time, drawn from a larger pool of potential trading ideas.
Each time you enter a new bet for a different instrument you need to
make a forecast, and then size your positions according to the size of
your account and the risk of the instrument.

This presents a problem because the make-up of your portfolio, and hence
the [correlation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of returns, will change
over time as different instruments come in and out. The simplest thing
to do is to allocate trading capital [equally]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} between potential opportunities. To ensure your
risk is properly controlled you must also limit the [maximum]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} number of bets that can be placed at any time.

So you should allocate your risk equally between a notional maximum
number of concurrent bets, each of which represents a potential [trading
subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. For example if the notional
maximum number is ten bets this implies each [instrument weight
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}will be 100% divided by 10, which equals
10% for each [instrument]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

For reasons that will become clear later in this chapter, I recommend
that this maximum shouldn't be more than 2.5 times the
[average]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} number of bets you expect to
be holding over time.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor257}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor258}Instrument diversification multiplier {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-87 .Heading-4}

Once you've parcelled out your [trading capital ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}to each [trading]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
[subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you're faced with a problem
you might have seen before in [[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168),
which is the issue of diversification reducing your risk. If you skipped
that chapter please go back and read the concept box on [page
138](#Autopilot_ebook-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor199). Diversified portfolios
of [volatility standardised]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} assets like
[trading]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} [subsystems]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} nearly always have a lower expected [standard
deviation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of returns than the individual
assets they are trading. The lower the average [correlation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, the worse the undershooting of risk will be.

You already know that the correlation between the two equity indices and
the bond in the simple example is very low (the rule of thumb value from
table 50 in [[appendix C]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350) is
0.1, and the estimated value I calculated in [[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116) was
negative). The correlation between the[ ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}subsystem returns is likely to be even lower. So
it's very unlikely that you'll get the same desired level of average
risk from a portfolio of these three instrument subsystems as you would
if you ran each one individually.

As in [[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168)
you're going to need to apply a factor to account for the
diversification in your portfolio: an [instrument diversification
multiplier]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. You multiply the portfolio
weighted positions you've calculated by this multiplier to ensure that
the overall portfolio has the right level of expected risk.

Staunch Systems Trader

Asset Allocating Investor

[Asset allocating investors ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}and [staunch
systems traders ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}should use the
[correlations]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} between the returns of
each instrument [trading subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} to
calculate the expected degree of diversification in the portfolio.

You have the option of either using estimated correlations from a
[back-test]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, or using rule of thumb
correlations.[[[116](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-119){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-119-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Once again be warned that tables 50 to 55 (from
[page 305](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor360) in [[appendix
C]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350))
give indicative correlations between instrument returns. [Staunch
systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} can multiply these by
0.7 to give the correlation between trading subsystem returns. [Asset
allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} should use
instrument return correlations without adjustment.

Once you have the correlations, from whichever source, you have two
options for the calculation: either use the formula in [[appendix
D]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor364) on
[page 311](#Autopilot_ebook-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor369), or the
approximations in table 18 ([page
140](#Autopilot_ebook-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor200)). The number of assets
in table 18 will be the number of subsystems you are running.

Let's return to the simple three asset example. From table 50 the
correlation between bonds and equities is 0.1, and from table 54 the
correlation between the S&P 500 and NASDAQ is around
0.75.[[[117](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-118){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-118-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} I'll assume for the sake of the example that I
have a [dynamic ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}trading system, so I'm
not a [static]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} asset allocator. This
implies I should multiply correlations by 0.7, giving the correlation
matrix in table 29. The average correlation is around 0.25, and from
table 18 with three assets I get a diversification multiplier
of 1.41.[[[118](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-117){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-117-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

It's possible to get very high values for the diversification
multiplier, if you have enough assets, and they are relatively
uncorrelated. However in a crisis such as the 2008 crash, correlations
tend to jump higher exposing us to serious losses -- an example of
[unpredictable risk]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. To avoid the
serious dangers this poses I strongly recommend limiting the value of
the multiplier to an absolute maximum of 2.5.

Table 29: Correlation matrix of trading subsystems for three example
assets

  ----------------- ------ ------ --------
                    Bond   S&P    NASDAQ
  US 20 year bond   1             
  S&P 500           0.07   1      
  NASDAQ            0.07   0.53   1
  ----------------- ------ ------ --------

Correlations are 0.7 of the indicative correlations of instrument
returns from tables 50 and 54 in [[appendix C]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-9}](#Autopilot_ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350).

Semi-automatic Trader

For [semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} I recommend
using the following simple calculation. The instrument diversification
multiplier should be your [maximum]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}
numbers of bets divided by the [average]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} number of
bets.[[[119](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-116){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-116-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} So if you had an average of four bets on at any
given time, with a maximum of five, then your multiplier would be 5
[÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 4 = 1.25.

I advocate that the multiplier is limited to a maximum of 2.5, since if
you end up frequently trading more than your average then your expected
risk will be too high. As I said earlier this means it's advisable that
the maximum number of bets isn't more than 2.5 times the expected
average.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor259}A portfolio of positions and trades {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-88 .Heading-4}

This section is relevant to all readers

You're now in a position to see how a complete [trading system]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} for the simple three asset example could work.

I'm assuming that I use futures to trade these assets and that I have an
[annualised cash volatility target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of
€100,000. The price volatility and exchange rates are correct as I
write, but I've used some arbitrary forecasts to make this example
interesting but not too specific. You'll see some more specific examples
in part four.

First of all tables 30 and 31 are there to remind you of the
calculations in earlier chapters for each of the three [trading]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [subsystems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Table 30: Calculating the instrument value volatility for the three
assets in the example portfolio

+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
|          | \(A\)    | \(B\)    | \(C\)    | \(D\)    | \(E\)    |
|          |          |          |          |          |          |
|          | Price    | Block    | In       | Exchange | In       |
|          | vo       | value    | strument | rate     | strument |
|          | latility |          | currency | USD/EUR  | value    |
|          | % per    |          | vo       |          | vo       |
|          | day      |          | latility |          | latility |
|          |          |          |          |          |          |
|          |          |          | A × B    |          | C × D    |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
| US 20    | 0.52     | \$1500   | \$780    | 0.88     | €686     |
| year     |          |          |          |          |          |
| bond     |          |          |          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
| S&P 500  | 0.84     | \$1145   | \$956    | 0.88     | €841     |
| equities |          |          |          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
| NASDAQ   | 0.87     | \$880    | \$766    | 0.88     | €674     |
| equities |          |          |          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+

Values in table assume we're using futures and assuming a Euro investor.
Figures correct as of January 2015.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor260}Table 31: Calculating the
subsystem position for the three trading subsystems in the example
portfolio

+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
|          | \(F\)    | \(G\)    | \(H\)    | \(J\)    | \(K\)    |
|          |          |          |          |          |          |
|          | In       | Daily    | Vo       | Combined | S        |
|          | strument | cash     | latility | forecast | ubsystem |
|          | value    | vo       | scalar   |          | position |
|          | vo       | latility |          |          |          |
|          | latility | target   | G ÷ F    |          | (H × J)  |
|          |          |          |          |          | ÷ 10     |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
| US 20    | €686     | €6,250   | 9.11     | 10       | 9.11     |
| year     |          |          |          |          |          |
| bond     |          |          |          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
| S&P 500  | €841     | €6,250   | 7.43     | -10      | -7.43    |
| equities |          |          |          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+
| NASDAQ   | €674     | €6,250   | 9.28     | -15      | -13.9    |
| equities |          |          |          |          |          |
+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+----------+

The investor has a €100,000 cash volatility target, which implies a
€6,250 daily
target.[[[120](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-115){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-115-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-11} Forecasts are arbitrary.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor261}The [subsystem
positions]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} in column K assume one
instrument is traded with the entire trading capital. In table 32 I
bring in the [instrument weights]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and
[instrument diversification multiplier ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}that I calculated earlier in this chapter. So to
get the final[ portfolio instrument position]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} I just need to multiply each [subsystem
position]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} by the relevant instrument
weight and the multiplier which compensates for the diversification in
the portfolio.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor262}Table 32: Calculating the
portfolio instrument position for the example portfolio

+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+
|             | \(K\)       | \(L\)       | \(M\)       | \(N\)       |
|             |             |             |             |             |
|             | Subsystem   | Instrument  | Instrument  | Portfolio   |
|             | position    | weight      | dive        | instrument  |
|             |             |             | rsification | position    |
|             |             |             | multiplier  |             |
|             |             |             |             | K × L × M   |
+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+
| US 20 year  | 9.11        | 50%         | 1.41        | 6.42        |
| bond        |             |             |             |             |
+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+
| S&P 500     | -7.43       | 25%         | 1.41        | -2.62       |
| equities    |             |             |             |             |
+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+
| NASDAQ      | -13.9       | 25%         | 1.41        | -4.91       |
| equities    |             |             |             |             |
+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+-------------+

The table is using the instrument weights and diversification multiplier
derived earlier in the chapter.

The final table 33 shows how I generate actual trades. To begin with I
round the instrument position to get a [rounded target position]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. This is the first time that I've rounded in my
calculations. I then compare this to the current position which I
already have. If I'd only just started trading this will be zero, but
column P shows some arbitrary current positions to demonstrate the
logic.

Finally I can calculate the size of any trade needed. I recommend that
if the current position is within 10% of the rounded target position,
then you shouldn't trade. I call this [position inertia]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.

So for example if I had a target position of 50 crude oil contracts and
my current position was between 45 and 55 contracts then I wouldn't
bother trading. Conversely if the current position was 42 contracts
versus a target of 50, I'd buy 8 contracts to hit my target. In the
table the positions are relatively small so position inertia isn't used.

Table 33: Calculating the required trade given the rounded target
positions and some arbitrary current positions

+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------+
|              | \(N\)        | \(P\)        | \(Q\)        | \(R\)  |
|              |              |              |              |        |
|              | Portfolio    | Rounded      | Current      | Trade  |
|              | instrument   | target       | position     |        |
|              | position     | position     |              | P - Q  |
|              |              |              |              |        |
|              |              | Round (N)    |              |        |
+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------+
| US 20 year   | 6.42         | 6            | 4            | Buy 2  |
| bond         |              |              |              |        |
+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------+
| S&P 500      | -2.62        | -3           | -2           | Sell 1 |
| equities     |              |              |              |        |
+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------+
| NASDAQ       | -4.91        | -5           | -5           | None   |
| equities     |              |              |              |        |
+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------------+--------+

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor263}CONCEPT: Position inertia

[Position inertia]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is a way of avoiding
small frequent trades that increase costs without earning additional
returns.

For example suppose your desired unrounded position is 133.48 Italian
government bond futures, which is 133 contracts rounded. If your desired
position goes to 133.52, 134 rounded, you'd buy one contract. If it
drops back to 133.48 you would sell again. But the changes in desired
position were only 0.04. Is it worth buying and selling, paying two lots
of commission and market spreads, for such a small adjustment? Probably
not.

The solution is to avoid trading until the target position is more than
10% away from the current position. For Italian bond futures the first
movement in the desired position to 133.52 wouldn't result in a trade,
since your current position of 133 would only be 0.75% away from the
desired rounded position of 134. In fact the target position would have
to go to 147 blocks before you traded.

Position inertia significantly reduces trading costs, which as you'll
see in the next chapter can seriously affect your returns. My research
shows position inertia usually has a negligible effect on pre-cost
performance, so there is no downside to using
it.[[[121](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-114){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#footnote-114-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

Trades can be done manually or by an algorithm if you have a completely
automated system. There is information on how I automate my own trading
with simple algorithms on my website (see [[appendix A]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-29.xhtml#_idTextAnchor335)).

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor264}Summary for creating a portfolio of trading subsystems {#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idParaDest-89 .Heading-4}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Subsystem position               | This is the number of            |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | blocks]{.Table_strong} you hold  |
|                                  | in each [trading                 |
|                                  | subsystem]{.Table_strong}, given |
|                                  | your [forecasts,                 |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}the instrument   |
|                                  | riskiness and your [daily cash   |
|                                  | volatility                       |
|                                  | target,]{.Table_strong} as       |
|                                  | calculated in [[chapter          |
|                                  | ten]{.URL                        |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229). |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | It is in instrument blocks.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument weights               | Each [trading                    |
|                                  | subsystem]{.Table_strong} should |
|                                  | have an [instrument              |
|                                  | weight]{.Table_strong}. Weights  |
|                                  | should add up 100%.              |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | [Staunch systems traders         |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}and [asset       |
|                                  | allocating investors             |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}can use          |
|                                  | [handcrafting]{.Table_strong} or |
|                                  | [bootstrapping]{.Table_strong}   |
|                                  | to find weights.                 |
|                                  | [Correlations]{.Table_strong}    |
|                                  | can be estimated from a          |
|                                  | [back-test ]{.Table_strong}of    |
|                                  | trading subsystems, or using     |
|                                  | rules of thumb. In the latter    |
|                                  | case you should multiply         |
|                                  | instrument return correlations   |
|                                  | from [[appendix C]{.URL          |
|                                  | ._i                              |
|                                  | dGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ |
|                                  | ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350) |
|                                  | by 0.7 if you're trading a       |
|                                  | [dynamic]{.Table_strong}         |
|                                  | strategy, or by 1.0 if you're    |
|                                  | asset allocating investors using |
|                                  | fixed constant forecasts.        |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | [Semi-automatic                  |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} should   |
|                                  | use equal instrument weights of  |
|                                  | 100% divided by the maximum      |
|                                  | number of possible bets.         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument diversification       | For [asset allocating            |
| multiplier                       | investors]{.Table_strong} and    |
|                                  | [staunch systems                 |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} this is  |
|                                  | calculated using the correlation |
|                                  | of [trading                      |
|                                  | subsystem]{.Table_strong}        |
|                                  | returns. The multiplier can be   |
|                                  | calculated precisely using the   |
|                                  | formula in [[appendix C]{.URL    |
|                                  | ._i                              |
|                                  | dGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ |
|                                  | ebook-31.xhtml#_idTextAnchor350) |
|                                  | on [page                         |
|                                  | 311](#Autopilot_                 |
|                                  | ebook-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor369) |
|                                  | or approximately using table 18  |
|                                  | on [page                         |
|                                  | 140](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor200). |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | For [semi-automatic              |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} this     |
|                                  | will be equal to the maximum     |
|                                  | number of bets divided by the    |
|                                  | average number of bets.          |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | I recommend that in all cases    |
|                                  | the multiplier is limited to a   |
|                                  | maximum value of 2.5.            |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Portfolio instrument position    | Equal to [instrument subsystem   |
|                                  | position]{.Table_strong}         |
|                                  | multiplied by [instrument        |
|                                  | weight]{.Table_strong} and then  |
|                                  | by [instrument diversification   |
|                                  | multiplier]{.Table_strong}.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Rounded target position          | This is the [portfolio           |
|                                  | instrument                       |
|                                  | position]{.Table_strong} rounded |
|                                  | to the nearest integer number of |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | blocks]{.Table_strong}.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Desired trade                    | After calculating the rounded    |
|                                  | target position you compare this |
|                                  | to your actual current position  |
|                                  | and generate the necessary       |
|                                  | trade. If the current position   |
|                                  | is within 10% of the target then |
|                                  | you don't need to trade          |
|                                  | ([position                       |
|                                  | inertia]{.Table_strong}).        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

This completes the main part of the framework. I've now shown you how to
create a complete [trading system]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2},
consisting of a portfolio of [trading subsystems]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. In the next chapter we will consider how to
design systems which cope with the varying costs you'd see trading at
different speeds, and with larger or smaller amounts of capital.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {._idFootnotes}
:::

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor265}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266}Chapter Twelve. Speed and Size {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-90 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

::: {._idGenObjectLayout-1}
::: {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idContainer030}
![](image/TradingSystemComponents_chap12.png){._idGenObjectAttribute-1}
:::
:::

[S]{._idGenDropcap-1}o far everything you've seen in the
[framework]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is equally relevant to a
nimble amateur trader, holding 100 share positions for a few hours, and
the portfolio manager of a massive hedge fund, following trends lasting
for months. However in practice these two people will need to trade
quite differently to account for the frequency and size of their trades.

In this chapter I will look at how you should design your [trading
systems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} given these two interrelated
issues of speed and account size.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor267}Chapter overview {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-91 .Heading-4}

  ---------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Speed of trading                                     The thorny question of how quickly you should trade given the costs of doing so.
  Decomposing and calculating the cost of trading      How to work out what you expect to pay in costs, given which instruments you are trading and the characteristics of your trading system.
  Using trading costs to make design decisions         Once you know your costs, how you should adjust your system to cope.
  [Trading with more or less capital]{.Table_strong}   The issues of trading with relatively small or large amounts of capital.
  Determining overall portfolio size                   Having a smaller account of capital limits how many instruments you can trade. I'll explain how best to determine the size and shape of your portfolio.
  ---------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor268}Speed of trading {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-92 .Heading-4}

How fast should you trade? If you go long USD/EUR FX at 11:05am on
Tuesday 23 September 2014, should you expect to be selling at 11:06? Or
by Thursday? Perhaps you will be holding on until December, or even
March the following year?

The answer to this question depends on two things: how you expect prices
to behave and the cost of each trade. Suppose you have a crystal ball
and know with certainty that prices will increase by 10 cents in the
next week. If it costs you 1 cent to trade then you should buy as you'll
definitely make 8 cents by next
Tuesday.[[[122](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-138){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-138-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

Unfortunately crystal balls are in short supply. Instead we have
[forecasts]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} which don't provide
certainty, but hopefully shift the odds in our favour. You might have a
[trading rule]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} where after looking at the
[back-test]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you expect to get a [Sharpe
ratio (SR)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of 1.5 before costs. But this
strategy pays out two-thirds of its profits in trading costs, so the
after-cost SR is just 0.5. If you were running at a 20% [volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you'd be expecting pre-cost
returns of 30% a year, annual costs of 20% and net returns of just
10%.[[[123](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-137){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-137-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Is it wise to trade this rule?

Personally I think it is very unwise indeed. If the actual pre-cost SR
turned out to be less than 1.0, giving raw returns below 20% a year,
then the strategy will lose money after costs. You'd be trading far too
quickly relative to the available pre-cost performance.

Overtrading is a result of overconfidence, one of the [cognitive
biases]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} I discussed in [[chapter
one]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor019). Only
someone who was very bullish would assume the realised pre-cost SR would
definitely be 1.0 or over in actual trading. You shouldn't trade systems
like this and hope for the best. Instead it's much better to design
trading systems that aren't vulnerable to such high levels of costs.

In the next part of the chapter I'm going to show you how to calculate
the cost of trading a particular instrument at a given speed, and how to
work out the likely costs you'll face. Then you'll see how to construct
trading systems to ensure you won't be making more money for brokers and
market makers, than for yourself.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor269}Calculating the cost of trading {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-93 .Heading-4}

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor270}The cost of execution {.Heading-5}

The first kind of cost we'll analyse is execution cost. Back-tests
nearly always assume that when executing a trade you will pay the
mid-price. But in practice the difference between the mid and the price
you achieve will depend on how large your trades are compared to the
available volume. This difference is the execution cost. Look at figure
24, which shows a snapshot of the order book for the Euro Stoxx European
equity index future. At this point the mid-price was halfway between the
bid of 3,369 and the offer of 3,370; equal to 3,369.5.

Smaller traders can assume they will pay at most half the usual spread
between bid and offer in execution cost, which is what you get from
submitting a market order that crosses the spread and hits the best bid
or offer. If you're selling 437 contracts or fewer then the price you'd
receive is 3,369. Similarly if you're buying fewer than 8 contracts the
most you would pay is 3,370. In this example the expected execution cost
is 0.5 of a point (half of the one point spread between 3369 and 3370).
If you placed limit orders you might do better, but it's better to be
conservative and assume costs are higher than you'd hope.

This assumption is reasonable if your typical trade is less than the
usual size available on the inside of the spread. Larger traders cannot
assume this, and I discuss this further in the final part of this
chapter. Also remember that this is not a book about high frequency
trading. These assumptions are wrong if you are constantly submitting
orders every few seconds or fractions of a second, as the order book
will be affected by each of your successive trades.

Figure 24: Order book for Euro Stoxx index March 2015 futures, as of 23
January 2015 11:13 GMT
