##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor271}Types of cost {.Heading-5}

The [execution cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} I calculated above
is a key element of costs, but it's not the only one. There are also
fees: commissions and taxes to pay. Here is a list of the types of cost
you might face.

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Expected execution cost per      | This is the [execution           |
| block                            | cost]{.Table_strong} you saw     |
|                                  | above; the difference between    |
|                                  | the mid-price and what you get   |
|                                  | when you actually trade.         |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Smaller traders can usually      |
|                                  | assume they will pay at most     |
|                                  | half the usual spread between    |
|                                  | bid and offer, as in the 0.5     |
|                                  | points for Euro Stoxx in figure  |
|                                  | 24. As a percentage of 3,370     |
|                                  | this will be 0.01484%.           |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | To get the cost in cash terms    |
|                                  | multiply the percentage spread   |
|                                  | in price points by the value of  |
|                                  | each 1%.[\*]{.Footnote-reference |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-6} For Euro  |
|                                  | Stoxx futures at a price of      |
|                                  | 3,370 this happens to be €337,   |
|                                  | so the execution cost is equal   |
|                                  | to 0.01484 multiplied by 3,370,  |
|                                  | which is €5.\*\*                 |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Fee per ticket                   | Many UK retail brokers impose a  |
|                                  | per trade commission regardless  |
|                                  | of the trade size. £5 to £15 is  |
|                                  | typical.                         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Fee based on trade size          | Other brokers charge per 100     |
|                                  | shares or single futures         |
|                                  | contract traded. My broker       |
|                                  | charges €3 per contract to trade |
|                                  | Euro Stoxx futures.              |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Percentage value fee             | Certain jurisdictions charge a   |
|                                  | percentage tax on the value of   |
|                                  | the trade, such as UK stamp duty |
|                                  | at 0.5%. Some brokers also       |
|                                  | charge percentage commissions on |
|                                  | larger transactions; 0.1% is     |
|                                  | typical.                         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

\* Remember this was defined in [[chapter ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229),
'Position sizing', [page
164](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor233), as the [block
value]{.Source_strong ._idGenCharOverride-10}, the amount we make in
cash terms when a price moves by 1%.

\*\* For Euro Stoxx each one point move in the futures price costs €10.
So a 1% move of 33.7 points is worth €337.

There may also be a [holding]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} cost for
certain instruments. Futures, spread bets and contracts for difference
require rolling over monthly or quarterly. Nearly all [collective
funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} impose an annual charge. These
holding costs are usually relatively small compared to trading costs,
and they don't affect decisions about speed, so we'll ignore
them.[[[124](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-136){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-136-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor272}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor273}Standardising cost measurement {.Heading-5}

Suppose it costs €8 in total to trade one Euro Stoxx futures contract
(€5 in [execution costs]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and €3 in
brokerage fees). Is this a lot? How does it compare with €5 to trade a
German 2 year bond Schatz futures contract? Naively, €5 sounds cheaper
than €8, but the answer is not as simple as you might expect. What if
you have to buy 20 Schatz versus 10 Euro Stoxx to achieve the same
amount of risk? (This is the volatility[ scalar ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}from [[chapter ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229),
[page 170](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor244).) Despite Schatz
looking like a bargain it's going to cost you €100 versus €80 to put
equivalent positions on, so Euro Stoxx is actually cheaper to trade.

This is another opportunity to use the technique of [volatility
standardisation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. You should use a
measure of costs that accounts for how risky different instruments are.
The measure is defined as follows: if you buy one [instrument
block]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and then sell it, how much does
that round trip cost when divided by the annualised risk of that
instrument? This [standardised cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is
equivalent to how much of your annualised raw [Sharpe ratio
(SR)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you'll lose in costs for each round
trip.

To calculate it you take the cost to trade one block in instrument
currency[ ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}[C,]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} and double it because a round trip is two trades.
You then divide by the annualised [standard deviation]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of the value of one block of an instrument. This
is the annualised version of the daily [instrument currency
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} [ICV ]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}(which was defined on [page
168](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor239)). Remember that to
annualise daily volatilities you need to multiply by
16.[[[125](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-135){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-135-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} So the standardised cost will be (2 ×
[C)]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} (16
× [ICV)]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}.

An important implication of this formula is that the effective cost of
trading is higher for instruments with lower price volatility. This is
another reason to exclude very low risk instruments from your portfolio.

As I'm writing this, the price of the Euro Stoxx future has volatility
of around 1.5% a day (the [instrument riskiness ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}defined on [page
165](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor235)), and as each 1% move
is worth €337 (the [block value ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}I
discussed on [page 164](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor233))
this equates to a daily standard deviation of the contract value of €506
(the [ICV]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} or [instrument currency
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}). When I annualise this I get
506 × 16 = €8,096. Given the cost of trading at €8 per block, this is a
standardised cost of 2 × €8 divided by €8,096, or 0.002 SR units.

Like most futures this is cheap to
trade.[[[126](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-134){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-134-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Average standardised costs in 2014 for the
futures contracts I traded ranged from around 0.001 SR for the most
liquid like the FTSE 100, up to around 0.03 SR for Australian interest
rate
futures.[[[127](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-133){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-133-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

Now let's look at a spread
bet[[[128](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-132){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-132-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} on the FTSE 100, where I am betting £1 per point.
First I need to work out the [instrument currency volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Price volatility                                                                    Currently 0.75% a day.
  Instrument block                                                                    £1 per point.
  Block value                                                                         With the FTSE at 6600 a 1% move will be 66 points, the value of which at £1 per point will be £66.
  Instrument currency volatility ([ICV]{.TableData_emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-7})   This is the [price volatility ]{.Table_strong}multiplied by the [block value]{.Table_strong}: 0.75 × £66 = £49.50.
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now I can work out the costs.

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Expected execution costs per block                                                  Half the typical spread is 4 points, i.e. £4.\* There are no fees or taxes.
  Total cost per block ([C]{.TableData_emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-7})               £4
  Instrument currency volatility ([ICV]{.TableData_emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-7})   From above £49.50.
  Cost in SR units                                                                    2 × C ÷ (16 × ICV) = 2 × £4 ÷ (16 × £49.50) = 0.01 SR
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------------------------------------------------

\* This is for a quarterly forward bet -- spreads on spot FTSE are
tighter but with my typical holding period they would end up being more
expensive.[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor274}

This is about ten times larger than for the FTSE 100 future, which isn't
surprising as the spread bet is a retail product with a smaller block
size.[[[129](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-131){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-131-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Generally bets on most major indices come in
around the 0.01 SR level, although those on individual shares will
usually cost more. I'll use 0.01 SR as the benchmark cost for spread
bets.

The third type of instrument we'll consider is [exchange traded funds
(ETFs)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. Like many equities, these are
cheaper to trade in 100 share blocks. The precise cost will depend on
volatility, and the volatility for ETFs depends on the underlying assets
and whether any leverage is used. I will be conservative and check the
cost of a relatively low volatility instrument, the iShares IGIL global
inflation linked bond ETF, which is one of the ETFs I use in the [asset
allocator]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} example in part four.

  -------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Price volatility                 Around 0.43% a day in early 2015.
  Instrument block                 100 shares.
  Block value                      100 shares at \$144 per share is \$14,400. 1% of this is \$144.
  Instrument currency volatility   This is the [block value]{.Table_strong} multiplied by the [price volatility:]{.Table_strong} \$144 × 0.43 = \$62
  -------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now for the costs.

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Expected execution cost per      | The typical bid offer on this    |
| block                            | ETF is 70 cents wide or 0.486%   |
|                                  | of the current price of \$144.   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | The block value is \$144. So if  |
|                                  | I pay half the spread I'd pay    |
|                                  | 0.486 × \$144 × 0.5 = \$35 for   |
|                                  | each block.                      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Fee per ticket                   | My broker charges me \$5.00 per  |
|                                  | trade. I assume there are no     |
|                                  | other fees or taxes due.         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Total cost per block             | \$35 + \$5 = \$40                |
| ([C]{.TableData_emphasis         |                                  |
| ._idGenCharOverride-7})          |                                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument currency volatility   | \$62 (from above).               |
| ([ICV]{.TableData_emphasis       |                                  |
| ._idGenCharOverride-7})          |                                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Cost in SR units                 | 2 × C ÷ (16 × ICV) = 2 × \$40 ÷  |
|                                  | (16 × \$62) = 0.08 SR            |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

ETFs with higher price volatility, such as equities, will cost less than
this on a risk normalised basis, but to be safe I'll use the figure of
0.08 SR as my expected cost for ETFs. The costs in these examples are
correct given current market conditions and the brokerage fees I'm
paying, but you should calculate your own figures before making any
decisions.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor275}Introducing turnover {.Heading-5}

What is the point in knowing it will cost us 0.08 [Sharpe ratio (SR)
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}units, 0.01 SR or 0.002 SR, to buy and
sell a risk adjusted amount of each instrument? It's pointless having
just a standardised measure of costs; you also need a standardised
measure of how quickly you're trading. Since you know the cost per round
trip in annualised SR units you'll need to count the number of round
trips done annually, where a round trip is a buy and then a sell.

I measured standardised costs by making them [volatility standardised.
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}Similarly each buy and sell should be of
a volatility standardised quantity of [instrument blocks]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. Because of the way the [framework]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} works, this is equivalent to your expected
average absolute position, or the position you'd have with an average
sized [forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} (defined as the
[volatility scalar]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} in [[chapter
ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229),
[page 170](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor244)).

The number of round trips per year is the [turnover]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. A turnover of 1 unit means you expect to do one
buy and one sell of an average sized position per year; so your average
holding period will be 12 months. Similarly a turnover of 52 units would
imply you're hanging on to positions for an average of one week. Because
turnover is volatility standardised it means you can consider the
trading pattern of each instrument in isolation (the so called [trading
subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} that was defined in [[chapter
ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229)),
without worrying about the rest of your portfolio.

For example suppose it costs a standardised 0.01 Sharpe ratio units for
each round trip in spread bets, and your turnover is 10 units of average
position each year. Then over a year of ten round trips (ten buys and
ten sells), you'd lose 10 × 0.01 = 0.10 SR in costs. That means an SR of
0.5 would be reduced to 0.5 - 0.1 = 0.4.

##### Where does turnover come from? {.Heading-5}

Before continuing you also need to understand where turnover comes from.
For what reasons would you want to trade? Here's a list, in order of
descending importance.

+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Forecast                       | A change in forecast from one or   |
|                                | more of the [trading rules         |
|                                | ]{.Table_strong}used by [staunch   |
|                                | systems traders]{.Table_strong},   |
|                                | or because a [semi-automatic       |
|                                | trader]{.Table_strong} is placing  |
|                                | new bets or closing old ones. This |
|                                | is the main source of position     |
|                                | changes, except for [asset         |
|                                | allocating                         |
|                                | investors]{.Table_strong} for whom |
|                                | forecasts do not change.           |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Instrument currency volatility | Movements in the expected daily    |
|                                | [standard                          |
|                                | deviation]{.Table_strong} of the   |
|                                | value of each instrument block     |
|                                | ([price volatility]{.Table_strong} |
|                                | multiplied by [block               |
|                                | size]{.Table_strong}). This is the |
|                                | second most important reason for   |
|                                | position changes.                  |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Trading capital                | Changes in the amount of capital   |
|                                | you currently have at risk. This   |
|                                | is determined by your profits and  |
|                                | losses and by any additional funds |
|                                | you add or withdraw. Ignoring      |
|                                | withdrawals and injections of      |
|                                | money, with the highest            |
|                                | recommended 50% [volatility        |
|                                | target]{.Table_strong}, this will  |
|                                | change by an average of 3% each    |
|                                | day.                               |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | You can't reduce the trades that   |
|                                | result from this component, except |
|                                | by using a lower percentage        |
|                                | volatility target. So with a       |
|                                | target of 16% it would move by     |
|                                | around 1% a day.                   |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Exchange rate                  | This is the exchange rate between  |
|                                | an instrument's currency and the   |
|                                | currency your current trading      |
|                                | capital is in. Rates usually move  |
|                                | by less than 1% a day in major     |
|                                | currencies.                        |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | You can't reduce these trades,     |
|                                | which in any case are relatively   |
|                                | small.                             |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| System parameters              | These are parameters in the        |
|                                | trading system which should only   |
|                                | change infrequently if at all:     |
|                                | [Forecast weights, Instrument      |
|                                | weights, ]{.Table_strong}forecast  |
|                                | and instrument [diversification    |
|                                | multipliers]{.Table_strong}, and   |
|                                | the [percentage volatility         |
|                                | target.]{.Table_strong}            |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | You can eliminate the trades that  |
|                                | result from this element by not    |
|                                | meddling with your system!         |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+

In the rest of this chapter I will focus on how [forecasts]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and changes in [price volatility ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}influence trading speed, since these can readily
be adjusted and have the biggest effect.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor276}Estimating the number of round trips {.Heading-5}

You can now calculate the expected costs of trading each instrument,
given the units of [turnover]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of the
trading system and the [standardised costs]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. But how do you find the turnover? There are
three ways to estimate it.

+-------------------------+-------------------------------------------+
| Sophisticated back-test | If you have access to decent              |
|                         | [back-testing]{.Table_strong} software,   |
|                         | or can write your own, then you can       |
|                         | include a function that estimates         |
|                         | turnover directly.                        |
+-------------------------+-------------------------------------------+
| Simple back-test        | More rudimentary back-testing tools can   |
|                         | give you an estimate of the average       |
|                         | number of [instrument                     |
|                         | blocks]{.Table_strong} traded each year   |
|                         | and the average absolute number of blocks |
|                         | you held. You can then calculate turnover |
|                         | as:                                       |
|                         |                                           |
|                         | Average number of blocks traded per year  |
|                         | ÷ (2 × average absolute number of blocks  |
|                         | held)                                     |
+-------------------------+-------------------------------------------+
| Rule of thumb           | The third alternative is to use rules of  |
|                         | thumb. This approach is fine for          |
|                         | [semi-automatic traders                   |
|                         | ]{.Table_strong}whose turnover will       |
|                         | depend on the tightness of their stop     |
|                         | loss, [asset allocating investors         |
|                         | ]{.Table_strong}who don't get turnover    |
|                         | from forecast changes, and for those      |
|                         | [staunch systems traders                  |
|                         | ]{.Table_strong}who are using only the    |
|                         | trading rules I provided in [[chapter     |
|                         | seven]{.URL                               |
|                         | ._idGenCharOverride-4}](#A                |
|                         | utopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168) |
|                         | (the [carry]{.Table_strong} and [EWMAC    |
|                         | ]{.Table_strong}rules).                   |
|                         |                                           |
|                         | There will be relevant rules of thumb     |
|                         | later in this chapter and in the example  |
|                         | chapters of part four.                    |
+-------------------------+-------------------------------------------+

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor277}Using trading costs to make design decisions {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-94 .Heading-4}

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor278}Setting a speed limit {.Heading-5}

Let's return to the Euro Stoxx future, which had a [standardised
cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of 0.002 [Sharpe ratio
(SR)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. Suppose you have a rule with a
[back-tested ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}pre-cost return of 0.40 SR,
and a [turnover]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of 50 round trips per
year. You'd expect to pay 50 × 0.002 = 0.10 SR annually in costs and
receive 0.30 SR of net performance.

What if you had an alternative rule, with a turnover of 100, but with a
pre-cost return of 0.60 SR? Though your costs have doubled to 0.20 SR,
the net return has increased to 0.40 SR. Surely you should choose the
more expensive option?

In [[chapter three]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor093) when
I discussed [fitting]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} I pointed out that
there is always considerable uncertainty about expected pre-cost
performance. An implication of that is you need a lot of historical data
to prove that a [trading rule variation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
is likely to be profitable (as shown in table 5 on [page
71](#Autopilot_ebook-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor107)), let alone make enough
money to cover its costs.

It also means you need a lot of data to make it probable that one
trading rule had better performance in the past than another. So
considerable evidence is required to justify trading a faster, and
apparently superior, variation rather than a slower, cheaper and
supposedly inferior alternative (see table 6 on [page
73](#Autopilot_ebook-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor109)).

The relative pre-cost Sharpe ratio[ ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}you
will achieve in practice is extremely uncertain, whilst expected costs
can be predicted with much more accuracy. There is one further danger in
blindly accepting the back-test results of expensive systems. What I've
shown you in this chapter is an estimate of current costs, which could
be very different from what they were 20 years ago. In many markets
trading costs were much higher in the distant past than they are now.

A fast trading rule might look great in the past, but if the market was
very expensive nobody could have actually exploited it. Now costs have
fallen there is a good chance the effect will vanish.

To ensure you don't chase performance, or trade faster than was viable
in the past, I recommend that you set a [speed limit]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}; a maximum expected turnover you will allow your
systems to have.

At the start of this chapter I said I wouldn't trade a strategy which
paid two-thirds of its profits out as costs, amounting to 1.0 in SR
annually. I personally think it's foolhardy to pay more than a third of
your expected profits in trading costs and I use that to set my own
speed limits. If you're being realistic then the Sharpe ratio from
trading each instrument[ ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}using its
[trading subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is unlikely to be
higher than 0.4 on
average.[[[130](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-130){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-130-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

This implies you should [never]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} pay
more than a third of 0.4, or 0.13 SR, in costs, regardless of how good
your back-test is. Remember that cost in SR terms is equal to the
[standardised cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} multiplied by the
turnover in round trips per year. So dividing 0.13 by the standardised
cost of an instrument will give you the turnover speed limit for that
instrument.

For a relatively cheap futures market like the Euro Stoxx this implies a
maximum turnover of 0.13[ ÷ ]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"}0.002 = 65 round
trips per year, or an average holding period of just under a week. With
the very cheapest instruments like NASDAQ futures you might be able to
double that turnover to give a holding period of a couple of days.

However this precludes day trading, which with two or more round trips
per day would be a turnover of at least 500 round trips each year. To
trade that fast without spending more than a third of your returns on
costs you'd need to get a significantly higher pre-cost Sharpe ratio, or
reduce your standardised cost to around 0.13 [÷]{lang="ar-SA"
lang="ar-SA"} 500 = 0.00025 SR units. That level of costs is around a
quarter of what the cheapest futures can achieve.

As it happens about a quarter of the standardised costs of these super
cheap futures are commissions of around \$1 per contract, with the rest
coming from [execution costs]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. So to day
trade you'd need to get your execution costs down to zero, which means
consistently achieving the mid-price or better. To trade even faster,
with three or more round trips a day, you'd have to be getting
consistently [negative]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} execution
costs.

In conclusion then, to day trade even the cheapest instruments you will
need to achieve very high pre-cost Sharpe ratios, or have an execution
strategy that consistently captures the spread. This isn't impossible,
but clearly only those with a proven record of achieving these goals
should contemplate trading this quickly. This capability is relatively
rare in practice, which is why the vast majority of amateur day traders
are unprofitable.

With a cost of 0.13 SR a year if you're using the maximum suggested 50%
[volatility target ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}(as defined on [page
146](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor211) in [[chapter nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205)),
then you'll be paying 0.13 × 50% = 6.5% a year in costs. This is a
reasonably large drag on performance and personally I wouldn't want to
see anything bigger.

As discretionary [semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
can't use back-tests they need to avoid over-confidence about their
performance expectations. Rather than a maximum [Sharpe ratio (SR)
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}of 0.40 on each instrument, you should
conservatively expect to get at most 0.25
SR.[[[131](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-129){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-129-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} This implies paying no more than a third of 0.25,
or 0.08 SR in costs annually. [Asset allocating investors]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, whose [static ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}trading systems will have similar performance to
the underlying assets, should also use this lower figure.

You're now ready to think about how you can customise your trading
systems with an understanding of costs. There are several areas in the
framework where performance expectations are used. These decisions
should now be made using costs as well as expected pre-cost performance.

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Which instruments to trade       | It's going to be difficult to    |
|                                  | trade                            |
|                                  | [instruments]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | with costs which are too high    |
|                                  | for the kind of system we want   |
|                                  | to use.                          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Deciding average holding period  | [Semi-automatic                  |
| by setting stop losses           | traders]{.Table_strong} should   |
| (semi-automatic traders)         | use stop losses which target a   |
|                                  | particular average holding       |
|                                  | period for positions. When       |
|                                  | setting the level of these stops |
|                                  | you need to consider the costs   |
|                                  | of your preferred instruments.   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Selecting, fitting and           | If you decide to                 |
| calibrating trading rules        | [fit]{.Table_strong} your        |
| (staunch systems traders)        | trading rules then you must      |
|                                  | consider both pre-cost [Sharpe   |
|                                  | ratio]{.Table_strong} and costs. |
|                                  | Remember that you have more      |
|                                  | certainty of costs than of       |
|                                  | pre-cost performance.            |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | With my preferred method of      |
|                                  | fitting you shouldn't look at    |
|                                  | pre-cost performance but should  |
|                                  | still exclude [trading rule      |
|                                  | variations]{.Table_strong} whose |
|                                  | likely costs will be too high.   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Setting forecast weights         | You need to use likely costs to  |
| (staunch systems traders)        | avoid overweighting trading rule |
|                                  | variations which perform well    |
|                                  | but are also relatively          |
|                                  | expensive to trade.              |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Setting the risk percentage      | When checking your Sharpe ratio  |
|                                  | to set the [risk                 |
|                                  | percentage]{.Table_strong}, you  |
|                                  | should use after-cost            |
|                                  | performance.                     |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Setting the look-back for        | After [forecast]{.Table_strong}  |
| estimating price volatility      | changes this is the next key     |
|                                  | source of turnover in your       |
|                                  | [trading                         |
|                                  | systems]{.Table_strong}. The     |
|                                  | costlier your trading is, the    |
|                                  | slower you should update your    |
|                                  | estimate of [price               |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Allocating instrument weights    | You should consider giving less  |
| (semi-automatic traders and      | weight to more expensive         |
| asset allocating investors)      | instruments, unless you think    |
|                                  | additional pre-cost performance  |
|                                  | compensates you.                 |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

##### Which instruments to trade? {.Heading-5}

There are two routes to take when designing your trading systems so that
they don't exceed any [speed limits]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.
Firstly you could decide what sort of speed you wish to trade at and
then choose [instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} that are cheap
enough to trade that quickly. Alternatively if you have a preference for
particular instruments then that will limit how fast you could trade
them. So for example if you can't trade futures and prefer
[ETFs]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, then that will heavily curtail
your trading speed.

If you use my recommended maximum of 0.08 [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} units paid annually in costs for [asset
allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and [semi-automatic
traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, then table 34 gives the maximum
possible standardised cost for a given holding period and
[turnover]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. It also shows instruments
which typically have that level of cost. You can then infer which
instruments to trade for a given speed of trading, or how quickly to
trade given a preferred set of instruments.

The table also shows the lower limit on how slowly you can trade a
particular kind of system, with a minimum achievable turnover in round
trips per year. Given your limit on what you're willing to pay in costs,
you can imply from this turnover which instruments will be too expensive
to trade at all.

Table 34: What speeds and holding periods can semi-automatic traders and
asset allocating investors trade with particular instruments?

  ---------------- ------------------ ----------------- -------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Holding period   Typical turnover   Maximum cost SR   Likely instruments                                       
  1 day            256                0.00031           None                                                     
  3 days           85                 0.001             Cheapest futures, e.g. NASDAQ                            
  1 month          12                 0.0067            Nearly all futures except short maturity bonds, STIR\*   
  6.5 weeks        8                  0.01              Index spread bets, e.g. FTSE                             
  3 months         4                  0.02              Individual equity spread bets                            
  6 months         2.0                0.04              Cheapest ETFs, e.g. equity indices                       Slowest semi-automatic trader
  One year         1.0                0.08              Benchmark inflation linked bond ETF                      
  2.5 years        0.4                0.20              Most individual equities                                 Slowest asset allocating investor
  ---------------- ------------------ ----------------- -------------------------------------------------------- -----------------------------------

The table shows for different holding periods the turnover in round
trips per year, maximum instrument cost if we pay no more than the
recommended 0.08 SR a year, and typical instruments at each cost level
(estimates as of January 2015).

\* Short term interest rate futures

[Asset allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} can get down
to an annual turnover of 0.40 round trips per year, implying that a cost
of 0.08 [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 0.40 = 0.20 SR is
affordable.[[[132](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-128){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-128-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} So ETFs, which we estimated will cost at most
0.08 SR, will be fine. As I explain in the next section, [semi-automatic
traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} with very wide stop losses will
realise a turnover of 2.0 round trips a year. A cost of 0.08
[÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 2.0 = 0.04 SR is the maximum they could
pay. This rules out ETFs but allows spread bets, which we costed at 0.01
SR.

I've done a similar analysis for [staunch systems traders]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, for whom I recommend a maximum of 0.13 SR units
of cost per year. Table 35 gives the figures and I've also shown how
each level of turnover relates to the trading rules I presented in
[[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168),
'Forecasts'.

Table 35: What speed systems can staunch systems traders use?

  ------------------ ----------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Typical turnover   Maximum cost SR   Likely instruments                                                     
  128                0.001             Cheapest futures, e.g. NASDAQ                                          
  54                 0.0024            Average future, e.g. WTI Crude                                         Fastest single trend following rule I use (this the [EWMAC]{.Table_strong} 2, 8 rule)\*
  28                 0.0046            Most futures except short end bonds, equity volatility indices, STIR   Second fastest single trend following rule (this is the [EWMAC]{.Table_strong} 4, 16 rule)
  12.5               0.01              Nearly all futures, index spread bets                                  Set of rules used in chapter 15
  7.5                0.017             Cheaper individual equity spread bets                                  Slowest trend following rule I use (this is the [EWMAC]{.Table_strong} 64, 256 rule)
  ------------------ ----------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The table shows for different levels of turnover in round trips per year
the maximum standardised instrument cost if you pay no more than the
recommended 0.13 SR a year for staunch systems traders, and typical
instruments at each cost level (estimates as of January 2015).

\* Defined in [[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168),
'Forecasts', and [[appendix B]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342)
([page 294](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor345)).

I've also shown the level of turnover that you'll get with the set of
trading rules that I advocate using in [[chapter fifteen]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-25.xhtml#_idTextAnchor319),
where I explain in detail the design of the staunch systems trader
example. Staunch systematic traders who use this recommended set of
trading rules, which is fairly slow, will get a turnover of 12.5 round
trips per
year.[[[133](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-127){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-127-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} You'd get a maximum [standardised cost]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of 0.13[ ÷ ]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"}12.5 =
0.01 SR, which is fine for nearly all major futures markets and index
spread bets.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor279}Deciding average holding period by setting stop losses {.Heading-5}

Semi-automatic trader

How should [semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
determine how quickly to trade? You might think it depends on how
trigger happy you are, with itchy fingered day traders clicking in and
out every few minutes, and relaxed macro traders trading every few weeks
or months.

But I advocate that traders exclusively use a systematic stop loss to
exit positions, no matter how they decide to enter them. This means the
average holding period will depend mainly on how tight, or loose, your
stops are set. You should then ensure that your trading style, and the
horizon you are trying to predict price movements for, is in line with
your stop levels.

If stops are set very tight then you'll have short holding periods and
high [turnover]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. You won't be able to
trade more expensive [instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} with
tight stops, since with a [speed limit]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
on what you're willing to pay in [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} costs there will be a maximum viable
[standardised cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Table 34 shows that with the cheapest futures you can use a relatively
tight stop, with a holding period of three days. However this would be
far too expensive if you're using spread bets. I mentioned a particular
spread betting system in the introductory chapter which held positions
for around a week. This would have a turnover of 52 round trips per
year. The standardised cost I'm using for spread bets is 0.01 SR, so
this gives costs of 52 × 0.01 = 0.52 annually.

Earlier ([page 160](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor223)) I
calculated the implied [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of that system at 160%. This implies you'd need
to make 0.52 × 160% = 83% a year on your [trading capital]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} before costs -- just to break even!

Table 34 shows that with spread bets you can't use stops with holding
periods of less than about six weeks. Furthermore it's unlikely that any
trader will be happy with a holding period of more than six months. So
the slowest turnover you can get is 2 round trips a year, which is the
figure I used earlier in the chapter when looking at instrument
selection.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor280}Selecting trading rules {.Heading-5}

Staunch systems trader

In [[chapter three]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor093) I
said I don't like to fit or calibrate [trading rules]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} using real performance. But that prohibition only
applies to pre-cost performance. I positively encourage you to
[back-test]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and find the expected
[turnover]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of your trading rules and then
reject [variations]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} which are too
expensive.

Using my recommended guideline for setting [speed limits]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} from above you don't want costs greater than 0.13
[Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. So you should be
rejecting trading rules with turnovers greater than 130 round trips per
year for the very cheapest futures (cost 0.001 SR for each unit of
turnover), over 65 round trips for the Euro Stoxx future (cost 0.002),
and with turnovers over 13 for a spread bet with a standardised cost of
0.01 SR.[[[134](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-126){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-126-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

It is simplest to use the same trading rules and variations for all the
[instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you trade. This means you
will need to drop any rules which are too fast for the most expensive
instrument you have; remaining rules will automatically be fine for the
cheaper ones. You'll see how this is done in more detail in part four,
[[chapter fifteen]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-25.xhtml#_idTextAnchor319),
which is the example chapter for the [staunch systems trader]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.

However if the set of instruments you have features trading costs that
vary significantly you might consider using faster variations only with
the cheapest instruments, and using a slower set of variations for the
expensive ones. Costs in my own portfolio vary by a factor of 40 across
the futures that I trade, so this is the approach I use.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor281}Trading slower rules -- Weights to trading rules {.Heading-5}

Staunch systems trader

How should you determine the [forecast weights]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} to use when forming a [combined forecast]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}? Unless you think that faster rules will give you
a pre-cost [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} advantage
which compensates for their costs you're going to have less weight on
higher turnover rules, especially in [instruments]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} which are expensive to trade.

If you use [bootstrapping]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} to determine
weights on a [rolling]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} [out of
sample]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} basis then you can include a
correction for costs in estimates of returns. Bootstrapping will
automatically determine the right weights, accounting for predictable
costs and uncertain profits. By comparing the weights found when pooling
results across the portfolio with those from individual instruments it
will also tell you if different forecast weights make sense, or if you
should use the same weights.

Personally I've never found consistent evidence in my own system that
faster trading rules, whether in general or as variations of a single
rule, have a higher pre-cost [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.[[[135](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-125){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-125-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} So if you're deciding weights using
[handcrafting]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} then I recommend that you
assume the same pre-cost SR for all trading rules, but account for their
different trading costs. You can then adjust forecast weights for
trading rules based on your estimate of the SR of costs, as we did in
[[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116) on
[page 96](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor131).

[How do we do this?]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}

Remember from [[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116) that
the adjustment is done by comparing the SR of an asset versus the
average across the portfolio. For each instrument and [trading rule
variation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, you should use the cost per
round trip and the turnover to calculate the total cost in SR units per
year. If you've decided to keep the same set of trading rules for all
instruments you may want to use the same adjustments on every
instrument, which means using the highest and most conservative
instrument standardised cost to do your adjustment, rather than the
specific cost for each instrument.

On a given instrument you should then take the average cost in SR points
over all rules you are finding weights for. Because costs are fairly
predictable you should use column A 'With Certainty' of the SR
adjustment table 12 in [[chapter four]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor116)
([page 96](#Autopilot_ebook-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor131)). As an extreme
example, if you think that a particular rule is going to cost 0.13 SR
per year, versus an average for all rules of 0.03 SR, then the
performance difference is 0.10 SR worse than average and you should
multiply its weight by 0.95.

With my recommended [speed limit]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
applying to exclude any trading rules with costs of more than 0.13 SR,
the adjustments made here will never be larger than 0.95 or 1.05. In
most cases they will be much smaller and not worth bothering with.
However if you're using a higher speed limit then adjusting for trading
rule costs could significantly alter your weights.

##### More conservative estimation of Sharpe ratio to set risk percentage {.Heading-5}

This section is relevant to all readers

Back in [[chapter nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205),
'Volatility targeting', I said that you should have realistic
expectations of likely [Sharpe ratio (SR) ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}to determine the optimal level of risk to take. If
you're doing your own [back-test]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, or
estimating your likely performance in some other way, this needs to be
done on an after-cost basis.

Alternatively if you're using the suggested figures for Sharpe ratio in
the relevant chapters of part four, then there's nothing more to do as
all these assumptions are post-cost.

##### Slower estimation of price volatility {.Heading-5}

This section is relevant to all readers

After forecasts the next source of [turnover]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} is the adjustment of positions to cope with
changes in the risk of each instrument -- the [instrument currency
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, which I defined on [page
168](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor239). This in turn depends
on how quickly you update your estimate of the [standard
deviation]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of daily percentage returns,
the [price volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, which was also
defined in [[chapter ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229).

I mentioned three ways of estimating price volatility in that chapter.
Two of these methods involved formally calculating the standard
deviation of price changes over a [volatility look-back period]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, either with a [moving average]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} or an [exponentially weighted moving average
(EWMA)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. I suggested that these should be
used by [asset allocators]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and [staunch
systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, with a default
look-back of 25 business days, or 36 days with an EWMA. The other method
involved eyeballing the chart, which I recommended for [semi-automatic
traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Asset allocating investor

Staunch systems trader

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor282}If you're measuring [price
volatility ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}using a simple [moving
average]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, or the equivalent exponentially
weighted [EWMA]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, should you use my
recommended look-back of 25 days (five weeks of trading days), or a
slower look-back of ten, or even 20 weeks? On [page
168](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor238), figure 23 showed that
slower look-backs are very sluggish to react to changes in risk. My
research suggests that look-backs over 20 weeks lead to much poorer
trading system performance. However faster look-backs change very
frequently and will generate additional [turnover]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and thus cost more to trade.

First let's quantify the effect of higher costs. You need to check the
turnover for each[ trading subsystem]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.
This is all the trading that you'll be doing for a particular
instrument. It will be what you get from any changes in
[forecasts]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, plus additional turnover as
your estimate of price volatility changes. You can ignore the other
reasons for trading that I discussed earlier, as these are relatively
unimportant.

If you have multiple trading rules then you need to work out the trading
coming from your [combined forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
across all trading rules. To get this first take the weighted average,
using [forecast weights]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, of the turnover
in round trips per year for each individual trading rule. Then multiply
this by the [forecast diversification multiplier ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}to get the turnover of the combined forecast.

If you're an [asset allocating investor]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
using the 'no-rule' rule of a constant forecast then there are no
forecast changes and you'll have a zero turnover from trading rules.

You then need to apply a correction for the effect of changing your
estimate of [price volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, which
always increases turnover. At the same time, you must also account for
the effect of [position inertia, ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}which I
introduced on [page 184](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor263) --
not trading any position change greater than 10%. This slows trading
down.

You can see the net result of these two corrections in table 36. The
table shows that changing the look-back has almost no effect if the
turnover of the original [combined forecast]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} is five or more round trips per year. So if
you're trading fast trading rules, on cheap instruments, then you can
safely use the default look-back.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor283}For slower rules it will
depend on the precise costs of your instruments. Take the 'no-rule' rule
used by asset allocating investors. As before let's assume you're
trading ETFs with a [standardised cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
of 0.08. With zero turnover in forecasts you should refer to the top row
of the table. Using the default five week look-back to estimate price
volatility gives a turnover of 1.6 round trips per year. You'd have
costs of 1.6 × 0.08 = 0.13 [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} units annually. This violates the recommended
[speed limit]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of paying 0.08 SR in costs
for asset allocating investors, who are unlikely to see high performance
from their static portfolios.

In contrast with a 20 week look-back, giving a turnover of 0.40, you'd
only pay 0.40 × 0.08 = 0.032 SR. This is a quarter of the original cost,
saves a substantial 0.10 SR units, and is well within the speed limit.
Notice that a turnover of 0.4 round trips per year is the lowest an
asset-allocating investor can achieve without slowing down their
estimate of price volatility beyond the maximum recommended 20 weeks.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor284}Table 36: What effect does
slowing down [y]{lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}our estimate of price
volatility have? A slower estimate can reduce final system turnover, if
underlying trading rules are also slow
