The table shows the final turnover, in round trips per year, we get from
changing the look-back for price volatility (columns) given a starting
level of turnover from trading rules (rows). Five weeks (25 business
days) is the default look-back. Starting turnover is the average
turnover of your trading rules, weighted by forecast weights, multiplied
by forecast diversification multiplier. Position inertia is used to slow
down trading.

\* Using a simple [moving average]{.Source_strong
._idGenCharOverride-10}. For an [exponentially weighted moving
average]{.Source_strong ._idGenCharOverride-10} the relevant look-backs
are 144 days instead of 100 days, 72 days instead of 50 days, and 36
days instead of 25 days.

Semi-automatic Trader

The first method of estimating price volatility I discussed in [[chapter
ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229) was
eyeballing; looking at the chart and figuring out what a typical daily
move was. It's obviously very difficult to [back-test]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} this method as everyone's perception is
different. However you should usually use a one month window on a chart
to find the [price volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, since
this will give similar results to the 25 day [volatility
look-back]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} I recommended for more formal
[moving average]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} methods. This is fine if
you're trading cheap [instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} like
futures.

With more expensive assets, like spread bets and [ETFs]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, then I'd suggest you still use a one month
window. However I would recommend that you also compare your estimate of
the cash risk of an existing position (the [instrument currency
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} defined on [page
168](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor239)) to the value you had
yesterday. If the estimate hasn't changed by more than 25% from its last
value then don't bother to adjust it.

Just like [position inertia]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} this will
reduce any trading caused by updating the volatility estimate on
existing positions and keep your costs down. However when opening new
positions you should use the most up-to-date estimate of volatility to
work out your initial trade size.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor285}Choice of instrument weights {.Heading-5}

Asset allocating investor

Staunch systems trader

This isn't relevant to [semi-automatic traders]{.NoteAsideStrong
._idGenCharOverride-2} who don't trade a fixed set of [trading
subsystems]{.NoteAsideStrong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Suppose you're running a portfolio including a cheap
[instrument]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} like a Euro Stoxx future,
and a more expensive one like two year German bond (Schatz) futures.
Perhaps you've managed to find [trading rules ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}that make sense for each instrument and fitted
some [forecast weights ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}to them; or you
could be an [asset allocating investor]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
who is going to use the single 'no-rule' rule with a constant
[forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. As in [[chapter eleven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor251) you
must now allocate your [trading capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
and think about the appropriate [instrument weights]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} to give to each of these two [trading]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [subsystems]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

Will you get lower after-cost performance from running trading systems
for the more expensive Schatz? If so then you should obviously give it a
lower instrument weight. But this is not a clear-cut decision. Academic
research shows that the extra costs of owning less liquid instruments
are often outweighed by higher profits, such as the additional returns
you get from investing in the equities of smaller firms. As I briefly
mentioned in [[chapter two]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor039),
'Systematic Trading Rules', we tend to get rewarded for being exposed to
illiquid assets.

My own research shows that more expensive instruments usually perform
somewhat better even once costs are taken into account; but only if
they're traded relatively slowly, with a [turnover]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of less than 15 round trips per year. The
improvement isn't usually strong enough to warrant a higher allocation,
but it does mean that under-weighting costly instruments doesn't make
sense. For asset-allocating investors[ ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}(whose turnover from table 36 will be at most
1.4), and slower [staunch systems traders]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, there is no need to consider adjusting
instrument weights in light of costs.

If you're a fast systems trader, with a turnover of more than 15 round
trips per year, then my research shows that you will see worse relative
performance after costs for expensive instruments. However if you've
followed the advice I gave earlier then you wouldn't be trading them
that quickly anyway, since you'd be breaking the speed limits implied by
the recommended limit of 0.08 [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} units or 0.13 SR units, per year spent on costs.

When you stick to the speed limit then only the very cheapest
instruments would have access to quick trading rules. So in general
you'd either be trading cheaper instruments more quickly than expensive
ones, or trading all your instruments at the same relatively slow pace.
In my own research into systems with these features I don't find any
difference in after-cost returns between instruments, regardless of
their standardised costs.

In conclusion, unless [bootstrapping]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
tells you differently, I would recommend assuming the same post-cost SR
for all trading subsystems. If you're using [handcrafted]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} instrument weights this means no Sharpe ratio
adjustment is needed to account for different cost levels.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor286}Trading with more or less capital {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-95 .Heading-4}

This section is relevant to all readers

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor287}Trading with a lot of capital {.Heading-5}

Let's have another look at the order book in figure 24. An investor who
wants to sell 437 contracts or fewer can assume they will pay at worst
half the spread, 0.5 price units, as they're selling at 3,369 compared
to the mid-price of 3,369.5. But a large hedge fund which needs to sell
more than this, like 5,000 lots, has a problem.

They have three options when they submit the order. Firstly they can
cross the spread and submit a single market order. They will get 3,369
for the first 437 lots, but the next 576 lots will go for 3,368, then
620 at 3,367 and so on. The average price received per lot will be
3365.2, which compared to the original mid of 3,369.5 gives an
[execution cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of 4.3 points. The exact
cost for any given trade will depend on the depth of the order book when
it is submitted.

Secondly they can offer 5,000 lots at 3,370, adding to the 7 lots
already on the order book. They might get lucky and meet a large buyer
who takes their offer. This would beat the mid-price by half a point,
and get an effectively negative execution cost. But it's much more
likely that on seeing this huge order other traders will push the price
down, leaving the order unfilled and the market at a worse level.

Finally the funds human traders or execution algorithims can break the
order up into chunks and execute it gradually. The order will take
longer to execute and there will be a lot of uncertainty about the fill
level, and even if each chunk is small other market participants will
see the individual orders coming through and react accordingly, which
will probably result in the price drifting down.

In none of these three cases is the execution cost guaranteed to be
exactly 0.5 points. If you are trading in large size, in relatively
illiquid markets and relatively quickly, you can't casually assume you
will pay half the spread. If your typical order size is going to be
greater than the usual depth available at the inside spread, you need to
do serious research to calculate your expected trading costs, and to
work hard at optimising your execution.

##### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor288}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor289}Trading with relatively little capital {.Heading-5}

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor290}Those with much lower fund
values have a different problem. Think back to the example in table 32
on [page 183](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor262). The imaginary
investor there had a €100,000 [annualised cash volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, and after working out the target
positions they needed to short 2.62 S&P 500 futures contracts, based on
the [combined forecast]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of -10. But what
if you are an amateur investor with only €40,000? Running at the maximum
recommended 50% percentage volatility target would give you a mere
€20,000 cash volatility target. With one-fifth of the risk you'd get
one-fifth of the target position. However you can't short the 0.524
contracts this equates to. You'd have to short a rounded position of
just one contract.

With a €20,000 volatility target if your S&P 500 combined forecast
doubles from -10 to -20, you'd still be short just one
contract.[[[136](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-124){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-124-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} With a forecast of -20 if the [price
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} then doubled, you'd still
have just the single
short.[[[137](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-123){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-123-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Your risk is much 'lumpier' than if you could
trade fractional contracts. The problem occurs because the minimum size
of the S&P future is quite
big.[[[138](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-122){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-122-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

As I explained in [[chapter six]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor154),
'Instruments', the minimum block size is relatively large for many other
instruments. Equities with very high prices -- like Berkshire Hathaway A
shares, currently trading at \$220,000 each -- are obviously going to be
an issue, whilst UK spread betting firms may not allow you to trade in
bets of only £1 per point on some indices.

Apart from the S&P, many other futures contracts tend to have large
nominal values including long maturity bonds, CME currency futures, and
some energy and agricultural commodities. An extreme example is the
Japanese 10 year bond future which currently has a price of around 150
million yen, or \$1.3 million. In some cases there are mini versions of
these large contracts, but these might not be liquid enough.

Even without a large minimum it may be impractical or uneconomic to
trade small sizes. With a per ticket cost of €4 to buy ETFs it doesn't
make sense to buy in blocks of less than 100.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor291}To find out where you
could have minimum size problems you need to work through the
calculations in earlier chapters, assuming the maximum combined
forecast. This will give you the highest possible position for an
instrument given the current level of volatility. My recommended maximum
absolute forecast of 20 will give you a maximum position of 2 ×
[volatility scalar ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}× [instrument
weight]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} × [instrument diversification
multiplier]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

[Staunch systems traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} will need to
check this for every instrument they are trading. [Semi-automatic
traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} should do the same calculation
for any instruments they are planning to place bets on. [Asset
allocating investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, whose forecast is
always a constant +10, should use half of the usual figure to get their
maximum position: 1 × [volatility scalar ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}× [instrument weight]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} × [instrument diversification
multiplier.]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}

Let's check the result for the example we're using. In table 31 ([page
182](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor260)), the volatility scalar
for the S&P 500 was 7.43. This was calculated with a €100,000 cash
volatility target, so with a €20,000 volatility target it will be
one-fifth the size. Hence the volatility scalar with a lower account
size is one-fifth of 7.43, or 1.49. From table 32 the instrument weight
is still 25%, and the diversification multiplier 1.41, so the maximum
possible position then is 2 × 1.49 × 25% × 1.41 = 1.05. This is clearly
too low. No matter what happens the position will be long or short one
contract (or none).

It is a matter of judgment as to what kind of maximum position you'd
find acceptable. My recommendation is to be satisfied with a maximum
possible position of four or more instrument blocks. If you can't get to
this level you will need to consider taking one of the following
steps.[[[139](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-121){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#footnote-121-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Increase weight to instrument    | If instead of 25% you put 100%   |
| (only in moderation)             | of the example portfolio into    |
|                                  | the S&P 500 then your maximum    |
|                                  | position would be a slightly     |
|                                  | more reasonable 3 contracts.\*   |
|                                  | However any increase in weight   |
|                                  | will have to come from other     |
|                                  | instruments hence moving         |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | weights]{.Table_strong} away     |
|                                  | from their optimum, unless you   |
|                                  | also take the next option.       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Reduce overall portfolio size    | It's better to increase the      |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | weight]{.Table_strong} by        |
|                                  | reducing the number of           |
|                                  | instruments you have, but still  |
|                                  | using the correct                |
|                                  | [bootstrapped]{.Table_strong} or |
|                                  | [handcrafted]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | weights. However this still      |
|                                  | reduces diversification. I'll go |
|                                  | into more detail about           |
|                                  | determining the ideal portfolio  |
|                                  | size in the next section.        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Remove instrument                | If the instrument size is        |
|                                  | unreasonably large, and you will |
|                                  | need to significantly reduce the |
|                                  | total number of assets in your   |
|                                  | portfolio to accommodate it,     |
|                                  | then you should exclude it.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Live with it                     | You might have a large portfolio |
|                                  | with just a few instruments      |
|                                  | where size is a problem. For     |
|                                  | example half a dozen of the      |
|                                  | futures contracts in my own      |
|                                  | portfolio of more than 40 have   |
|                                  | maximum positions of less than   |
|                                  | four contracts.                  |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | On average my portfolio risk and |
|                                  | return is about right, even if   |
|                                  | it is lumpier than I would       |
|                                  | prefer on individual             |
|                                  | instruments. However I still     |
|                                  | require that maximum positions   |
|                                  | are at least one instrument      |
|                                  | block, or there is clearly no    |
|                                  | point including the relevant     |
|                                  | asset.                           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor292}

\* The instrument weight goes from 25% to 100% and the instrument
diversification multiplier falls from 1.41 to 1, as there's only one
instrument. The former increases the maximum position, the latter
reduces it but by less. The new maximum position calculation is 2 x 1.49
x 100% x 1.0 = 2.98.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor293}Determining overall portfolio size {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-96 .Heading-4}

Asset Allocating Investor

Staunch Systems Trader

Given that small account sizes present problems, how would an [asset
allocating investor]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} with a few thousand
euros decide how many of the thousands of [exchange traded
funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} to hold? How does a [staunch
systems trader]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} without millions of
dollars in [trading capital ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}work out
which of the 200 or so liquid futures contracts they should trade?

The principle you should follow is to hold the most diversified
portfolio possible without running into any problems with maximum
positions. Ideally you want at least one instrument from each major
[asset class]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. For each asset class you
should choose instruments that don't give you a maximum position that is
too small; as I said above my recommendation would be to avoid anything
with a maximum of less than four instrument blocks.

You then only add additional instruments to an asset class if this
doesn't cause a small maximum position problem to appear either in the
new instrument or in an existing one. You should also consider costs and
the other characteristics which I discussed in [[chapter six]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor154),
'Instruments'. You'll see examples of constructing these portfolios in
the relevant example chapters fourteen and sixteen, in part four.

Semi-automatic Trader

[Semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} generally have
smaller portfolios, and these are of an ad-hoc group of instruments,
rather than the fixed set used by others. But you should still check you
have no issues with low maximum positions on anything you're likely to
trade, for example by using my recommendation to have at least a four
block maximum position.

Remember from [[chapter eleven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor251),
'Portfolios', ([page 179](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor255))
that your portfolio weights are 100% divided equally by the maximum
number of bets you're likely to make, so adjusting [instrument
weights]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is not an option for dealing
with size problems. So if you have problems you should consider reducing
the size of your portfolio by lowering the maximum number of bets, or
not trading that instrument.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor294}Summary of tailoring systems for costs and capital {#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idParaDest-97 .Heading-4}

##### Standardised cost estimate {.Heading-5}

+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Cost to trade one block        | [Execution costs]{.Table_strong}:  |
|                                | Half the bid-offer spread except   |
|                                | for those with large account       |
|                                | sizes, dealing in illiquid markets |
|                                | or with very fast trading.         |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | Fixed fees per ticket.             |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | Fixed fees per position unit       |
|                                | traded.                            |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | Percentage value fees and taxes.   |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | The total cost to trade an         |
|                                | [instrument block]{.Table_strong}  |
|                                | should be calculated in the same   |
|                                | currency as the instrument's       |
|                                | value.                             |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Instrument currency volatility | The expected daily [standard       |
|                                | deviation]{.Table_strong} of the   |
|                                | value of one [instrument           |
|                                | block]{.Table_strong} in the       |
|                                | currency of the instrument, as     |
|                                | calculated in [[chapter ten]{.URL  |
|                                | ._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot |
|                                | _ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229), |
|                                | 'Position Sizing', ([page          |
|                                | 168](#Autopilot_                   |
|                                | ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor239)). |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Standardised cost              | Annualised cost in [Sharpe ratio   |
|                                | (SR) ]{.Table_strong}per round     |
|                                | trip (buy and sell):               |
|                                |                                    |
|                                | Twice the cost to trade one block  |
|                                | divided by annualised instrument   |
|                                | currency volatility (daily         |
|                                | instrument currency volatility     |
|                                | multiplied by 16).                 |
+--------------------------------+------------------------------------+

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

|                                  | (Number of[ instrument           |

|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | (2 × average absolute number of  |
|                                  | blocks held)                     |

| rules                            | [back-test]{.Table_strong} or    |

| instrument                       | trading[ ]{.Table_strong}rule,   |
|                                  | in round trips per year,         |
|                                  | multiplied by [standardised      |

|                                  | instrument.                      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Turnover from forecast changes   | [Staunch systems                 |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong}:[        |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}Can get this     |
|                                  | from back-test, or by taking a   |

|                                  | [forecast                        |

| Cost of trading subsystem        | [Turnover]{.Table_strong} for    |
|                                  | [trading                         |

|                                  | cost]{.Table_strong} for         |

##### Taking action to cope with costs {.Heading-5}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Speed limit                      | I recommend that the costs of an |
|                                  | instrument's [trading            |
|                                  | subsystem]{.Table_strong} should |
|                                  | be at most one-third of a        |
|                                  | conservative estimate of the     |
|                                  | pre-cost [Sharpe ratio           |
|                                  | (SR)]{.Table_strong}. [Staunch   |
|                                  | systems traders]{.Table_strong}  |
|                                  | should assume a maximum pre-cost |
|                                  | SR of 0.40 and others an SR of   |
|                                  | 0.25.                            |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | This implies that [staunch       |
|                                  | systems traders]{.Table_strong}  |
|                                  | should not pay more than 0.13    |
|                                  | Sharpe ratio per year in costs,  |
|                                  | and [semi-automatic traders      |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}and [asset       |
|                                  | allocators]{.Table_strong} no    |
|                                  | more than 0.08 SR.               |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Which instruments to trade       | For a given type of trader there |
|                                  | is a minimum level of            |
|                                  | [turnover]{.Table_strong} in     |
|                                  | round trips per year. Given a    |
|                                  | limit on how much you are        |
|                                  | prepared to pay in costs a year, |
|                                  | and this minimum level of        |
|                                  | turnover, you will get a maximum |
|                                  | feasible [standardised           |
|                                  | cost]{.Table_strong}. This       |
|                                  | implies that certain instruments |

|                                  | ratio (SR)]{.Table_strong} units |
|                                  | on costs would exclude all       |
|                                  | instruments with costs greater   |
|                                  | than 0.20 SR.                    |
|                                  |                                  |

|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong}, who I   |
|                                  | also suggest shouldn't pay more  |
|                                  | than 0.08 SR on costs, the       |
|                                  | lowest achievable turnover with  |
|                                  | a very loose stop loss is 1.8    |
|                                  | round trips per year, so         |
|                                  | instruments with standardised    |
|                                  | costs greater than 0.044 SR      |
|                                  | can't be used.                   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | For [staunch systems traders     |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}using my         |
|                                  | suggested set of trading rules   |
|                                  | the minimum turnover would be    |
|                                  | 12.5 round trips per year, which |
|                                  | with my recommended limit of     |
|                                  | 0.13 SR a year on costs, implies |
|                                  | that you shouldn't use           |
|                                  | instruments with costs greater   |
|                                  | than 0.01 SR.                    |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

|                                  | trader ]{.Table_strong}then I    |

##### Too much capital {.Heading-5}

  --------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  When simple cost calculations aren't enough   If you can't always trade for half the spread, due to trades larger than the typical size at the top of the order book, then you need to use more complex cost models to account for [execution costs]{.Table_strong}.
  --------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

##### Too little capital {.Heading-5}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Calculate maximum possible       | [Staunch systems traders         |
| position                         | ]{.Table_strong}and              |
|                                  | [semi-automatic                  |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} should   |
|                                  | assume the maximum [combined     |
|                                  | forecast]{.Table_strong} of +20, |
|                                  | which implies a maximum possible |
|                                  | position of:                     |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | 2 × [volatility scalar           |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}× [instrument    |
|                                  | weight]{.Table_strong} ×         |
|                                  | [instrument diversification      |
|                                  | multiplier]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | For [asset allocating            |
|                                  | investors]{.Table_strong} there  |
|                                  | is a constant forecast of +10,   |
|                                  | which implies a maximum possible |
|                                  | position of:                     |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | 1 × [volatility scalar           |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}× [instrument    |
|                                  | weight]{.Table_strong} ×         |
|                                  | [instrument diversification      |
|                                  | multiplier]{.Table_strong}       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Very low rounded maximum         | I recommend taking action if the |
| possible position                | maximum possible position is     |
|                                  | less than four [instrument       |
|                                  | blocks]{.Table_strong}.          |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Consider dropping the            |
|                                  | instrument, increasing the       |
|                                  | portfolio weight and/or reducing |
|                                  | the number of instruments traded |
|                                  | overall.                         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Sufficiently high rounded        | No action required.              |
| maximum possible position        |                                  |
|                                  | I would personally be happy if   |
|                                  | the maximum possible position    |
|                                  | was at least four [instrument    |
|                                  | blocks]{.Table_strong}.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

##### Determining portfolio size and make-up {.Heading-5}

  -------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Asset allocating investors and Staunch Systems Traders   You should hold the most diversified portfolio possible, consistent with avoiding issues with maximum positions that are too small. I recommend having a maximum possible position of at least four [instrument blocks ]{.Table_strong}in any [instrument]{.Table_strong}.
  Semi-automatic traders                                   Portfolio size is determined by the maximum number of bets. Check you won't have an issue with low maximum positions with any [instrument]{.Table_strong} before trading.
  -------------------------------------------------------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is the end of [[part three]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-13.xhtml#_idTextAnchor135).
Part four will delve further into customising the framework as we look
at three examples in detail: the [semi-automatic trader]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, the [asset allocating investor]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} and the [staunch systems trader]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.
