[[]{#Autopilot_ebook-22.xhtml#_idTextAnchor295}Part
Four.]{.PartStart_modif1 ._idGenCharOverride-5} Practice

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor296}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor297}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor298}Chapter Thirteen. Semi-automatic Trader {#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idParaDest-99 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

[I]{._idGenDropcap-1}n this final part of the book I'll show you how to
put the framework into action for three specific examples. This first
chapter is for [semi-automatic traders,]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
who make their own discretionary [forecasts]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} about price movements, but then use my systematic
[framework]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} to manage their capital and
position risk.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor299}Chapter overview {#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idParaDest-100 .Heading-4}

  --------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Who are you?          Introducing the semi-automatic trader.
  Using the framework   How you will use the systematic framework as a semi-automatic trader.
  Process               The process you need to follow each morning and as you are trading throughout the day.
  Trading diary         A diary showing how you could have traded the markets as a semi-automatic trader in late 2014.
  --------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor300}Who are you? {#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idParaDest-101 .Heading-4}

As a semi-automatic trader you want to make your own calls on the
market, but within a systematic framework. With stop losses, risk
targeting and position sizing taken care of you can focus on getting the
buy or sell decision correct. This will give you the best of both worlds
-- your human ability to interpret and process information, combined
with a system giving the correct amount of risk.

It will not be easy sticking to the framework. The system may force you
to trade, or prevent you from trading, when you would rather do
otherwise. However there are significant benefits in sticking to a
consistent strategy. I strongly recommend that you design a system
you're comfortable with and then do not deviate from it.

In the specific example discussed in this chapter we'll assume you're
going to be trading spread bets on equity indices and FX, as reasonable
sized amateur investors with a notional [trading capital]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of £100,000. You could be trading full-time, or
on a part-time basis for perhaps an hour each morning. However you can
apply this framework to any market where leverage is possible and to any
account size.

As a semi-automatic trader you'll be trading sporadically as
opportunities arise. This presents some challenges in using my
systematic framework, which normally requires a series of calculations
to be performed each time you trade.

To reduce the workload you'll first do some daily housekeeping on your
current portfolio: closing out any positions that have hit stop losses,
adjusting estimates of [trading capital ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}and [price volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}, and doing any trades on existing positions that
are now required. You'll then put on any new trades which look promising
based on overnight moves.

If you're trading part-time you will leave stop loss orders with your
broker and go to your day job. However if you're trading full-time
you'll continue to watch the markets, potentially putting further trades
on, and closing positions that hit stop losses. You won't however repeat
the more time-consuming housekeeping tasks until the next morning.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor301}Using the framework {#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idParaDest-102 .Heading-4}

##### Instrument choice: size and costs {.Heading-5}

As [semi-automatic traders]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you don't
trade a fixed set of instruments. At any given time you will have a
small number of positions, or 'bets', open. Those positions are drawn
from a larger pool of instruments that you've formed opinions on.

You'll be using quarterly spread bets, with all bets in multiples of £1
per point. For example if you go long the FTSE at £2 per point, and it
goes from 6500 to 6501, then you'd earn
£2.[[[140](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-146){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-146-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} The bet size will be determined by the broker's
minimum bet size, usually between £1 and £10 depending on the
instrument. In line with the advice in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', ([page
210](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor288)) you should check that
any position you're putting on would be at least four instrument blocks
with a maximum forecast of 20.

You should also check that the [standardised costs]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of trading any potential instrument will be 0.01
[Sharpe ratio]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} units or less, for reasons
explained in detail below. Finally you should avoid very low volatility
instruments, for the numerous reasons enumerated throughout [[part
three]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-13.xhtml#_idTextAnchor135).

##### Forecasts and stop losses {.Heading-5}

As I discussed in [[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168),
'Forecasts', ([page 125](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor178))
you'll need to have a graduated opinion on likely price movements
whenever you decide to open a new position or 'bet'. These need to be
translated into quantifiable [forecasts]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2},
as in table 37. Note if you weren't using spread bets or other
derivatives, and couldn't short sell assets, you'd need to limit
yourself to making positive forecasts.

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor302}Table 37: Translating
opinions into a quantified
forecast[[[141](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-145){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-145-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-6}

  ------------------ ------------- ------ ----------- --------- ---------- ----- ------------ -----------------
  Very strong sell   Strong Sell   Sell   Weak sell   Neutral   Weak buy   Buy   Strong buy   Very strong buy
  -20                -15           -10    -5          0         5          10    15           20
  ------------------ ------------- ------ ----------- --------- ---------- ----- ------------ -----------------

I strongly recommend that you do not change your forecast once a bet is
open. Otherwise you might be tempted to take profits too early, or
double up on losses. You can sometimes add new bets on top of an
existing position, of which more later. But you will be using a
systematic trailing stop loss rule to close all your positions -- no
other exit rules are permitted.

The stop loss rule is loosely related to the 'early loss taker' system
I've used for examples in previous chapters. It uses stops set at a
multiple of the current value of the daily [standard deviation ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}of prices.

+-------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Parameter X                   | You should set a parameter X which  |
|                               | will be related to how long you     |
|                               | want to hold positions for and your |
|                               | trading costs.\* I recommend X = 4, |
|                               | for reasons I explain below.        |
+-------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Volatility units              | The expected [standard              |
|                               | deviation]{.Table_strong} of daily  |
|                               | price changes, in price points.     |
|                               | Note we use volatility in price     |
|                               | points, not percentage points as    |
|                               | normal. The volatility in price     |
|                               | points is equal to the percentage   |
|                               | point volatility (price volatility  |
|                               | as defined in [[chapter ten]{.URL   |
|                               | ._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilo   |
|                               | t_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229), |
|                               | 'Position sizing', on '["Price      |
|                               | volatility" on page                 |
|                               | 165](#Autopilot                     |
|                               | _ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor235)), |
|                               | multiplied by the current price. In |
|                               | this chapter you'll find this by    |
|                               | eyeballing a one month chart of     |
|                               | recent price changes (as discussed  |
|                               | in [[chapter ten]{.URL              |
|                               | ._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilo   |
|                               | t_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229), |
|                               | [page                               |
|                               | 165](#Autopilot                     |
|                               | _ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor235)), |
|                               | since you're probably going to be   |
|                               | using charts to decide on your      |
|                               | entries. You need to update this    |
|                               | estimate throughout the life of the |
|                               | trade and adjust the stop           |
|                               | accordingly. (As suggested in       |
|                               | [[chapter twelve]{.URL              |
|                               | ._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilo   |
|                               | t_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266), |
|                               | you don't need to update your       |
|                               | volatility estimate unless it has   |
|                               | changed by more than 25% from the   |
|                               | previous value.)                    |
|                               |                                     |
|                               | For example as I write this the     |
|                               | standard deviation of oil prices is |
|                               | \$1.5 per day.                      |
+-------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Trailing stop loss when long  | If the price of the instrument      |
|                               | falls by more than                  |
|                               | [X]{.Table_strong} volatility units |
|                               | from the high achieved since entry  |
|                               | then you should close your          |
|                               | position. As an example suppose the |
|                               | high of crude was \$70, so with X = |
|                               | 4 and volatility units of \$1.5,    |
|                               | that implies a stop loss of \$70 -  |
|                               | (4 × \$1.5) = \$64                  |
+-------------------------------+-------------------------------------+
| Trailing stop loss when short | You will generate a closing trade   |
|                               | when a price rises by more than     |
|                               | [X]{.Table_strong} volatility units |
|                               | from the low reached after entry.   |
|                               | So again for crude if the recent    |
|                               | low was \$30, then that implies a   |
|                               | stop loss of \$30 + (4              |
|                               | [×]{lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}      |
|                               | \$1.5) = \$46                       |
+-------------------------------+-------------------------------------+

\* 'X' is equivalent to the 'B' parameter in the generic 'A and B'
system which I describe in [[appendix B]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342).

What value of X should you use? It depends on whether you have a short
or long-term forecasting horizon, and on the costs of your instruments.
So with a shorter forecasting horizon you'll cut more quickly, hold
positions for less time, and it will be more expensive to trade. It's
important to match the forecasting horizon and the tightness of your
stop loss. There is no point making a forecast of prices for the next
six months if you're likely to be stopped out by next Tuesday. Table 38
shows the average holding period for a given value of
X.[[[142](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-144){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-144-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor303}Table 38: How does the X
parameter in [y]{lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}our stop loss rule determine
[y]{lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}our average holding period? Smaller values
of X mean tighter stop losses and shorter horizons, but require cheaper
instruments

  ------------ ------------------------ -------------------------------- -----------------
  Value of X   Average holding period   Turnover, round trips per year   Maximum cost SR
  1            4 days                   64                               0.0013
  2            9 days                   29                               0.0027
  3            17 days                  15                               0.0053
  4            6.5 weeks                8                                0.01
  8            13 weeks                 4.0                              0.02
  10           26 weeks                 2.0                              0.04
  ------------ ------------------------ -------------------------------- -----------------

The table shows for each value of X (rows): the average holding period,
in business days or weeks; the implied turnover in round trips per year;
and the maximum instrument cost if we pay no more than 0.08 Sharpe ratio
units per year. The turnover includes the additional trading we get from
changes in price volatility and also assumes we don't trade position
changes of less than 10% (position inertia).

As you saw in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', you need to adjust X given the cost of the
[instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you're trading. The final
column of table 38 shows a suggested maximum feasible [standardised
cost]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, in [Sharpe ratio (SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} units per year, for a given value of X. This is
calculated using my recommended [speed limit]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of spending 0.08 SR units per year on costs, from
[[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266).

Also in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266)
([page 193](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor274)) I suggested you
use a costs figure of 0.01 SR units as the standardised cost for all of
your spread bets. The table shows you could safely use an X of 4 or
higher with spread bets, although if you're trading cheap futures
contracts you could go quicker.

I don't advise using different stop loss systems for different
instruments as this complicates life and can lead to mistakes. So the
value of X should be set conservatively using the cost of the most
expensive instrument you're likely to trade.

In this chapter we are going to use an X of 4, which means I assume
you're predicting trends that last for just over six weeks.

Note that unlike in the original 'early loss taker' system you won't be
using a profit target. Why not? My own research shows no evidence that
systematic profit targets work
consistently.[[[143](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-143){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-143-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} This is because most markets exhibit trending
behaviour, and a profit target will get you out of trends too early.

To summarise you mustn't close your trade until you hit a stop loss;
then you must close it. Sticking to this rule will be difficult, but
will mean that your risk is controlled and that your returns will have
the favourable positive [skew ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}of [trend
following]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} traders.

##### Volatility targeting {.Heading-5}

I've already set the initial[ trading capital]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of this example at £100,000. But how aggressive
should you be with this money -- what should your [percentage]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} be? You need to refer back to [[chapter
nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205),
'Volatility Targeting'. In this example I am going to assume that your
[Sharpe ratio]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} [(SR)]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} will be at least 0.30 after costs, which I
calculate below as 0.08 SR units. This is comfortably under my
recommended maximum expectation of 0.50 (from [page
52](#Autopilot_ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor088)). Referring to column D
of table 26 ([page 158](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor220)) an
SR of 0.30 gives a risk percentage of 15%, and I'm assuming you can cope
with the pain that implies.

This implies you'll have an annual [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} of £100,000 × 15% = £15,000 and a daily target of
£15,000[ ÷ ]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"}16 =
£937.50.[[[144](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-142){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-142-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Because you're using spread bets there is no
concern about hitting a relatively modest risk percentage of 15%. As
always you should avoid low volatility assets where the required
leverage will be too high.

You'll be measuring your account value daily, recalculating [trading
capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, and adjusting your [volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} accordingly. This might trigger
trades on existing positions and the new volatility target will be used
to size any new bets you make.

##### Position sizing {.Heading-5}

You can now think about 'Position sizing', as discussed in [[chapter
ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229).
First you need to measure the recent percentage [standard deviation
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}of returns ([price volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}) in each market to determine the expected daily
variation in the value of one [instrument block]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} (the [instrument currency volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}).

I'd suggest you use the eyeball technique described in [[chapter
ten]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor229)
([page 165](#Autopilot_ebook-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor237)), which you're
already using to calculate the volatility estimate needed for stop loss
levels. This should be done daily on any instruments where you have
positions, and ideally on anything you might place bets on
today.[[[145](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-141){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-141-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Changes in price volatility for your existing
positions might trigger trades.

From table 38 ([page 224](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor303))
with the suggested X of 4 you would expect to get a turnover of 8 after
accounting for the effects of sizing your position. With [standardised
costs]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of 0.01 SR units for spread bets
this gives us an annual cost of 8 × 0.01 = 0.08 [Sharpe ratio (SR)
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}units, which just squeaks in at the
maximum level of 0.08 SR that I recommended in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size'.

With 15% annualised volatility that equates to a performance drag from
costs of 0.08 × 15% = 1.2% a year. This is more than most [passive
ETFs]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, but significantly cheaper than a
hedge fund. You'd also have to pay perhaps 0.6% for rolling your
quarterly
bets.[[[146](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-140){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-140-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

You can save on costs by following the advice in the [[chapter
twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', and not adjusting your estimate of [instrument
currency volatility ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}if the previous
level looks about right, say within 25% of the current value.

As you're spread betting, the value of a 1% move (the [block
value]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}) depends on the bet size in pounds
per point, so you won't need to use exchange rates even when betting on
non-UK markets. So for example with crude oil at \$65, a 1% move of
\$0.65 or 0.65 points, at a broker's minimum of £10 a point, will have a
value of £6.50.

With this information you can use your [volatility target ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}to work out the [volatility scalars ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}every morning to use for both existing and
potential trades.

##### Portfolios {.Heading-5}

To size your bets you need to determine your preferred
[maximum]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4} and [average]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} number of concurrent bets, as I explained in
[[chapter eleven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor251),
'Portfolios'. These terms are self explanatory: the maximum number of
bets is the most positions you will have on at any given time, and the
average is what you expect to have on a typical day. As I said in
[[chapter eleven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor251), the
[instrument weight]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} you use to size each
of your positions will be 100% divided by the maximum number of bets.

The [instrument diversification multiplier]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} will be the maximum number of bets divided by the
average. This multiplier ensures the total average absolute forecast
will be 10 with the average number of bets on, which ensures your
forecasting is consistent with the rest of the systematic
[framework]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. I recommended that the
multiplier shouldn't be greater than 2.5, which limits the value of the
maximum relative to the average.

If you already have your maximum number of bets on, and you desperately
need to make a new bet, then sorry: you can't. Wait until one of your
existing bets has stopped out. If they all continue to be profitable
then perhaps your new bet wasn't necessary after all. Although this may
seem rigid, sticking to this ensures that you have the correct risk on
for each bet and it will stop you from putting on dangerous numbers of
simultaneous bets. Make sure you set your maximum high enough when
designing your system to cover your likely appetite for extra bets.

Earlier I said you shouldn't change the forecast on an existing bet.
However you can add to positions by placing a new, separate, bet on an
instrument which you're already holding. This allows pyramiding
positions, such as buying into a strengthening trend. The new bet is
completely distinct, will have its own stop loss, and could be closed at
a different time. A new bet on an existing position counts towards your
maximum number of bets.

This can't be used as a loophole to close positions; you can't put on a
new bet which will make your existing position smaller or reverse it.
Finally, to avoid concentrating your risk too heavily I strongly
recommend that you don't have a total of more than 40 forecast units bet
on any asset at once, added up over all the bets on that instrument. So
a forecast of +10 on your first bet, followed by +25 on your second for
the same instrument would be acceptable. But you couldn't place a third
bet of more than +5.

In this chapter I am going to assume you have a maximum of four
positions, with an average of three. So each position will have an
instrument weight of 100% [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 4 = 25%, and
the diversification multiplier is 4 [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} 3 =
1.33.

As you saw in [[chapter nine]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor205),
'Volatility targeting', ([page
160](#Autopilot_ebook-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor223)) the use of systematic
stop losses allows you to compare my framework with traditional money
management systems, where you put a fixed percentage of your capital at
risk. Each bet you make puts at risk a percentage of capital equal to:

(X × forecast × [percentage volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}) [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} (10 × 16 ×
average number of bets)

In this example with an X of 4, the average forecast of 10, a 15%
volatility target and an average of three positions you are putting (4 ×
10 × 15%) [÷]{lang="ar-SA" lang="ar-SA"} (10 × 16 × 3) = 1.25% of your
capital at risk on average per bet. With the maximum forecast of 20
you'd be risking twice that, 2.5%. This is relatively large, but
remember that you expect to be holding these positions for around six
weeks.

##### Trading in practice {.Heading-5}

You should first check to see if any stop losses on existing positions
have been hit, and trade on those immediately if you didn't leave stop
losses with your
broker.[[[147](#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-139){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#footnote-139-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} Then you can look at potential new entries and
come up with a numeric forecast for them. On every new trade you'll need
to calculate a stop loss based on the value of X, the [price
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and the entry price.

Stop losses on existing positions will also need to be adjusted if price
volatility changes. They also need to be moved up if prices reach new
highs (for longs), and down for new lows (on shorts).

Part-time daily traders should then update the stop loss limits they
have left with their brokers and go to work at their real jobs. If
you're still trading intra-day you will continue to monitor the market
for new entries and to see if stops have been hit. You could also update
stop loss levels intra-day if significant new levels are reached, but
with the relatively large value of X I've suggested for this chapter
this probably isn't necessary.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor304}Process {#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idParaDest-103 .Heading-4}

Here is the process to follow when running this system. The trading
diary in the next section illustrates in more detail how the
calculations are done.

##### Daily housekeeping {.Heading-5}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Check stops                      | If not using stop orders left    |
|                                  | with brokers check to see if any |
|                                  | stop levels have been hit on     |
|                                  | existing positions, and if       |
|                                  | necessary do closing trades.     |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Get account value                | Get today's account value and    |
|                                  | calculate your current [trading  |
|                                  | capital]{.Table_strong}. Your    |
|                                  | [annualised cash]{.Table_strong} |
|                                  | [volatility target               |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}is current       |
|                                  | capital × [percentage volatility |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} (In this  |
|                                  | chapter we use 15%).             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Daily cash volatility target     | Daily cash volatility target     |
|                                  | equals one-sixteenth of          |
|                                  | [annualised cash]{.Table_strong} |
|                                  | [volatility                      |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong}. (As      |
|                                  | usual this is the 'square root   |
|                                  | of time' rule; with around 256   |
|                                  | business days in a year you      |
|                                  | should divide by 16 to go from   |
|                                  | annual to daily risk.)           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Get latest prices                | Get charts for all instruments   |
|                                  | you have positions on and        |
|                                  | ideally anything you might wish  |
|                                  | to trade today.                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Calculate daily price volatility | Using a one month chart eyeball  |
|                                  | the [price                       |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong} of    |
|                                  | each instrument you own, or      |
|                                  | might trade today. With FX rate  |
|                                  | of 1 and relevant [block         |
|                                  | values]{.Table_strong} convert   |
|                                  | this to [instrument value        |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | For                              |
|                                  | [existing]{.TableData_emphasis   |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-7} positions |
|                                  | if the volatility level you used |
|                                  | yesterday is not within 25% of   |
|                                  | today's level then update your   |
|                                  | estimate; otherwise stick with   |
|                                  | yesterday's estimate. For        |
|                                  | [potential]{.TableData_emphasis  |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-7} positions |
|                                  | you should always use the most   |
|                                  | up-to-date estimate.             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Recalculate stops                | Update stops on existing         |
|                                  | instruments so they trail new    |
|                                  | highs (for longs) or new lows    |
|                                  | (for shorts), and account for    |
|                                  | the latest value of [price       |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Volatility scalar                | This is equal to the [daily cash |
|                                  | volatility                       |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} divided   |
|                                  | by instrument value volatility   |
|                                  | for each instrument.             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Desired subsystem position on an | Original                         |
| existing bet                     | [forecast]{.Table_strong}        |
|                                  | multiplied by [volatility        |
|                                  | scalar, ]{.Table_strong}divided  |
|                                  | by 10.                           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Desired portfolio instrument     | Desired subsystem position       |
| position on an existing bet      | multiplied by instrument weight  |
|                                  | and by instrument                |
|                                  | diversification multiplier.      |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | With the maximum of four bets    |
|                                  | used in the example, and an      |
|                                  | average of 3 bets, you should    |
|                                  | use an instrument weight of 25%  |
|                                  | and a multiplier of 1.33.        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Desired target position on an    | [Portfolio instrument            |
| existing bet                     | position]{.Table_strong} rounded |
|                                  | to nearest whole block.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Issue trades on existing         | Compare current position to      |
| positions                        | target position. If out by more  |
|                                  | than 10% then issue adjusting    |
|                                  | orders ([position                |
|                                  | inertia]{.Table_strong}).        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Check setups                     | Check charts and news for        |
|                                  | potential new bets. If any exist |
|                                  | go to instructions for new       |
|                                  | position opening.                |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

##### Further intra-day process (for full-time traders only) {.Heading-5}

  ------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Check stop losses   Check to see if any stop losses have been triggered. If not using broker stops then issue closing trades.
  Check setups        Check charts and news for potential trade setups. If any exist go to instructions for new position opening.
  ------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

##### New position opening {.Heading-5}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Calculate forecast               | Use table 37 ([page              |
|                                  | 223](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor302)) |
|                                  | to calculate your forecast for   |
|                                  | the instrument.                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Is it allowed?                   | Do you already have the maximum  |
|                                  | number of bets on? (For the      |
|                                  | example system this is four.)    |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Is the bet on an instrument with |
|                                  | an existing position? If so it   |
|                                  | must not reduce or reverse the   |
|                                  | position, or put the total       |
|                                  | absolute forecast across all     |
|                                  | bets for this instrument over    |
|                                  | 40.                              |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Calculate instrument value       | If not pre-calculated in the     |
| volatility                       | morning, using a one month chart |
|                                  | eyeball the [price               |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong} of    |
|                                  | each instrument. With FX rate of |
|                                  | 1 and relevant [block            |
|                                  | values]{.Table_strong} convert   |
|                                  | this to [instrument value        |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Calculate stop loss              | Use X multiplied by the daily[   |
|                                  | price volatility]{.Table_strong} |
|                                  | in price points to find where to |
|                                  | set the stop loss relative to    |
|                                  | the entry price. In this chapter |
|                                  | I've used an X of 4.             |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Volatility scalar                | This morning's [daily cash       |
|                                  | volatility                       |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} divided   |
|                                  | by instrument value volatility   |
|                                  | for the instrument.              |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Subsystem position               | [Forecast]{.Table_strong}        |
|                                  | multiplied by [volatility        |
|                                  | scalar, ]{.Table_strong}divided  |
|                                  | by 10.                           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Portfolio instrument position    | Subsystem position multiplied by |
|                                  | instrument weight (25% in the    |
|                                  | example) and then multiplied by  |
|                                  | instrument diversification       |
|                                  | multiplier (1.33 in the          |
|                                  | example).                        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Rounded target position          | Portfolio instrument position    |
|                                  | rounded to nearest block.        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Trade                            | You should now put on the trade  |
|                                  | and once completed calculate the |
|                                  | initial stop loss relative to    |
|                                  | the entry price. With judicious  |
|                                  | use of spreadsheets the above    |
|                                  | steps can be completed in a few  |
|                                  | moments.                         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor305}

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor306}Trading diary {#Autopilot_ebook-23.xhtml#_idParaDest-104 .Heading-4}

Here is some paper trading I did using the semi-automatic trading
system. All the calculations here have been done with a spreadsheet,
which is available from my website. Prices and other values are rounded
to make the example clearer.

##### 15 October 2014 {.Heading-5}

Typical minimum spread bets are £10 a point on crude, £5 on US S&P 500
equities and £1 on the European Euro Stoxx equity index. These and the
price determine the [block value. ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}Note
that the block values are all in British pounds, the same currency as my
account, even though the prices are quoted in other currencies, as we're
betting in £1 per point. So the FX rates are all 1. Starting [trading
capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} is £100,000; annual [volatility
target]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} at 15% is £15,000 and starting
daily volatility target is one sixteenth of that, £937.50.

+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
|                                    | Crude   | S&P 500 | Euro Stoxx |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Daily volatility target (A)        | £937.50 | £937.50 | £937.50    |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Price (B)                          | 83      | 1880    | 2900       |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Bet size, per point (C)            | £10     | £5      | £1         |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| FX (D)                             | 1       | 1       | 1          |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Block value (E)                    | £8.30   | £94     | £29        |
|                                    |         |         |            |
| C × B ÷ 100                        |         |         |            |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Price volatility, points (F)       | 1       | 20      | 50         |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Price volatility, % (G)            | 1.20    | 1.06    | 1.72       |
|                                    |         |         |            |
| F ÷ B                              |         |         |            |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Instrument currency volatility (H) | £10     | £100    | £50        |
|                                    |         |         |            |
| G × E                              |         |         |            |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Instrument value volatility (I)    | £10     | £100    | £50        |
|                                    |         |         |            |
| H × D                              |         |         |            |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+
| Volatility scalar (J)              | 93.75   | 9.375   | 18.75      |
|                                    |         |         |            |
| A ÷ I                              |         |         |            |
+------------------------------------+---------+---------+------------+

For my forecasts I'm feeling very bullish on US equities, bullish on
crude and bearish on European equities.

+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
|                                               | Crude | S&P 500 | Euro Stoxx |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Forecast (K)                                  | 10    | 15      | -10        |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Subsystem position, blocks (L)                | 93.75 | 14.06   | -18.75     |
|                                               |       |         |            |
| K × J ÷ 10                                    |       |         |            |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Instrument weight (M)                         | 25%   | 25%     | 25%        |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Instrument diversification multiplier (N)     | 1.33  | 1.33    | 1.33       |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Portfolio instrument position, blocks (O)     | 31.25 | 4.687   | -6.2       |
|                                               |       |         |            |
| L × M × N                                     |       |         |            |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Rounded target position, blocks (P = round O) | 31    | 5       | -6         |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Entry price (Q)                               | 83    | 1880    | 2900       |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Stop loss offset (R)                          | 4     | 80      | 200        |
|                                               |       |         |            |
| F × X with X = 4                              |       |         |            |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+
| Stop loss (S)                                 | 79    | 1800    | 3100       |
|                                               |       |         |            |
| Q plus or minus R                             |       |         |            |
+-----------------------------------------------+-------+---------+------------+

So I go long crude at 31 × £10 = £310 a point with a \$79 stop loss,
long S&P 500 at 5 × £5 = £25 per point with an 1800 stop loss, and short
Euro Stoxx at 6 × £1 = £6 per point with a 3100 stop loss.

##### 29 October 2014 {.Heading-5}

  ------------------------------------------- -------- --------- ------------
                                              Crude    S&P 500   Euro Stoxx
  Forecast                                    10       15        -10
  Entry price                                 83       1880      2900
  Current price                               81       1980      3020
  High (Low) since entry (T)                  83       1980      \(2900\)
  Stop loss (S = T plus or minus R)           79       1900      3100
  Current position, blocks                    31       5         -6
  Gain (Loss)                                 (£620)   £2,500    (£720)
  Portfolio instrument position, blocks (O)   31.5     4.73      -6.3
  Rounded target position, blocks (P)         32       5         -6
  ------------------------------------------- -------- --------- ------------

No stops triggered and the trailing stop loss for S&P 500 has been moved
up. The net profit in my account is £1,160, so current capital is
£101,160 and the daily volatility target is £948. The [price
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and [instrument value
volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} hasn't changed on any
instrument by more than 25%, so I haven't adjusted these. Current
positions are still within 10% of the target; there is no need to buy
one more [instrument block]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of crude to
get to a rounded position of 32.

I feel very strongly about the S&P bet now and I'm going to add to my
position. So I will bet a separate +25 forecast, taking me up to the
maximum of +40 for all S&P 500 bets. It will also help hedge my Euro
Stoxx short, which is doing very badly though hasn't yet hit it's stop.
I now have four bets on, two of which are in S&P 500, so I can't place
any more.

+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
|               | S&P 500 (2) |   |               | S&P 500 (2) |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Daily         | £948        |   | Forecast (K)  | 25          |
| volatility    |             |   |               |             |
| target (A)    |             |   |               |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Price (B)     | 1980        |   | Subsystem     | 23.7        |
|               |             |   | position (L)  |             |
|               |             |   |               |             |
|               |             |   | K × J ÷ 10    |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Bet size, per | £5          |   | Instrument    | 25%         |
| point (C)     |             |   | weight (M)    |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| FX (D)        | 1           |   | Instrument    | 1.33        |
|               |             |   | di            |             |
|               |             |   | versification |             |
|               |             |   | multiplier    |             |
|               |             |   | (N)           |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Block value   | £99         |   | Portfolio     | 7.88        |
| (E)           |             |   | instrument    |             |
|               |             |   | position (O)  |             |
| C × B ÷ 100   |             |   |               |             |
|               |             |   | L × M × N     |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Price         | 20          |   | Rounded       | 8           |
| volatility,   |             |   | target        |             |
| points (F)    |             |   | position, and |             |
|               |             |   | trade (P =    |             |
|               |             |   | round O)      |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Price         | 1.01        |   | Entry price   | 1980        |
| volatility, % |             |   | (Q)           |             |
| (G)           |             |   |               |             |
|               |             |   |               |             |
| F ÷ B         |             |   |               |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Instrument    | £100        |   | Stop loss     | 80          |
| currency      |             |   | offset (R)    |             |
| volatility    |             |   |               |             |
| (H)           |             |   | F × X with X  |             |
|               |             |   | = 4           |             |
| G × E         |             |   |               |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Instrument    | £100        |   | Stop loss (S) | 1900        |
| value         |             |   |               |             |
| volatility    |             |   | Q plus or     |             |
| (I)           |             |   | minus R       |             |
|               |             |   |               |             |
| H × D         |             |   |               |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+
| Volatility    | 9.46        |   |               |             |
| scalar (J)    |             |   |               |             |
|               |             |   |               |             |
| A ÷ I         |             |   |               |             |
+---------------+-------------+---+---------------+-------------+

I go long another eight £5 a point blocks of S&P 500. Because we're
trading at a recent high this has the same stop level as my existing
bet.

##### 4 November 2014 {.Heading-5}

Sadly my long in crude is now closed.

  ------------------------------------------- --------------------- ------------- ------------- ------------
                                              Crude                 S&P 500 (1)   S&P 500 (2)   Euro Stoxx
  Forecast                                    10                    15            25            -10
  Entry price                                 83                    1880          1980          2900
  Current price                               78.7                  2018          2018          3034
  High (Low) since entry (T)                  83                    2018          2018          \(2900\)
  Stop loss (S = T plus or minus R)           79                    1938          1938          3100
  Current Position, blocks                    31                    5             8             -6
  Gain (Loss)                                 (£1,333)              £3,450        £1,520        (£804)
  Portfolio instrument position, blocks (O)   0.0 (Hit stop loss)   4.81          8.01          -6.43
  Rounded target position, blocks (P)         0                     5             8             -6
  ------------------------------------------- --------------------- ------------- ------------- ------------

The net gain is £2,833, so trading capital is up to £102,833 and daily
volatility target is £964. Again [price volatility]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} hasn't changed significantly on any instrument.
Apart from closing crude there are no adjustment trades needed. I'm
tempted to take profits on my only profitable trade, the S&P 500 longs,
but I can't because it isn't part of the system!

##### 13 November 2014 {.Heading-5}

I decide, belatedly, to short crude at \$75 with a forecast of -10 which
corresponds to a position of short 32 blocks with a stop of \$79.

##### 21 November 2014 {.Heading-5}

Ouch! Euro Stoxx has also bitten the dust.

  ------------------------------------------- ----------- ------------- ------------- --------------
                                              Crude (2)   S&P 500 (1)   S&P 500 (2)   Euro Stoxx
  Forecast                                    -10         15            25            -10
  Entry price                                 75          1880          1980          2900
  Current price                               76          2063          2063          3130
  High (Low) since entry (T)                  \(75\)      2063          2063          \(2900\)
  Stop loss (S = T plus or minus R)           79          1983          1983          3100
  Position                                    -32         5             8             -6
  Gain (Loss)                                 (£320)      £4,575        £3,320        (£1,380)
  Portfolio instrument position, blocks (O)   -32.8       4.9           8.2           0.0 Hit stop
  Rounded target position, blocks (P)         -33         5             8             0
  ------------------------------------------- ----------- ------------- ------------- --------------

My accumulated profit is £4,862. If I wasn't trading systematically I'd
also be tempted to cut my oil short, as I don't seem to be calling crude
very well.

##### 28 November 2014 {.Heading-5}

My crude oil is finally paying off -- crude drops \$5 in one day! The
current [price volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of \$1 per day
for crude now looks too low, so I'm going to double it to \$2. I'm up
£7,682 in accumulated profits, so the daily [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} is £1,010.

+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
|                                           | Crude (2) | S&P 500 (1) | S&P 500 (2) |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Daily volatility target (A)               | £1,010    | £1,010      | £1,010      |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Forecast                                  | -10       | 15          | 25          |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Entry price                               | 75        | 1880        | 1980        |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Current price                             | 68        | 2067        | 2067        |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Price volatility, points (F)              | 2         | 20          | 20          |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Stop loss offset (R)                      | 8         | 80          | 80          |
|                                           |           |             |             |
| F × X with X=4                            |           |             |             |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| High (Low) since entry (T)                | \(68\)    | 2067        | 2067        |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Stop loss (S)                             | 76        | 1987        | 1987        |
|                                           |           |             |             |
| T plus or minus R                         |           |             |             |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Position, blocks                          | -32       | 5           | 8           |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Gain / Loss £                             | £2,240    | £4,675      | £3,480      |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Instrument value volatility (I)           | £20       | £100        | £100        |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Volatility scalar (J)                     | 50.5      | 10.1        | 10.1        |
|                                           |           |             |             |
| A ÷ I                                     |           |             |             |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Portfolio instrument position, blocks (O) | -16.8     | 5.05        | 8.41        |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Rounded target position, blocks (P)       | -17       | 5           | 8           |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+
| Trade                                     | Buy 15    | No          | No          |
+-------------------------------------------+-----------+-------------+-------------+

I have to reduce my crude bet and buy 15 blocks of crude to get to my
target position. Primarily because of the change in [price volatility
]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}the current position was more than 10%
away from the desired rounded [portfolio instrument position]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}. Note the stop loss gap in crude is also doubled
to 4 × \$2 = \$8, which is added to the new low of \$68 to give a stop
level of \$76, but my position is virtually halved so I have the same
amount of capital at risk on this bet.

Although this looks like taking profits it is not; the system is trading
automatically to keep the amount of capital at risk constant. If I
hadn't adjusted the stop then the expected holding period of my bet
would have shortened. Because I've adjusted the stop I also need to cut
the position, or I'll have increased the risk on the trade.

This feels like a good place to stop this diary as I've shown most of
the system's most interesting features. Notice how the system went
against my natural instincts when I wanted to close trades too early, or
let losses run. Hopefully this should show you the benefits of
rigorously sticking to a position management [framework]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} once you've designed it.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {._idFootnotes}
:::

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor307}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor308}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor309}Chapter Fourteen. Asset Allocating Investor {#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idParaDest-105 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

[T]{._idGenDropcap-1}his chapter is for [asset allocating
investors]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} who mostly don't believe that
assets' prices can be forecasted, and use the 'no-rule' trading rule
within a systematic [framework ]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}to
allocate [trading capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} between
different assets.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor310}Chapter overview {#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idParaDest-106 .Heading-4}

  -------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Who are you?                                                   Introducing the asset allocating investor.
  Using the framework                                            How you will use the systematic framework for asset allocation.
  []{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor311}Weekly process   The weekly process that asset allocators should use.
  Trading diary                                                  A diary showing how you could have invested over a few hectic weeks of 2008.
  -------------------------------------------------------------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor312}Who are you? {#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idParaDest-107 .Heading-4}

If you're an [asset allocating investor]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}
you believe the best returns can be obtained by investing in a
diversified portfolio of assets without trying to predict relative risk
adjusted returns. The systematic [framework]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} in this book will allow you to do that, using the
'no rule' rule I introduced in [[chapter seven]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168),
'Forecasts'. This rule has a constant forecast of +10, implying you
think all assets will have the same [Sharpe ratio]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.

To get access to different[ asset classes]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} I assume you'll be using [exchange traded funds
(ETFs)]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}, which are relatively cheap
[collective funds]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. These will allow you
to access a wide range of asset classes whilst only holding positions in
a relatively small number of instruments. I am assuming that you're not
comfortable, or unable, to use leverage or to do [short selling]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}.[[[148](#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#footnote-151){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#footnote-151-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3} In the example of this chapter I use a notional
[trading capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} of €10,000,000, the
size of a small pension fund, although everything in this chapter is
relevant to those with much larger and smaller portfolios.

Your imaginary pension fund trustees have specified some constraints on
the portfolio. You can only invest in bonds and equities, and no more
than 40% of your capital can be allocated to bonds. Within the equity
portfolio no more than 30% can be put into emerging markets. In bonds
you can't put more than 25% into emerging markets and no more than 25%
into inflation linked bonds. All constraints are expressed in risk
adjusted proportions.

An extension to the basic example will be to adjust [instrument
weights]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} according to some expectations
about asset [Sharpe ratios]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}. These aren't
produced by a systematic [trading rule]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2},
but this setup is common in institutional investing when allocations
need to be adjusted to reflect in-house or consultants' strategic views.

#### []{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor313}Using the framework {#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#_idParaDest-108 .Heading-4}

##### Instrument choice: diversification, costs, volatility and size {.Heading-5}

You have quite a large account size, so in theory you could have an
allocation to a large number of ETFs without having any difficulties
with the minimum size problem I discussed in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size' ([page
210](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor288)). However to make this
example more tractable I am going to limit your portfolio to just ten
[instruments]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

In selecting ETFs I've generally gone for those with the lowest total
expense ratio (this is an annual holding cost you pay regardless of how
often you trade). Additionally, because you can't use leverage there
would be issues hitting the desired [percentage]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} [volatility target]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2} if your portfolio contains very low
[volatility]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} assets, as you'll see below.

In particular you should be concerned about bond ETFs that could have a
[sigma]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} below 5% per year. To avoid this
problem I've generally suggested longer maturity bond funds on which the
volatility will be higher. Low risk instruments have other problems of
course, which I've discussed earlier in the book, including generally
higher [standardised costs]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2}.

I'll assume, as I did in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size', that you trade in 100 share blocks as this is more
economical. Using this size you could implement this strategy with just
€100,000 of [trading capital]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} and no
issues of minimum
size.[[[149](#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#footnote-150){._idFootnoteLink
._idGenColorInherit}]{#Autopilot_ebook-24.xhtml#footnote-150-backlink}]{.Footnote-reference
._idGenCharOverride-3}

The costs of ETFs will vary depending on their volatility, so we'll
conservatively use the standardised cost for the lowest risk ETF (IGIL:
global inflation linked bonds) for all instruments. The relevant cost of
0.08 [Sharpe ratio]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} units was calculated
in [[chapter twelve]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor266),
'Speed and Size'.

There are several other considerations when choosing ETFs and you should
consult one of the books mentioned in [[appendix A]{.URL
._idGenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_ebook-29.xhtml#_idTextAnchor335) to
understand these. Finally, this selection does not constitute an
endorsement for these specific products, and the information I've used
to choose them will almost certainly change in the future.

Table 39 shows the final set of instruments. In the example you're
constrained in your portfolio allocations by an outside investor. You
have limits on how much you can allocate to bonds and equities, and to
emerging markets and inflation linked bonds. This is reflected in the
asset grouping I've chosen in the table. You need the grouping to
allocate the [instrument weights]{.strong ._idGenCharOverride-2} in your
portfolio, which I'll do below using the [handcrafting ]{.strong
._idGenCharOverride-2}method.

Table 39: What ETFs are [you]{lang="en-US" lang="en-US"} trading and how
should they be grouped?
