Sharpe ratio to be. At some point some instruments or trading rules in
your portfolio will go badly wrong. Limit your exposure to this by
trading the most diversified set of instruments and rules that you can.
Stay away from low volatility instruments -- they're expensive to trade
and dangerous.

The best systematic traders will be [diligent]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4} when creating their systems, but [lazy
]{.Emphasis ._idGenCharOverride-4}when running them. Put the hard work
into designing a safe system that you are comfortable with and then do
not change it. Make a commitment: don't be tempted to meddle, improve or
risk manage. These time-consuming activities usually destroy
performance.

Finally to make money you need to be [lucky]{.Emphasis
._idGenCharOverride-4}. Even if you do everything right you could still
be unprofitable if the chance turns against you. You can't entirely
eliminate risk from investing but you should quantify it, and make sure
you can cope with the likely downside.

So it only remains for me to say: Good Luck!

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {._idFootnotes}
:::

</div>

[]{#Autopilot_ebook-27.xhtml}

<div>

### []{#Autopilot_ebook-27.xhtml#_idTextAnchor330}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-27.xhtml#_idTextAnchor331}[]{#Autopilot_ebook-27.xhtml#_idTextAnchor332}Glossary {#Autopilot_ebook-27.xhtml#_idParaDest-118 .Heading-3 lang="en-US" lang="en-US"}

+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Active fund,                     | A [collective fund               |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}where securities |
| Active management                | are bought and sold actively to  |
|                                  | try and outperform the general   |
|                                  | market. Normally more expensive  |
|                                  | than [passive                    |
|                                  | funds]{.Table_strong}, as active |
|                                  | fund managers think they can     |
|                                  | generate, and charge for,        |
|                                  | [alpha]{.Table_strong}. Hedge    |
|                                  | funds are an extreme example of  |
|                                  | active funds. As distinct from   |
|                                  | passive funds and [passive       |
|                                  | management]{.Table_strong}. See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 116](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor163). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Alpha                            | The returns of an                |
|                                  | [active]{.Table_strong}          |
|                                  | [manager]{.Table_strong} or      |
|                                  | trader can be split into         |
|                                  | [beta]{.Table_strong} and        |
|                                  | [alpha]{.Table_strong}. The beta |
|                                  | are the returns you could get    |
|                                  | from investing in the general    |
|                                  | market, i.e. in a                |
|                                  | [passive]{.Table_strong} [index  |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}fund. Any        |
|                                  | additional return due to the     |
|                                  | manager's skill is alpha.        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Alternative beta                 | A kind of [beta]{.Table_strong}, |
|                                  | but which requires active        |
|                                  | trading to achieve. So for       |
|                                  | example to earn the [equity      |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong} premium,   |
|                                  | which is the return from being   |
|                                  | long low price:earnings (PE) and |
|                                  | short high PE equities, you need |
|                                  | to buy and sell the appropriate  |
|                                  | shares at the right time. Like   |
|                                  | [beta]{.Table_strong}, and       |
|                                  | unlike [alpha]{.Table_strong},[  |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}this kind of     |
|                                  | return does not require skill.   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Annualised cash volatility       | See [volatility                  |
| target                           | target]{.Table_strong}.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Asset allocating investor        | An investor who usually does not |
|                                  | forecast asset prices, but uses  |
|                                  | a systematic                     |
|                                  | [framework]{.Table_strong} to    |
|                                  | create the best possible         |
|                                  | portfolio. See [page             |
|                                  | 3](#Autopilot                    |
|                                  | _ebook-5.xhtml#_idTextAnchor006) |
|                                  | and [page                        |
|                                  | 237](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-24.xhtml#_idTextAnchor307). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Asset class                      | Name for a general kind of       |
|                                  | investable security, e.g.        |
|                                  | equities, bonds, commodity       |
|                                  | futures and commercial property  |
|                                  | are all asset classes.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Back-testing                     | A back-test is a historical      |
|                                  | simulation of what performance   |
|                                  | and behaviour would have been    |
|                                  | for a trading system, based on   |
|                                  | the data and prices that         |
|                                  | occurred in the past. See [page  |
|                                  | 20](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor028). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Beta                             | Used as a shorthand for getting  |
|                                  | returns from being exposed to    |
|                                  | the market generally, without    |
|                                  | having any particular skill; as  |
|                                  | distinct from                    |
|                                  | [alpha]{.Table_strong}. Owning a |
|                                  | [passive index fund,             |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}such as an ETF   |
|                                  | linked to the French CAC 30      |
|                                  | stock market, will give you      |
|                                  | [beta]{.Table_strong} returns    |
|                                  | for the relevant market. See     |
|                                  | also [alternative                |
|                                  | beta]{.Table_strong}.            |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Also: to what extent a           |
|                                  | particular asset is exposed to   |
|                                  | the overall market. Low beta     |
|                                  | assets have less exposure to the |
|                                  | overall market, and are safer,   |
|                                  | than high beta assets.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Block value                      | The increase in value of one     |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | block]{.Table_strong} when it's  |
|                                  | price goes up by one percentage  |
|                                  | point. See [page                 |
|                                  | 164](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor233). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Bootstrapping                    | A method of [portfolio           |
|                                  | optimisation]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | which takes into account the     |
|                                  | uncertainty in historical data,  |
|                                  | unlike [single period            |
|                                  | optimisation]{.Table_strong}.    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 85](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor123). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Calibration                      | Fine tuning a [trading           |
|                                  | rule]{.Table_strong} by looking  |
|                                  | at the performance or behaviour  |
|                                  | of [variations]{.Table_strong}   |
|                                  | during                           |
|                                  | [back-testing]{.Table_strong}; a |
|                                  | kind of                          |
|                                  | [fitting]{.Table_strong}. See    |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 60](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor098). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Carry                            | A trading strategy where you     |
|                                  | profit from the difference       |
|                                  | between yields and funding costs |
|                                  | if prices remain unchanged. A    |
|                                  | recommended carry trading rule   |
|                                  | is discussed in [[chapter        |
|                                  | seven]{.URL                      |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor168), |
|                                  | on [page                         |
|                                  | 129](#Autopilot_                 |
|                                  | ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor182) |
|                                  | and in [[appendix B]{.URL        |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342), |
|                                  | from [page                       |
|                                  | 297](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor349). |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | See also [FX                     |
|                                  | Carry]{.Table_strong}.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Cash volatility target           | See [volatility                  |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong}.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Cognitive bias                   | A psychological flaw in human    |
|                                  | thought processes which results  |
|                                  | in poor decisions being made.    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 18](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor023). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Collective fund                  | A kind of fund which buys you a  |
|                                  | share in a portfolio of          |
|                                  | securities. These can be         |
|                                  | [exchange traded                 |
|                                  | funds]{.Table_strong}, mutual    |
|                                  | funds in the US, or in the UK    |
|                                  | investment trusts and unit       |
|                                  | trusts. Collective funds can be  |
|                                  | [active]{.Table_strong} or       |
|                                  | [passive]{.Table_strong}. See    |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 116](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor163). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Combined forecast                | The forecast you get for a       |
|                                  | particular                       |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong}      |
|                                  | after combining the individual   |
|                                  | forecasts from multiple [trading |
|                                  | rules]{.Table_strong} and        |
|                                  | multiplying by the [forecast     |
|                                  | diversification                  |
|                                  | multiplier]{.Table_strong}. See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 135](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor188). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Correlation,                     | A measure of how two things      |
|                                  | co-move. Normally used in        |
| Correlated                       | relation to daily returns from   |
|                                  | assets or profits from [trading  |
|                                  | rules]{.Table_strong}.           |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | A correlation of -1 indicates    |
|                                  | two things always move in        |
|                                  | opposite directions, +1          |
|                                  | indicates they always move in    |
|                                  | the same direction and 0 means   |
|                                  | there is no linear relationship  |
|                                  | (uncorrelated).                  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Daily cash volatility target     | See [volatility                  |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong}.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Data first                       | A kind of                        |
|                                  | [fitting]{.Table_strong} where   |
|                                  | you specify a very general       |
|                                  | trading rule or model of price   |
|                                  | movement with many parameters    |
|                                  | and then use a statistical       |
|                                  | method to find the best choice   |
|                                  | of parameters from the available |
|                                  | data. Opposite to [ideas         |
|                                  | first]{.Table_strong}. See [page |
|                                  | 32](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor042). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Data mining                      | The process of                   |
|                                  | [fitting]{.Table_strong} to      |
|                                  | historical data in such a way    |
|                                  | that you will always find at     |
|                                  | least one apparently profitable  |
|                                  | [trading rule]{.Table_strong}.   |
|                                  | For example, if you run          |
|                                  | thousands of                     |
|                                  | [back-tests]{.Table_strong} for  |
|                                  | different rules over a           |
|                                  | particular historical period, at |
|                                  | least one will look great.       |
|                                  | Because they are highly tuned to |
|                                  | fit the past, data mined trading |
|                                  | rules usually perform badly in a |
|                                  | future that is never the same.   |
|                                  | Also see                         |
|                                  | [over-fitting]{.Table_strong}.   |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Derivative                       | A way of benefiting from an      |
|                                  | asset going up or down in price  |
|                                  | without actually owning it.      |
|                                  | Futures, spread bets, options    |
|                                  | and contracts for difference are |
|                                  | all examples of derivatives.     |
|                                  | Derivatives often provide more   |
|                                  | [leverage ]{.Table_strong}than   |
|                                  | the underlying assets.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Diversification multiplier       | A portfolio will have a lower    |
|                                  | [standard                        |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | returns than its individual      |
|                                  | assets, assuming they are not    |
|                                  | perfectly                        |
|                                  | [correlated]{.Table_strong}.     |
|                                  | This assumes the assets all have |
|                                  | the same [standard               |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | returns.                         |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | The diversification multiplier   |
|                                  | multiplies portfolio returns to  |
|                                  | get them back to the same        |
|                                  | standard deviation as the        |
|                                  | underlying assets. It is always  |
|                                  | 1, for portfolios where all      |
|                                  | assets are perfectly correlated, |
|                                  | or larger than 1. See            |
|                                  | [instrument diversification      |
|                                  | multiplier]{.Table_strong} and   |
|                                  | [forecast diversification        |
|                                  | multiplier. ]{.Table_strong}See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 138](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor199). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Dynamic                          | A strategy where we actively buy |
|                                  | and sell assets to express       |
|                                  | opinions on risk adjusted asset  |
|                                  | returns. As opposed to           |
|                                  | [static]{.Table_strong}. See     |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 44](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor072). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Equity value                     | A type of [relative              |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong} trading in |
|                                  | the equity market where you buy  |
|                                  | 'cheap' stocks with low price    |
|                                  | earnings (PE), high divided      |
|                                  | yield or similar value           |
|                                  | characteristics, and sell        |
|                                  | 'expensive' stocks with high PE, |
|                                  | low yield or similar.            |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Equity volatility index          | A measure of how volatile an     |
|                                  | equity market is expected to be, |
|                                  | derived from option prices.      |
|                                  | Sometimes called the 'fear       |
|                                  | index'. The key European index   |
|                                  | is the V2TX and in the US it's   |
|                                  | the VIX. Futures can be traded   |
|                                  | on each of these indices. See    |
|                                  | skew concept box, [page          |
|                                  | 40](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor063). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Exchange traded fund (ETF)       | Usually a type of [passive       |
|                                  | indexed]{.Table_strong},[        |
|                                  | collective fund]{.Table_strong}  |
|                                  | which can be traded and owned    |
|                                  | like a normal equity, and has    |
|                                  | low fees. Some ETFs implement    |
|                                  | [dynamic]{.Table_strong}         |
|                                  | strategies and are more          |
|                                  | expensive.                       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Execution cost                   | Part of the cost of trading an   |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong},     |
|                                  | equal to the difference between  |
|                                  | the mid-price and the traded     |
|                                  | price.[ ]{.Table_strong}Those    |
|                                  | who do not trade in large sizes  |
|                                  | can assume they will pay the bid |
|                                  | (if selling) or offer (if        |
|                                  | buying), which implies it will   |
|                                  | cost them half the difference    |
|                                  | between the bid and the offer to |
|                                  | trade. See [page                 |
|                                  | 189](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor270). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Expanding window                 | A way of doing [out of           |
|                                  | sample]{.Table_strong} testing.  |
|                                  | You pretend you are at a point   |
|                                  | in the past and then use data    |
|                                  | from the beginning of your price |
|                                  | history up to that point to      |
|                                  | choose trading rules or          |
|                                  | portfolio weights. You then test |
|                                  | those rules or weights on the    |
|                                  | data for a year after that       |
|                                  | point. Then you move forward a   |
|                                  | year to the next point and       |
|                                  | repeat. See [page                |
|                                  | 64](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor103). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Exponentially weighted moving    | A kind of [moving                |
| average (EWMA)                   | average]{.Table_strong} where    |
|                                  | you weight more recent           |
|                                  | observations more heavily.       |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | If Pt is the most recent data    |
|                                  | point[ ]{.Table_strong}then the  |
|                                  | EWMA is:                         |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | (A × Pt) + (A × (1 - A) × Pt -   |
|                                  | 1) + (A × (1 - A)2 × Pt - 1) +   |
|                                  | ((A × (1 - A)3 × Pt - 2) \...    |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | You can also calculate this      |
|                                  | recursively. If you have         |
|                                  | yesterday's EWMA Et-1 then       |
|                                  | today's EWMA is:                 |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | (A × Pt) + (Et - 1 × (1 - A))    |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Used in the [EWMAC               |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}trading rule and |
|                                  | also in the estimation of [price |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Exponentially weighted moving    | A [trend following               |
| average crossover (EWMAC)        | ]{.Table_strong}trading rule     |
|                                  | where you take a fast            |
|                                  | [exponentially weighted moving   |
|                                  | average (EWMA)]{.Table_strong}   |
|                                  | of the price with a short        |
|                                  | look-back, and a slow EWMA with  |
|                                  | a longer look-back. If the fast  |
|                                  | EWMA is above the slow then      |
|                                  | prices have been rising and you  |
|                                  | buy, and vice versa.             |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 127](#Autopilot_                 |
|                                  | ebook-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor181) |
|                                  | and [page                        |
|                                  | 294](#Autopilot_                 |
|                                  | ebook-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor345) |
|                                  | in [[appendix B]{.URL            |
|                                  | ._id                             |
|                                  | GenCharOverride-4}](#Autopilot_e |
|                                  | book-30.xhtml#_idTextAnchor342). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Fitting                          | The process of picking the best  |
|                                  | [trading rule]{.Table_strong}.   |
|                                  | Usually this would involve       |
|                                  | running                          |
|                                  | [back-tests]{.Table_strong} over |
|                                  | historical data and finding the  |
|                                  | most profitable rule. See [page  |
|                                  | 60](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor098). |
|                                  | See also [ideas                  |
|                                  | first]{.Table_strong} and [data  |
|                                  | first]{.Table_strong}, and       |
|                                  | [over-fitted]{.Table_strong}.    |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Forecast                         | An estimate of how much an       |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong}'s    |
|                                  | price will go up or down,        |
|                                  | translated into a numeric scale. |
|                                  | The forecast can either be the   |
|                                  | discretionary forecast of a      |
|                                  | [semi-automatic                  |
|                                  | trader]{.Table_strong} or the    |
|                                  | fixed forecast of the [asset     |
|                                  | allocating                       |
|                                  | investor]{.Table_strong}; for    |
|                                  | [staunch systems                 |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} a single |
|                                  | systematic [trading              |
|                                  | rule]{.Table_strong} or a        |
|                                  | [combined forecast               |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}blending         |
|                                  | different trading rules. See     |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 120](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-16.xhtml#_idTextAnchor170). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Forecast diversification         | The [diversification             |
| multiplier                       | multiplier]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  | required so that the [combined   |
|                                  | forecast]{.Table_strong} for a   |
|                                  | particular instrument has the    |
|                                  | expected average absolute value  |
|                                  | (recommended value: 10). See     |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 138](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor199). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Forecast scalar                  | A value you multiply a           |
|                                  | [forecast]{.Table_strong} by to  |
|                                  | ensure it has the right average  |
|                                  | absolute value (recommended      |
|                                  | value: 10). See [page            |
|                                  | 311](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-32.xhtml#_idTextAnchor367). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Forecast weights                 | The weights used to combine      |
|                                  | forecasts from multiple [trading |
|                                  | rules]{.Table_strong} into a     |
|                                  | single [combined                 |
|                                  | forecast]{.Table_strong} for a   |
|                                  | particular                       |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong}. See |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 137](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-17.xhtml#_idTextAnchor194). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Foreign exchange (FX) carry      | A foreign exchange (FX) trading  |
|                                  | strategy where you borrow in low |
|                                  | interest currencies, exchange    |
|                                  | the money for a high interest    |
|                                  | currency, put it into the bank,  |
|                                  | and earn the difference. This    |
|                                  | strategy will fail if the high   |
|                                  | interest currency depreciates by |
|                                  | more than the gap in interest    |
|                                  | rates. See also                  |
|                                  | [carry]{.Table_strong}.          |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Framework                        | The process I describe in this   |
|                                  | book to translate [forecasts     |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}into actual      |
|                                  | investment decisions. The        |
|                                  | framework includes components    |
|                                  | for combining trading rules and  |
|                                  | [instruments]{.Table_strong},    |
|                                  | [volatility                      |
|                                  | targeting]{.Table_strong} and    |
|                                  | position sizing. Together with   |
|                                  | any trading rules the framework  |
|                                  | makes up the [trading            |
|                                  | system]{.Table_strong}.[         |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}See [page        |
|                                  | 105](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-14.xhtml#_idTextAnchor140). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Fundamental data                 | Non-price data used for          |
|                                  | forecasting, e.g. earnings of a  |
|                                  | firm, inflation rates or weather |
|                                  | forecasts. Opposite of           |
|                                  | [technical]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  | [data]{.Table_strong}. See [page |
|                                  | 49](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor083). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Gaussian normal distribution     | A statistical distribution which |
|                                  | is shaped like a bell, often     |
|                                  | assumed for returns. If your     |
|                                  | returns are Gaussian normal then |
|                                  | you will see returns one         |
|                                  | [sigma]{.Table_strong} or less   |
|                                  | around the average about 68% of  |
|                                  | the time, and returns two sigma  |
|                                  | or less about 95% of the time.   |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 27](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor037). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Half-Kelly                       | See [Kelly                       |
|                                  | criterion]{.Table_strong}.       |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Handcrafted optimisation         | A method of [portfolio           |
|                                  | optimisation]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | where you set weights by hand,   |
|                                  | grouping assets together and     |
|                                  | using only estimates of          |
|                                  | [correlations]{.Table_strong}    |
|                                  | (and optionally, [Sharpe         |
|                                  | ratios]{.Table_strong}). See     |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 88](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor126). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Ideas first                      | A type of                        |
|                                  | [fitting]{.Table_strong} where   |
|                                  | you conceive an idea, choose a   |
|                                  | specific trading rule and then   |
|                                  | [back-test]{.Table_strong} the   |
|                                  | rule on historical data.         |
|                                  | Opposite to [data                |
|                                  | first]{.Table_strong}. See [page |
|                                  | 32](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor042). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| In sample                        | Using historical data to select  |
|                                  | trading rules or portfolio       |
|                                  | weights and then testing those   |
|                                  | rules on the same data, which    |
|                                  | means including future           |
|                                  | information. A bad thing.        |
|                                  | Opposite to [out of              |
|                                  | sample]{.Table_strong}. See      |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 62](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor101). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Index tracker                    | See [passive                     |
|                                  | fund]{.Table_strong}.            |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument                       | Something you trade or invest    |
|                                  | in, e.g. an equity like Apple    |
|                                  | stock, an oil futures contract   |
|                                  | or a spread bet on the EUR/USD   |
|                                  | FX rate. See [page               |
|                                  | 111](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor152). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument block                 | 'One' of an                      |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong} --   |
|                                  | the minimum discrete unit you    |
|                                  | can economically trade an        |
|                                  | instrument in, which might be    |
|                                  | 100 shares, one futures contract |
|                                  | or £1 a point on a spread bet.   |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 164](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor233). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument currency volatility   | The expected daily [standard     |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | returns of owning one            |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | block]{.Table_strong}, measured  |
|                                  | in the currency of the           |
|                                  | instrument. Equal to [price      |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  | multiplied by [block             |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong}. See [page |
|                                  | 168](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor239). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument diversification       | A type of [diversification       |
| multiplier                       | multiplier ]{.Table_strong}that  |
|                                  | accounts for the diversification |
|                                  | across the returns of [trading   |
|                                  | subsystems]{.Table_strong}. See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 179](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor257). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument value volatility      | The expected daily [standard     |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | returns of owning one            |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | block]{.Table_strong}, measured  |
|                                  | in the same currency as the      |
|                                  | trader or investor's [trading    |
|                                  | capital]{.Table_strong}. Equal   |
|                                  | to [instrument currency          |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  | multiplied by the relevant[      |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}exchange rate.   |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 169](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor241). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Instrument weights               | In my [framework]{.Table_strong} |
|                                  | the weights that different       |
|                                  | [trading subsystems,             |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}each[            |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}trading a single |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong},     |
|                                  | have in a portfolio of           |
|                                  | subsystems. See [page            |
|                                  | 175](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor249). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Kelly criterion                  | A way of determining the         |
|                                  | [percentage]{.Table_strong}      |
|                                  | [volatility                      |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} you       |
|                                  | should use, given your           |
|                                  | expectation of [Sharpe ratio     |
|                                  | (SR)]{.Table_strong}. A SR of    |
|                                  | 0.5 implies you should use a     |
|                                  | risk percentage of 50%. However  |
|                                  | it is safer to use               |
|                                  | [Half-Kelly]{.Table_strong} and  |
|                                  | set the risk to half what Kelly  |
|                                  | requires, i.e. for an SR of 0.5  |
|                                  | set risk to 25%. See [page       |
|                                  | 153](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor218). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Law of active management         | An equation which states that    |
|                                  | the information ratio of a       |
|                                  | trading strategy is proportional |
|                                  | to the square root of the number |
|                                  | of independent bets made each    |
|                                  | year. See [page                  |
|                                  | 47](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor080). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Leverage                         | Borrowing to invest, either in   |
|                                  | an explicit way or by using a    |
|                                  | derivative such as a future or   |
|                                  | spread bet where your exposure   |
|                                  | is greater than your initial     |
|                                  | cash payment.                    |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Liquidity                        | How easy it is to trade quickly  |
|                                  | in size without changing the     |
|                                  | price significantly. Shares in   |
|                                  | blue chip companies are liquid   |
|                                  | because you can usually sell \$1 |
|                                  | million worth within minutes at  |
|                                  | close to the prevailing price. A |
|                                  | \$1 million house cannot be sold |
|                                  | in a single day without giving   |
|                                  | buyers a substantial discount    |
|                                  | from what it could achieve given |
|                                  | more time. The blue chip shares  |
|                                  | are liquid; the house is not.    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 41](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor065). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Mean reversion                   | Any trading strategy where you   |
|                                  | assume asset prices will revert  |
|                                  | to an equilibrium or fair value. |
|                                  | For example, you might think     |
|                                  | 1.70 is a fair value for the     |
|                                  | price of the GPB/USD FX rate. If |
|                                  | the rate goes below 1.70 you'll  |
|                                  | buy pounds and sell dollars, and |
|                                  | if it goes higher you'd sell     |
|                                  | pounds (and buy dollars).        |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | See also [relative               |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong}.           |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Merger arbitrage                 | A trading strategy, where if a   |
|                                  | merger or takeover is occurring  |
|                                  | you buy the company to be        |
|                                  | acquired, and usually short sell |
|                                  | the acquirer as a hedge.         |
|                                  | Normally this is done at a       |
|                                  | discount to the takeover price,  |
|                                  | the gap reflecting uncertainty   |
|                                  | about whether a deal will go     |
|                                  | ahead. Profits can be made by    |
|                                  | selecting deals where the gap is |
|                                  | too large given the level of     |
|                                  | uncertainty. This is a negative[ |
|                                  | skew]{.Table_strong} style of    |
|                                  | trading, because occasional      |
|                                  | large losses are made when deals |
|                                  | fall through.                    |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Momentum                         | When asset prices go up after    |
|                                  | previous rises; or go down after |
|                                  | falls. [Trend following          |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}rules try and    |
|                                  | profit from momentum.            |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Moving average                   | A weighted average of a value    |
|                                  | over the last N points in time,  |
|                                  | where N is the look-back window. |
|                                  | The weighting can either be a    |
|                                  | simple average, with all points  |
|                                  | equally weighted, or an          |
|                                  | [exponentially                   |
|                                  | weighted]{.Table_strong} [moving |
|                                  | average]{.Table_strong}, with    |
|                                  | more recent points given a       |
|                                  | higher weight.                   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Used in the estimation of [price |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}.      |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Open interest                    | The number of futures contracts  |
|                                  | outstanding for a particular     |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong}. A   |
|                                  | measure of how actively an       |
|                                  | instrument is traded.            |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Out of sample                    | Using historical data to select  |
|                                  | trading rules or portfolio       |
|                                  | weights without looking into the |
|                                  | future. A good thing, in         |
|                                  | contrast to [in                  |
|                                  | sample]{.Table_strong}. See      |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 63](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor102). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Over-fitted                      | When something is                |
|                                  | [fitted]{.Table_strong} in such  |
|                                  | a way that it matches the past   |
|                                  | data too well and is unlikely to |
|                                  | perform well when actually       |
|                                  | traded. Sometimes referred to as |
|                                  | curve fitting. Also see [data    |
|                                  | mining]{.Table_strong}. Refer to |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 60](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor096). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Passive fund,                    | A type of [collective            |
|                                  | fund]{.Table_strong} which is    |
| Passive index,                   | invested and managed passively   |
|                                  | in a portfolio of securities     |
| Passive management               | according to some predefined     |
|                                  | weights. Usually these funds are |
|                                  | [index trackers                  |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}which follow     |
|                                  | indices such as the FTSE 100 or  |
|                                  | S&P 500. Passive funds are a     |
|                                  | cheap way to get broad market,   |
|                                  | or [beta]{.Table_strong},        |
|                                  | exposure. As distinct from       |
|                                  | [active funds]{.Table_strong}.   |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 116](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-15.xhtml#_idTextAnchor163). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Percentage volatility target     | The target expected annualised   |
|                                  | percentage risk your trading     |
|                                  | system is exposed to. When       |
|                                  | multiplied by [trading           |
|                                  | capital]{.Table_strong} we get   |
|                                  | the [annualised cash volatility  |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong}. See also |
|                                  | [volatility                      |
|                                  | target]{.Table_strong} and [page |
|                                  | 147](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-18.xhtml#_idTextAnchor213). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Portfolio optimisation           | The process of finding the       |
|                                  | optimal (best) portfolio by      |
|                                  | deciding in which [portfolio     |
|                                  | weights]{.Table_strong} to hold  |
|                                  | your assets.                     |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Normally a portfolio consists of |
|                                  | positions in various assets,     |
|                                  | e.g. stocks and bonds. In my     |
|                                  | [framework]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  | portfolios can be of (a)         |
|                                  | multiple [trading                |
|                                  | rules]{.Table_strong} for which  |
|                                  | we find [forecast                |
|                                  | weights]{.Table_strong}, and for |
|                                  | (b) [trading]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | [subsystems]{.Table_strong}, one |
|                                  | per [instrument]{.Table_strong}, |
|                                  | for which we optimise            |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | weights]{.Table_strong}.         |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | You should use                   |
|                                  | [handcrafting]{.Table_strong} or |
|                                  | [bootstrapping]{.Table_strong}   |
|                                  | to calculate portfolio weights;  |
|                                  | I do not recommend [single       |
|                                  | period                           |
|                                  | optimisation]{.Table_strong}.    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 80](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-12.xhtml#_idTextAnchor119). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Portfolio weighted position      | The position you hold in an      |
|                                  | instrument at the trading system |
|                                  | level. Because a trading system  |
|                                  | consists of a portfolio of       |
|                                  | [trading]{.Table_strong}         |
|                                  | [subsystems]{.Table_strong}, one |
|                                  | for each instrument, this is     |
|                                  | equal to the instrument's[       |
|                                  | subsystem                        |
|                                  | position]{.Table_strong}         |
|                                  | multiplied by the [instrument    |
|                                  | weight ]{.Table_strong}and the   |
|                                  | [instrument diversification      |
|                                  | multiplier. ]{.Table_strong}See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 183](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor261). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Portfolio weights                | In general the weights that      |
|                                  | assets have in a portfolio. See  |
|                                  | [instrument                      |
|                                  | weights]{.Table_strong} and      |
|                                  | [forecast                        |
|                                  | weights]{.Table_strong}.         |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Position inertia                 | If the current position is less  |
|                                  | than 10% away from the [rounded  |
|                                  | target position]{.Table_strong}  |
|                                  | then to reduce costs you         |
|                                  | shouldn't trade. See [page       |
|                                  | 184](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-20.xhtml#_idTextAnchor263). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Predictable risk                 | The component of volatility in   |
|                                  | asset prices or portfolio        |
|                                  | returns which you can predict    |
|                                  | using estimates of [standard     |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} and    |
|                                  | [correlation]{.Table_strong}.    |
|                                  | See [unpredictable               |
|                                  | risk]{.Table_strong} and [page   |
|                                  | 44](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor075). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Price volatility                 | The expected [standard           |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | instrument price daily returns,  |
|                                  | in percentage points per day.    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 165](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-19.xhtml#_idTextAnchor235). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Relative value                   | Any trading strategy where you   |
|                                  | buy something that is cheap, and |
|                                  | sell something similar that is   |
|                                  | expensive as a hedge. For        |
|                                  | example, see [equity             |
|                                  | value]{.Table_strong}. Often a   |
|                                  | negative [skew                   |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}strategy, as we  |
|                                  | suffer occasional large losses.  |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Risk parity                      | A type of [passive               |
|                                  | fund]{.Table_strong} where       |
|                                  | assets are held in equal shares  |
|                                  | according to expected risk. See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 44](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor074). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Risk premium                     | The reward you get from          |
|                                  | investing in something that      |
|                                  | carries some risk. For example,  |
|                                  | equities are riskier than bonds  |
|                                  | so we should expect higher       |
|                                  | returns to compensate us. See    |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 36](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor056). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Rolling window                   | A method of [out of              |
|                                  | sample]{.Table_strong} testing.  |
|                                  | You pretend you are at a point   |
|                                  | in the past and then use data    |
|                                  | from the last N years (where N   |
|                                  | can be any value) up to that     |
|                                  | point to choose trading rules or |
|                                  | portfolio weights. You then test |
|                                  | those rules or weights on data   |
|                                  | for a year after that point.     |
|                                  | Then you move forward a year to  |
|                                  | the next point and repeat. See   |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 64](#Autopilot_e                 |
|                                  | book-11.xhtml#_idTextAnchor103). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Semi-Automatic Trader            | A trader who makes their own     |
|                                  | forecasts about price movements  |
|                                  | in a discretionary fashion, but  |
|                                  | then incorporates them into a    |
|                                  | systematic                       |
|                                  | [framework]{.Table_strong}. See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 3](#Autopilot                    |
|                                  | _ebook-5.xhtml#_idTextAnchor007) |
|                                  | and [page                        |
|                                  | 221](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-23.xhtml#_idTextAnchor296). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Sharpe ratio (SR)                | A measure of how profitable a    |
|                                  | trading strategy is, with        |
|                                  | returns adjusted for risk.       |
|                                  | Formally it is the mean return   |
|                                  | over some time period divided by |
|                                  | the [standard                    |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong} of     |
|                                  | returns over the same time       |
|                                  | period. In this book I normally  |
|                                  | use the annualised Sharpe ratio  |
|                                  | -- annualised returns divided by |
|                                  | annualised standard deviation.   |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | If we're not using derivatives   |
|                                  | the 'risk free' interest rate    |
|                                  | should be deducted from          |
|                                  | annualised returns before        |
|                                  | calculating the Sharpe ratio.    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 38](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor061). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Short selling                    | A way of benefiting from an      |
|                                  | asset's fall in price, usually   |
|                                  | used for equity trading. You     |
|                                  | would usually borrow the stock   |
|                                  | with a loan agreement before     |
|                                  | selling it. On closing the trade |
|                                  | the stock is returned and you'll |
|                                  | profit if the price has fallen   |
|                                  | since inception.                 |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Sigma                            | Shorthand for one unit of        |
|                                  | [standard                        |
|                                  | deviation]{.Table_strong}. See   |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 27](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor037). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Single period optimisation       | The classic way of performing a  |
|                                  | [portfolio                       |
|                                  | optimisation]{.Table_strong}:    |
|                                  | using a single average over      |
|                                  | historical data of asset return  |
|                                  | means, [standard                 |
|                                  | deviations]{.Table_strong} and   |
|                                  | [correlations]{.Table_strong};   |
|                                  | these estimates are then used to |
|                                  | do a single optimisation.        |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Skew                             | A measure of how symmetric the   |
|                                  | returns from an asset or         |
|                                  | [trading rule]{.Table_strong}    |
|                                  | are. Positive skew means you get |
|                                  | more losses and fewer gains. But |
|                                  | the average loss is smaller than |
|                                  | the average gain. Negative skew  |
|                                  | means you get more gains and     |
|                                  | fewer losses, and average losses |
|                                  | are larger than average gains.   |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 38](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor062). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Speed limit                      | A limit on how quickly you       |
|                                  | should trade an                  |
|                                  | [instrument]{.Table_strong}. The |
|                                  | speed limit will depend on the   |
|                                  | [standardised                    |
|                                  | cost]{.Table_strong} of the      |
|                                  | instrument, and how much you are |
|                                  | prepared to pay in [Sharpe ratio |
|                                  | (SR) ]{.Table_strong}units per   |
|                                  | year in costs. I recommend       |
|                                  | paying no more than 0.13 SR      |
|                                  | units if you are a [staunch      |
|                                  | systems trader                   |
|                                  | ]{.Table_strong}and 0.08 SR      |
|                                  | units for [semi-automatic        |
|                                  | traders]{.Table_strong} and      |
|                                  | [asset-allocating                |
|                                  | investors]{.Table_strong}. See   |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 197](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor278). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Standard deviation               | A measure of how dispersed some  |
|                                  | data is around its average       |
|                                  | value. If your data points are   |
|                                  | x1, x2, ... xn then the average  |
|                                  | x\* = (x1 + x2 + ... xn) ÷ n.    |
|                                  | The standard deviation is √{(1 ÷ |
|                                  | n)\[(x1 - x\*)2 + (x2 - x\*)2 +  |
|                                  | ... (xn - x\*)2\]}               |
|                                  |                                  |
|                                  | Often applied to daily returns   |
|                                  | in prices or trading system      |
|                                  | profits. An approximate way to   |
|                                  | calculate an annualised standard |
|                                  | deviation of returns is to       |
|                                  | multiply the daily standard      |
|                                  | deviation by 16 (the square root |
|                                  | of 256, approximately the number |
|                                  | of trading days in one year).    |
|                                  | See [page                        |
|                                  | 27](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-8.xhtml#_idTextAnchor037). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Standardised cost                | A [volatility                    |
|                                  | standardised]{.Table_strong}     |
|                                  | method of measuring costs which  |
|                                  | is comparable across             |
|                                  | [instruments]{.Table_strong}. To |
|                                  | calculate, add up all the costs  |
|                                  | of trading                       |
|                                  | [C]{.TableData_emphasis          |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-7},          |
|                                  | including [execution             |
|                                  | costs]{.Table_strong}, fees and  |
|                                  | taxes. Then double to get the    |
|                                  | cost of a 'round trip' (a buy    |
|                                  | and sell), and divide by the     |
|                                  | annualised [instrument cash      |
|                                  | volatility]{.Table_strong}       |
|                                  | ([ICV]{.TableData_emphasis       |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-7}) of the   |
|                                  | instrument. The formula is 2 ×   |
|                                  | [C ]{.TableData_emphasis         |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-7}÷ (16 ×    |
|                                  | [ICV]{.TableData_emphasis        |
|                                  | ._idGenCharOverride-7}). See     |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 191](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-21.xhtml#_idTextAnchor272). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Static                           | A strategy where you do not      |
|                                  | actively trade the assets you    |
|                                  | are investing in because of      |
|                                  | changes in expected risk         |
|                                  | adjusted returns. As opposed to  |
|                                  | [dynamic. ]{.Table_strong}See    |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 44](#Autopilot_                  |
|                                  | ebook-9.xhtml#_idTextAnchor072). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Staunch Systems Trader           | A trader who uses one or more    |
|                                  | systematic trading rules and     |
|                                  | incorporates them into a         |
|                                  | systematic                       |
|                                  | [framework]{.Table_strong}. See  |
|                                  | [page                            |
|                                  | 4](#Autopilot                    |
|                                  | _ebook-5.xhtml#_idTextAnchor008) |
|                                  | and [page                        |
|                                  | 257](#Autopilot_e                |
|                                  | book-25.xhtml#_idTextAnchor317). |
+----------------------------------+----------------------------------+
| Subsystem position               | The position nominally held by a |
|                                  | single instrument's [trading     |
|                                  | subsystem]{.Table_strong},       |
|                                  | trading only one instrument with |
|                                  | the entire [trading              |
|                                  | capital]{.Table_strong}, given a |
|                                  | [forecast ]{.Table_strong}of     |
